    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_count_by_color(Objs, Counts).
    % Counts = [1-2, 2-1]  (color 1 appears twice, color 2 once)

qu_count_by_size(+Objs, -Counts): sorted Size-N pairs giving the number of objs
with each cell count.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_count_by_size(Objs, Counts).
    % Counts = [1-2, 2-1]  (two single-cell objs, one two-cell obj)

qu_count_by_form(+Objs, -Counts): sorted Form-N pairs giving the number of objs
with each origin-normalized shape.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(3,4),r(3,5)])],
    qu_count_by_form(Objs, Counts).
    % Counts = [[r(0,0),r(0,1)]-2]  (both normalize to the same horizontal bar)


Finding Extreme Colors

qu_most_frequent_color(+Objs, -Color): the color appearing in the most objs.
On ties, returns the smallest color value.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_most_frequent_color(Objs, Color).
    % Color = 1  (appears twice)

    qu_most_frequent_color([obj(2,[r(0,0)]), obj(5,[r(0,1)])], Color).
    % Color = 2  (tie; smallest color wins)

qu_least_frequent_color(+Objs, -Color): the color appearing in the fewest objs.
On ties, returns the smallest color value.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_least_frequent_color(Objs, Color).
    % Color = 2  (appears once)


Finding Extreme Objects

qu_largest_obj(+Objs, -Obj): the obj with the most cells.
On ties, returns the first obj in input order.

    O1 = obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)]),
    O2 = obj(2,[r(1,0)]),
    qu_largest_obj([O1,O2], Obj).
    % Obj = obj(1, [r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])

qu_smallest_obj(+Objs, -Obj): the obj with the fewest cells.
On ties, returns the first obj in input order.

    O1 = obj(1,[r(0,0)]),
    O2 = obj(2,[r(1,0),r(1,1),r(1,2)]),
    qu_smallest_obj([O1,O2], Obj).
    % Obj = obj(1, [r(0,0)])


Totals and Averages

qu_total_cells(+Objs, -N): total cell count across all obj terms.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)]), obj(2,[r(1,0),r(1,1)])],
    qu_total_cells(Objs, N).
    % N = 5

qu_avg_size(+Objs, -N): floor-average cell count per obj (integer division).

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)]), obj(2,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_avg_size(Objs, N).
    % N = 2  (4 cells // 2 objs)

    Objs2 = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)]), obj(3,[r(1,0),r(1,1),r(1,2)])],
    qu_avg_size(Objs2, N2).
    % N2 = 2  (6 cells // 3 objs, floor)


Uniformity Tests

These predicates succeed if all objs in the list share a single value for the tested
attribute, and fail otherwise.

qu_all_same_color(+Objs): succeed if all objs have the same color.

    qu_all_same_color([obj(3,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)]), obj(3,[r(2,0)])]).
    % succeeds

    qu_all_same_color([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(1,0)])]).
    % fails (two different colors)

qu_all_same_size(+Objs): succeed if all objs have the same cell count.

    qu_all_same_size([obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(1,0),r(1,1)])]).
    % succeeds (both have 2 cells)

qu_all_same_form(+Objs): succeed if all objs have the same origin-normalized shape.
Objs may be at different positions and have different colors.

    qu_all_same_form([obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(3,4),r(3,5)])]).
    % succeeds (both normalize to [r(0,0),r(0,1)])

    qu_all_same_form([obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(0,0),r(1,0)])]).
    % fails (horizontal bar vs vertical bar - different forms)


Enumerating Distinct Values

qu_colors(+Objs, -Colors): sorted list of distinct color values.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_colors(Objs, Colors).
    % Colors = [1, 2]

qu_sizes(+Objs, -Sizes): sorted list of distinct cell counts.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])],
    qu_sizes(Objs, Sizes).
    % Sizes = [1, 2]  (sizes 1 and 2; no duplicates)


Design Notes

Two-phase count pattern: qu_count_by_color, qu_count_by_size, and qu_count_by_form
all use: (1) findall distinct keys -> sort; (2) for each key, findall objs matching
that key -> length. This produces sorted output without dynamic state.

Tie-breaking for extremes: The Color-N list from qu_count_by_color is sorted by color
ascending. member+cut finds the first match, which is the smallest color at the target
count. For qu_largest_obj and qu_smallest_obj, findall preserves input list order, so
member+cut finds the first object in input order.

Uniformity test pattern: findall values -> sort/2 (deduplicates) -> require singleton
[_]. One line per test predicate.

Library imports: library(lists): member/2, min_list/2, max_list/2, sum_list/2.
Built-ins: findall/3, sort/2, length/2.

42/42 PLUnit tests pass.


Chapter 135: Object List Filtering (sift pack, si_* prefix, Layer 153)

The sift pack filters lists of obj(Color, Cells) terms by various predicates.
All 14 predicates take an input Objs list and return Out, the subset of objects
that satisfy the given condition. An empty result list [] is a valid output.


Object Term Format

The sift pack operates on obj(Color, Cells) terms where Color is an integer and
Cells is a sorted list of r(Row, Col) cell coordinates.


Color Filters

si_by_color(+Objs, +Color, -Out): keep objs whose color equals Color.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])],
    si_by_color(Objs, 1, Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])]

si_not_color(+Objs, +Color, -Out): keep objs whose color differs from Color.

    si_not_color([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 1, Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(0,1)])]

si_color_in(+Objs, +Colors, -Out): keep objs whose color is a member of Colors.

    si_color_in([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], [1,3], Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])]

si_color_not_in(+Objs, +Colors, -Out): keep objs whose color is not in Colors.

    si_color_not_in([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], [1,3], Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(0,1)])]


Size Filters

si_by_size(+Objs, +N, -Out): keep objs with exactly N cells.

    si_by_size([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], 1, Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])]

si_larger_than(+Objs, +N, -Out): keep objs with strictly more than N cells.

    si_larger_than([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)])], 1, Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)])]

si_smaller_than(+Objs, +N, -Out): keep objs with strictly fewer than N cells.

    si_smaller_than([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2)])], 2, Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)])]


Extreme Size Filters

These return ALL objects tied for the extreme value, unlike the query pack's
qu_largest_obj and qu_smallest_obj which return only the first tied object.

si_max_size(+Objs, -Out): keep all objs with the maximum cell count.

    si_max_size([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2),r(0,3)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2),r(0,3)])]

    si_max_size([obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(1,0),r(1,1)])], Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(1,0),r(1,1)])]  (tie: both returned)

si_min_size(+Objs, -Out): keep all objs with the minimum cell count.

    si_min_size([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1),r(0,2),r(0,3)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])]  (tie: both returned)


Form Filter

si_by_form(+Objs, +Form, -Out): keep objs whose origin-normalized cell list equals
Form. Normalization translates the cell set so that min row=0 and min col=0, then
sorts. Exact structural match; not D4-invariant.

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(3,4),r(3,5)]), obj(3,[r(0,0),r(1,0)])],
    si_by_form(Objs, [r(0,0),r(0,1)], Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0),r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(3,4),r(3,5)])]


Color Frequency Filters

si_unique_color(+Objs, -Out): keep objs whose color appears exactly once in Objs.

    si_unique_color([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(0,1)])]

si_shared_color(+Objs, -Out): keep objs whose color appears more than once in Objs.

    si_shared_color([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])]


Border Filters

si_on_border(+Objs, +H, +W, -Out): keep objs with at least one cell on the border
of an H-by-W grid. Border = row 0, row H-1, col 0, or col W-1.

    si_on_border([obj(1,[r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(1,1)])], 3, 3, Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,1)])]  (row 0 is the border; row 1 is interior)

si_off_border(+Objs, +H, +W, -Out): keep objs with no cell on the grid border.

    si_off_border([obj(1,[r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(1,1)])], 3, 3, Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(1,1)])]


Design Notes

All predicates use findall. Empty output [] is always valid; no predicate fails
because no objects match.

si_max_size and si_min_size return ALL tied objects; si_unique_color and
si_shared_color use the two-phase count pattern from the query pack. si_by_form
uses a local si_norm_ helper (not imported from pair or xform) for self-containment.

Library imports: library(lists): member/2, memberchk/2, min_list/2, max_list/2.
Built-ins: findall/3, sort/2, length/2.

42/42 PLUnit tests pass.


Chapter 136: Bulk Color Operations on Object Scenes (pigment pack, pg_* prefix, Layer 154)

The pigment pack manipulates colors across entire scenes (lists of obj(Color, Cells)
terms). It covers uniform recoloring, single-color replacement, color swapping,
color table application, color table inference, zip-based recoloring, frequency-targeted
recoloring, and color table utilities.


Object Term Format

The pigment pack operates on obj(Color, Cells) terms where Color is an integer.
A scene is a list of such terms.


Uniform and Targeted Recoloring

pg_recolor_all(+Objs, +Color, -Out): set every obj's color to Color.

    pg_recolor_all([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)])], 5, Out).
    % Out = [obj(5,[r(0,0)]), obj(5,[r(0,1)])]

pg_recolor_one(+Objs, +From, +To, -Out): change all From-colored objs to To; keep others.

    pg_recolor_one([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 1, 5, Out).
    % Out = [obj(5,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(5,[r(1,0)])]

pg_swap(+Objs, +C1, +C2, -Out): exchange colors C1 and C2 throughout the scene.

    pg_swap([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 1, 2, Out).
    % Out = [obj(2,[r(0,0)]), obj(1,[r(0,1)]), obj(2,[r(1,0)])]


Color Table Operations

pg_apply_table(+Objs, +Table, -Out): apply the color table (lenient - keep unmapped).

    pg_apply_table([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], [1-5, 2-6], Out).
    % Out = [obj(5,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])]  (3 not in table, kept)

pg_apply_table_strict(+Objs, +Table, -Out): apply table; exclude unmapped objs.

    pg_apply_table_strict([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(3,[r(1,0)])], [1-5, 2-6], Out).
    % Out = [obj(5,[r(0,0)])]  (3 not in table, excluded)

pg_infer_table(+Objs1, +Objs2, -Table): infer color mapping from same-position obj pairs.

    pg_infer_table([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(1,0)])],
                   [obj(3,[r(0,0)]), obj(4,[r(1,0)])], Table).
    % Table = [1-3, 2-4]


Zip-Based Recoloring

pg_zip_recolor(+Objs, +Colors, -Out): pair each obj with a color from the Colors list.

    pg_zip_recolor([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)])], [5,6,7], Out).
    % Out = [obj(5,[r(0,0)]), obj(6,[r(0,1)])]  (Colors has 3, Objs has 2, truncated)


Frequency-Targeted Recoloring

pg_majority_to(+Objs, +Color, -Out): recolor the most frequent color.

    pg_majority_to([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 9, Out).
    % Out = [obj(9,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(9,[r(1,0)])]

pg_minority_to(+Objs, +Color, -Out): recolor the least frequent color.

    pg_minority_to([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 9, Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(9,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])]

pg_unique_to(+Objs, +Color, -Out): recolor uniquely-colored objs.

    pg_unique_to([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 9, Out).
    % Out = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(9,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])]

pg_shared_to(+Objs, +Color, -Out): recolor shared-color objs.

    pg_shared_to([obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(1,[r(1,0)])], 9, Out).
    % Out = [obj(9,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(0,1)]), obj(9,[r(1,0)])]


Color Table Utilities

pg_invert_table(+Table, -Inv): swap From and To in each pair.

    pg_invert_table([1-5, 2-6], [5-1, 6-2]).

pg_table_from(+Table, -Froms): sorted From colors.

    pg_table_from([3-9, 1-5], [1,3]).

pg_consistent(+Table): succeed if no From maps to two different To values.

    pg_consistent([1-5, 2-6]).    % succeeds
    pg_consistent([1-5, 1-6]).    % fails

Library imports: library(lists): member/2, memberchk/2, min_list/2, max_list/2.
Built-ins: findall/3, sort/2, length/2.

42/42 PLUnit tests pass.


Chapter 137: Delta Pack (dl_* Prefix, Layer 155)

The delta pack compares two object scenes and computes what changed.

A scene is a list of obj(Color, Cells) terms. Two objects match iff they have
the same Cells list (exact cell-set equality via Prolog unification).

dl_added(+S1, +S2, -Added): objects in S2 with no cell-set match in S1.
dl_removed(+S1, +S2, -Removed): objects in S1 with no cell-set match in S2.
dl_matched(+S1, +S2, -Pairs): O1-O2 pairs sharing identical cell sets.
dl_recolored(+S1, +S2, -Changes): C1-C2 color change pairs for matched objects.
dl_unchanged(+S1, +S2, -Objs): objects with identical color+cells in both.
dl_color_gain(+S1, +S2, -Colors): colors in S2 not in S1.
dl_color_loss(+S1, +S2, -Colors): colors in S1 not in S2.
dl_count_diff(+S1, +S2, -N): N = length(S2) - length(S1).
dl_size_diff(+S1, +S2, -N): N = total_cells(S2) - total_cells(S1).
dl_is_added_only(+S1, +S2): no removals or recolorings.
dl_is_removed_only(+S1, +S2): no additions or recolorings.
dl_is_recolor_only(+S1, +S2): no additions or removals.
dl_is_stable(+S1, +S2): scenes are identical.
dl_scene_diff(+S1, +S2, -delta(Added, Removed, Recolored, Unchanged)): all four components.

Example:

    S1 = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(1,1)])],
    S2 = [obj(3,[r(0,0)]), obj(4,[r(2,2)])],
    dl_scene_diff(S1, S2, delta(Added, Removed, Recolored, Unchanged)).
    % Added     = [obj(4,[r(2,2)])]
    % Removed   = [obj(2,[r(1,1)])]
    % Recolored = [1-3]
    % Unchanged = []

Library imports: library(lists): member/2, sum_list/2.
Built-ins: findall/3, sort/2, length/2, \+.

42/42 PLUnit tests pass.


Chapter 138: Group Pack (gp_* Prefix, Layer 156)

The group pack partitions a list of obj(Color, Cells) terms into named groups.

Groups are Key-[Objs] pairs sorted by key.

gp_by_color(+Objs, -Groups): partition by color.
gp_by_size(+Objs, -Groups): partition by cell count.
gp_by_row(+Objs, -Groups): partition by minimum row of cells.
gp_by_col(+Objs, -Groups): partition by minimum column of cells.
gp_by_form(+Objs, -Groups): partition by origin-normalized cell list (shape).
gp_size_of(+Groups, -N): number of groups.
gp_flatten(+Groups, -Objs): flatten all groups into a single list.
gp_largest_group(+Groups, -Key-Grp): group with the most objects.
gp_smallest_group(+Groups, -Key-Grp): group with the fewest objects.
gp_singleton_groups(+Groups, -Singles): groups with exactly one object.
gp_shared_groups(+Groups, -Shared): groups with more than one object.
gp_group_sizes(+Groups, -Sizes): sorted distinct group cardinalities.
gp_all_same_size(+Groups): succeed iff all groups have the same cardinality.
gp_keys(+Groups, -Keys): sorted list of group keys.

Example:

    Objs = [obj(1,[r(0,0)]), obj(2,[r(1,1)]), obj(1,[r(0,1)])],
    gp_by_color(Objs, Groups).
    % Groups = [1-[obj(1,[r(0,0)]),obj(1,[r(0,1)])], 2-[obj(2,[r(1,1)])]]

Library imports: library(lists): member/2, memberchk/2, min_list/2, max_list/2.
Built-ins: findall/3, sort/2, length/2.

42/42 PLUnit tests pass.

Chapter 139: Proximity Pack (px_*)

The proximity pack computes distances and spatial relationships between
obj(Color, Cells) terms.

All distance measures use Manhattan distance (sum of absolute row and column
differences). Centroid distance is the Manhattan distance between the integer-
truncated average positions of two objects' cells.

1. Centroid and Distance

px_centroid(+Obj, -R, -C): integer-truncated centroid of an obj term.

Example:
  ?- px_centroid(obj(red,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(1,0),r(1,1)]), R, C).
  R = 0, C = 0.

px_centroid_dist(+O1, +O2, -D): Manhattan distance between centroids.

  ?- px_centroid_dist(obj(a,[r(0,0)]), obj(b,[r(3,4)]), D).
  D = 7.

px_min_cell_dist(+O1, +O2, -D): minimum distance between any cell pair.

  ?- px_min_cell_dist(obj(a,[r(0,0),r(1,0)]), obj(b,[r(1,2)]), D).
  D = 2.

2. Touching

px_touching(+O1, +O2): succeed if any cell of O1 is 4-adjacent to O2.
4-adjacent means cells share an edge (Manhattan distance = 1).

  ?- px_touching(obj(a,[r(1,1)]), obj(b,[r(1,2)])).
  true.

  ?- px_touching(obj(a,[r(1,1)]), obj(b,[r(2,2)])).
  false.

3. Finding Nearest and Farthest

px_nearest(+Objs, +Ref, -Nearest): object with smallest centroid distance.
px_farthest(+Objs, +Ref, -Farthest): object with largest centroid distance.
Ties go to the earliest object in Objs.

  ?- px_nearest(
       [obj(blue,[r(5,5)]), obj(green,[r(1,1)])],
       obj(red,[r(0,0)]),
       N).
  N = obj(green,[r(1,1)]).

4. Sorting and Filtering

px_sort_by_dist(+Objs, +Ref, -Sorted): Objs sorted ascending by centroid distance.

px_within_dist(+Objs, +Ref, +D, -Near): objects at centroid distance at most D.
px_beyond_dist(+Objs, +Ref, +D, -Far): objects at centroid distance strictly > D.

5. Pair Detection

px_closest_pair(+Objs, -O1-O2): unordered pair with minimum centroid distance.
px_farthest_pair(+Objs, -O1-O2): unordered pair with maximum centroid distance.

6. Contact Filters

px_touching_objs(+Objs, +Ref, -Touching): sub-list that touches Ref.
px_non_touching_objs(+Objs, +Ref, -NonTouching): sub-list not touching Ref.

7. Distance Rank

px_dist_rank(+Objs, +Ref, -Ranked): D-Obj pairs sorted ascending by distance.

Chapter 140: Link Pack (lk_*)

The link pack builds and manipulates correspondence links between two lists
of obj(Color, Cells) terms. A link is an O1-O2 pair.

1. Building Links

lk_by_position(+Objs1, +Objs2, -Links): zip two lists element-wise.

  ?- lk_by_position(
       [obj(red,[r(0,0)]), obj(blue,[r(1,0)])],
       [obj(green,[r(0,0)]), obj(yellow,[r(1,0)])],
       Links).
  Links = [obj(red,[r(0,0)])-obj(green,[r(0,0)]),
           obj(blue,[r(1,0)])-obj(yellow,[r(1,0)])].

lk_by_nearest(+Objs1, +Objs2, -Links): each O1 linked to its centroid-nearest O2.
lk_by_color(+Objs1, +Objs2, -Links): all pairs sharing the same color atom.
lk_by_size(+Objs1, +Objs2, -Links): all pairs with the same cell count.
lk_by_form(+Objs1, +Objs2, -Links): all pairs with the same normalized shape.

2. Extracting and Manipulating

lk_source(+Links, -Sources): O1 from each link.
lk_target(+Links, -Targets): O2 from each link.
lk_invert(+Links, -Inverted): swap O1 and O2 in every link.
lk_count(+Links, -N): number of links.

3. Applying Correspondence

lk_apply_color(+Links, -Result): recolor O1 with O2's color; keep O1's cells.
lk_apply_cells(+Links, -Result): replace O1's cells with O2's cells; keep O1's color.

4. Filtering and Unlinked

lk_filter_same_color(+Links, -Same): keep links where both share a color.
lk_filter_diff_color(+Links, -Diff): keep links where colors differ.
lk_unlinked(+Objs, +Links, -Unlinked): objects not appearing as O1 in any link.

Chapter 141: Layout Pack (lt_*)

The layout pack analyzes the collective spatial arrangement of a list of
obj(Color, Cells) terms.

1. Global Bounding Box
lt_global_bbox(+Objs, -R1, -C1, -R2, -C2): bounding box over ALL cells of all objs.
lt_bbox_area(+Objs, -Area): (R2-R1+1) * (C2-C1+1).

2. Row and Column Range
lt_row_range(+Objs, -MinR, -MaxR): range of topmost rows.
lt_col_range(+Objs, -MinC, -MaxC): range of leftmost columns.
lt_row_count(+Objs, -N): distinct topmost row count.
lt_col_count(+Objs, -N): distinct leftmost col count.

3. Line Detection
lt_all_same_row(+Objs): succeed iff all share the same topmost row.
lt_all_same_col(+Objs): succeed iff all share the same leftmost column.

4. Centroid of All
lt_centroid_of_all(+Objs, -R, -C): integer-truncated centroid of all centroids.

5. Grid Detection
lt_is_grid(+Objs, -Rows, -Cols): objects' centroids form an R x C grid.

6. Diagonal Detection
lt_is_diagonal_dr(+Objs): R-C constant (downward-right diagonal).
lt_is_diagonal_dl(+Objs): R+C constant (downward-left diagonal).

7. Uniform Spacing
lt_gap_h(+Objs, -Gap): uniform horizontal centroid gap (fails if non-uniform).
lt_gap_v(+Objs, -Gap): uniform vertical centroid gap.

Chapter 142: Object Symmetry Analysis (objsym pack, os_* prefix, Layer 170)

The objsym pack provides bounding-box-relative symmetry operations for individual
obj(Color, Cells) terms. All transforms are computed relative to the object's own
bounding box, so no prior normalization is required.

1. Bounding Box
os_bbox(+Obj, -MinR, -MinC, -MaxR, -MaxC): bounding box of the object's cell set.
os_normalize(+Obj, -NormObj): translate so bbox top-left is at r(0,0).
os_translate(+Obj, +DR, +DC, -Moved): shift all cells by (DR, DC).

2. Reflection and Rotation
os_reflect_h(+Obj, -Reflected): mirror left-right about vertical center (new C = MinC+MaxC-C).
os_reflect_v(+Obj, -Reflected): mirror top-bottom about horizontal center (new R = MinR+MaxR-R).
os_rotate180(+Obj, -Rotated): rotate 180 degrees (apply both flips).
os_rotate90(+Obj, -Rotated): rotate 90 degrees CW, normalized to origin (new NR=C-MinC, NC=MaxR-R).

3. Symmetry Tests
os_is_hsymm(+Obj): sorted cells == sorted cells of reflect_h.
os_is_vsymm(+Obj): sorted cells == sorted cells of reflect_v.
os_is_rot180(+Obj): sorted cells == sorted cells of rotate180.
os_is_rot90(+Obj): sorted normalized cells == sorted normalized cells after rotate90.
os_has_symmetry(+Obj): any symmetry test passes (cut on first).
os_symmetries(+Obj, -List): list of symmetry atoms [h, v, rot180, rot90] that hold.

4. D4 Equivalence
os_equivalent(+Obj1, +Obj2): same shape under any of 8 D4 orientations (color ignored).

Chapter 143: Object Shape Classification (objbound pack, ob_* prefix, Layer 171)

The objbound pack classifies the shape of individual obj(Color, Cells) terms
without requiring a grid argument.

1. Bounding Box Dimensions
ob_bbox_h(+Obj, -H): bounding box height (MaxR - MinR + 1).
ob_bbox_w(+Obj, -W): bounding box width (MaxC - MinC + 1).
ob_bbox_area(+Obj, -Area): H * W.

2. Shape Type Tests
ob_is_rect(+Obj): cell count = bbox area (no holes).
ob_is_hline(+Obj): H = 1.
ob_is_vline(+Obj): W = 1.
ob_is_single(+Obj): H = 1 and W = 1.
ob_is_square_bbox(+Obj): H = W.

3. Holes and Hollow Objects
ob_holes(+Obj, -HoleCells): bbox cells not in the object.
ob_n_holes(+Obj, -N): hole count.
ob_is_hollow(+Obj): N > 0.

4. Frame Detection
ob_is_frame(+Obj): H>=3, W>=3, all border cells present, no interior cells.

5. Perimeter and Hull
ob_perimeter(+Obj, -P): total exposed cell edges.
ob_dense_hull(+Obj, -HullObj): solid bbox rectangle with same color.

Chapter 144: Object Pair Relations (objrel Pack, or_*, Layer 172)

The objrel pack computes pairwise relationships between two
obj(Color, Cells) terms. Colors are ignored; all reasoning uses
cell positions only.

1. Overlap
or_overlap/2: share at least one cell.
or_shared_cells/3: cells in both objects.
or_n_shared/3: count of shared cells.

2. Adjacency
or_touch4/2: 4-adjacent (Manhattan 1), no overlap.
or_touch8/2: 8-adjacent (Chebyshev 1, includes diagonal), no overlap.

3. Containment and Union
or_contains/2: every cell of Obj2 is in Obj1.
or_union_cells/3: sorted union of both cell sets.

4. Distance
or_dist/3: Euclidean distance between centroids (float).
or_manhattan/3: Manhattan distance between centroids.

5. Direction and Alignment
or_direction/3: compass direction (n/s/e/w/ne/nw/se/sw/same).
or_aligned_h/2: same centroid row.
or_aligned_v/2: same centroid column.

6. Bounding Box Gaps
or_gap_rows/3: empty rows between bounding boxes (0 if touching/overlap).
or_gap_cols/3: empty columns between bounding boxes.

Chapter 145: Object List Filtering (objfilter Pack, of_*, Layer 173)

The objfilter pack selects objects from a list of obj(Color, Cells) terms
by color, size, or shape. No cross-pack dependencies.

1. Color Filters
of_by_color/3: objects of a given color.
of_not_color/3: objects excluding a given color.

2. Size Filters
of_exact_size/3: objects with exactly N cells.
of_min_size/3: objects with >= N cells.
of_max_size/3: objects with <= N cells.

3. Shape Filters
of_is_rect/2: objects filling their bounding box (no holes).
of_is_hline/2: objects spanning a single row (bbox height = 1).
of_is_vline/2: objects spanning a single column (bbox width = 1).
of_is_single/2: single-cell objects.
of_is_hollow/2: objects with at least one hole in bbox (L-shapes, frames).

4. Aggregate Selection
of_largest/2: object with the most cells (first on ties).
of_smallest/2: object with the fewest cells (first on ties).

5. Generic Meta-Predicates
of_filter/3: include(Goal, Objs, Filtered) — custom test per object.
of_partition/4: partition(Goal, Objs, In, Out) — split by goal.

Chapter 145: Grid Canvas and Object Rendering (canvas Pack, cv_*, Layer 174)

This is WP 195. The canvas pack bridges the obj(Color, Cells) world and the
2D grid world with 14 predicates.

1. Canvas Creation
cv_blank/4: create H x W grid filled with Bg.
cv_size/3: get grid height and width.

2. Painting Objects
cv_paint/3: paint one obj term onto a grid.
cv_paint_all/3: paint a list of obj terms in order.
cv_paint_at/5: translate by (DR,DC) then paint.
cv_paint_clip/3: paint with out-of-bounds cells silently skipped.
cv_paint_bg/4: paint only onto background-valued cells.

3. Erasing Objects
cv_erase/4: fill obj cells with background value.

4. Extracting Objects
cv_extract/3: extract obj(Color, Cells) for all Color cells.
cv_extract_all/3: extract all non-bg objects from a grid.

5. Convenience
cv_render/5: create blank canvas and paint all objects.
cv_move/6: erase obj at current position, repaint at offset.
cv_stamp/3: extract obj as minimal bounding-box grid.
cv_blit/5: paste a subgrid at a given position.

Chapter 146: Object Sequence and Progression Analysis (objseq Pack, oq_*, Layer 175)

1. Color and Size Sequences
oq_color_seq/2: extract color list in list order.
oq_size_seq/2: extract cell-count list in list order.

2. Centroid and Step Sequences
oq_centroid_seq/2: centroid sequence as r(R,C) terms.
oq_step_seq/2: consecutive centroid deltas as dr(DR,DC) terms.

3. Monotone Tests
oq_is_growing/1: sizes strictly increasing.
oq_is_shrinking/1: sizes strictly decreasing.

4. Constant Step Analysis
oq_const_step/3: all centroid steps equal (DR, DC).
oq_const_row_step/2: all row steps equal DR.
oq_const_col_step/2: all col steps equal DC.

5. Periodic Sequences
oq_color_period/3: minimal color-sequence period P and unit cycle.
oq_size_period/3: minimal size-sequence period P and unit cycle.

6. Collinearity and Prediction
oq_collinear/1: all centroids on a straight line.
oq_next_centroid/2: predict next position from constant step.
oq_zip_colors/3: zip two obj lists into C1-C2 color pairs.


Chapter 147: Object-Pair Change Analysis and Rule Application (objdelta Pack, dp_*, Layer 176)

1. Single-Pair Deltas
dp_color_delta/3: extract C1-C2 color change from one O1-O2 pair.
dp_pos_delta/3: extract dr(DR,DC) centroid displacement from one pair.
dp_size_delta/3: extract size change N2-N1 from one pair.

2. Pair Tests
dp_same_color/2: O1 and O2 share the same color.
dp_same_form/2: O1 and O2 share the same normalised shape.
dp_same_pos/2: O1 and O2 share the same centroid.

3. Color Map Operations
dp_color_map/2: extract sorted distinct C1-C2 map from a list of pairs.
dp_apply_color/3: apply single C1-C2 rule; fail if color does not match.
dp_apply_color_map/3: apply first matching Map entry to one object.
dp_apply_map_all/3: apply Map to a list; unmatched objects unchanged.

4. Constant Displacement
dp_const_dr/2: all pairs share the same row delta DR.
dp_const_dc/2: all pairs share the same col delta DC.

5. Cell-Level Analysis
dp_common_cells/3: positions in both O1 and O2.
dp_cell_diff/4: Added (in O2 not O1) and Removed (in O1 not O2).


Chapter 148: Object Tiling and Multi-Copy Layout (objcopy Pack, tc_*, Layer 177)

1. Placement
tc_place_at/4: place obj with bbox top-left at (R0, C0).
tc_recolor_all/3: recolor all objs to a uniform color.

2. Tiling
tc_tile_row/4: N copies along a row, Step cols apart.
tc_tile_col/4: N copies along a column, Step rows apart.
tc_tile_grid/6: NR x NC grid of copies.
tc_at_positions/3: one copy per r(R,C) in a position list.

3. Alignment
tc_align_top/2: all objs to minimum top row.
tc_align_left/2: all objs to minimum left col.

4. Packing
tc_pack_row/5: pack in a row with Gap between bboxes.
tc_pack_col/5: pack in a column with Gap between bboxes.

5. Spreading and Centering
tc_spread_h/4: redistribute at fixed column intervals.
tc_spread_v/4: redistribute at fixed row intervals.
tc_center/4: center in H x W canvas.
tc_flip_h/3: reflect within canvas width W.


Chapter 149: Object-List Correspondence and Matching (objmatch Pack, mx_*, Layer 178)

The objmatch pack finds correspondences between two obj(Color, Cells) term
lists using four matching strategies and provides delta analysis on pairs.

1. Matching Strategies
mx_by_color/3: match by same color.
mx_by_size/3: match by same cell count.
mx_by_form/3: match by same normalized shape (color and position ignored).
mx_by_nearest/3: greedy nearest-centroid matching.

2. Unmatched Objects
mx_unmatched/5: extract objects from each list not appearing in any pair.

3. Pair Filtering
mx_filter_changed_color/2: keep only pairs where color changed.
mx_filter_same_color/2: keep only pairs where color is unchanged.

4. Delta Extraction
mx_color_deltas/2: c1-c2 color change per pair.
mx_pos_deltas/2: dr(DR,DC) centroid displacement per pair.
mx_size_deltas/2: N2-N1 size change per pair.

5. Uniformity Tests
mx_all_same_color_delta/1: all pairs share the same color change.
mx_all_same_pos_delta/1: all pairs share the same positional delta.
mx_all_same_size_delta/1: all pairs share the same size change.

6. Positional Zip
mx_zip/3: index-wise pairing of two equal-length lists.

Chapter 150: Object Merging, Set Operations, and Component Splitting (objmerge Pack, mg_*, Layer 179)

The objmerge pack provides cell-set operations and structural operations on
obj(Color, Cells) terms.

1. Cell-Set Operations
mg_union_cells/4: sorted union of two obj cell sets.
mg_intersect_cells/4: sorted intersection.
mg_diff_cells/4: O1 minus O2 cell set.
mg_sym_diff_cells/4: symmetric difference.

2. Concatenation
mg_concat_h/4: place O2 right of O1 bbox with Gap.
mg_concat_v/4: place O2 below O1 bbox with Gap.

3. List Operations
mg_merge_list/3: union of all cells in a list of objs.
mg_subtract_list/4: remove cells of any obj in list from base.

4. Bounding-Box Operations
mg_expand_bbox/2: fill bounding box with obj's color.
mg_hollow_bbox/2: 1-cell-wide outer frame of bounding box.
mg_pad/4: add P layers of border cells with new color.

5. Connected Components
mg_split_cc4/2: split into 4-connected component objs.
mg_split_cc8/2: split into 8-connected component objs.
mg_n_components4/2: count 4-connected components.


Chapter 151: Object-List Aggregate Attribute Analysis (objattr, oa_*, Layer 180)

The objattr pack provides aggregate statistics and rankings over a list of obj(Color, Cells)
terms. It gives a reasoner a bird's-eye view of a scene without manual accumulation.

1. Cell and Color Counts
oa_total_cells/2: total cells across all objects.
oa_color_counts/2: Color-Count pairs (number of objects per color).
oa_cell_counts_by_color/2: Color-Total pairs (total cells per color).
oa_n_colors/2: number of distinct colors.
oa_n_objs_of_color/3: object count for a specific color.

2. Color Dominance
oa_dominant_color/2: color with the most total cells.
oa_rarest_color/2: color with the fewest total cells.
oa_unique_color/2: backtrackable; each color appearing in exactly one object.

3. Rankings
oa_size_rank/2: objects sorted by cell count descending.
oa_pos_rank/2: objects sorted by top-left corner, row-major order.
oa_majority_size/2: most frequently occurring cell count.

4. Uniformity Tests
oa_all_same_color/1: all objects share the same color.
oa_all_same_size/1: all objects have the same cell count.
oa_all_same_form/1: all objects have the same normalized shape (position and color ignored).


Chapter 152: Object-List Grouping by Shared Attribute (objgroup, og_*, Layer 181)

The objgroup pack partitions a list of obj(Color, Cells) terms into Key-ObjList groups
sharing a common attribute value. It fills the gap between objfilter (which selects
individual objects) and objattr (which aggregates over all objects).

1. Grouping Operations
og_by_color/2: partition into Color-ObjList groups, sorted by Color.
og_by_size/2: partition into Size-ObjList groups, sorted by Size ascending.
og_by_form/2: partition by normalized shape (translate-to-origin, sort cells).
og_by_row/2: partition by top-left row, sorted ascending.
og_by_col/2: partition by top-left col, sorted ascending.

2. Group Inspection
og_n_groups/2: count of groups.
og_n_members/3: filter groups to those with exactly N members.
og_singletons/2: groups with exactly 1 member.
og_largest/2: the group with the most members.
og_smallest/2: the group with the fewest members.

3. Group Analysis
og_all_same_size/1: all groups have the same member count.
og_sort_desc/2: sort groups by member count descending.
og_flat/2: flatten groups back to a single obj list.
og_filter_size/4: keep groups with member count in Min..Max inclusive.


Chapter 153: Scene-Level Comparison of Two Object Lists (scenecmp, sm_*, Layer 182)

The scenecmp pack compares two obj(Color, Cells) lists (Before and After) at the
inventory level without requiring object-to-object matching.

1. Introspection (Single Scene)
sm_n_objs/2: count of objects.
sm_total_cells/2: total cell count.
sm_colors/2: sorted distinct colors.
sm_forms/2: sorted distinct normalized shapes.

2. Symmetric Comparison
sm_same_n_objs/2: same object count.
sm_same_total_cells/2: same total cell count.
sm_same_colors/2: same color set.
sm_same_forms/2: same form set.

3. Asymmetric Comparison
sm_added_colors/3: colors in After not in Before.
sm_removed_colors/3: colors in Before not in After.
sm_added_forms/3: forms in After not in Before.
sm_removed_forms/3: forms in Before not in After.
sm_n_color_change/3: |added colors| + |removed colors|.
sm_any_change/2: any difference in count, color, or form; fails if none.


Chapter 154: Object-List Spatial and Attribute Query Against a Reference (objlocate, lq_*, Layer 183)

The objlocate pack queries a list of obj(Color, Cells) terms for those satisfying a
spatial or attribute relationship to a given reference object.

1. Directional Queries (by centroid)
lq_above/3: centroid row strictly less than Ref.
lq_below/3: centroid row strictly greater than Ref.
lq_left_of/3: centroid col strictly less than Ref.
lq_right_of/3: centroid col strictly greater than Ref.

2. Adjacency Queries
lq_touching4/3: 4-adjacent to Ref (Manhattan distance 1, no overlap).
lq_touching8/3: 8-adjacent to Ref (Chebyshev distance 1, no overlap).
lq_overlapping/3: sharing at least one cell with Ref.

3. Attribute Queries
lq_same_color/3: same color atom as Ref.
lq_same_form/3: same normalized shape as Ref (translate-to-origin, sort).
lq_aligned_h/3: same top-left row as Ref.
lq_aligned_v/3: same top-left column as Ref.

4. Distance Queries
lq_nearest/3: single object with smallest squared centroid distance to Ref.
lq_farthest/3: single object with largest squared centroid distance to Ref.
lq_n_touching4/3: count of 4-touching objects.


Chapter 155: scenexf Pack - Scene-Level Uniform Transformation (sx_*, Layer 184)

The scenexf pack applies transformations uniformly to every object in a scene
(a list of obj(Color, Cells) terms). Where objxf transforms a single object,
scenexf applies the same operation to the entire list. Layer 184, WP 205.

Loading:
:- use_module(pack(scenexf/prolog/scenexf)).

14 exported predicates:

1. Color Transformations
sx_recolor/4: replace objects of OldColor with NewColor (atom equality).
sx_recolor_all/3: set all objects to NewColor.
sx_apply_color_map/3: apply Old-New color pairs; unmatched objects unchanged.

2. Spatial Transformations
sx_shift/4: translate all objects by (DR, DC); DR/DC may be negative.
sx_to_origin/2: shift scene so bounding-box top-left is at r(0,0).
sx_reflect_h/3: flip all objects horizontally; r(R,C) -> r(R, Width-1-C).
sx_reflect_v/3: flip all objects vertically; r(R,C) -> r(Height-1-R, C).

3. Filtering
sx_remove_color/3: discard all objects of Color.
sx_keep_color/3: keep only objects of Color.

4. Ordering
sx_sort_size_desc/2: sort by cell count descending (negated-count key + msort).
sx_sort_size_asc/2: sort by cell count ascending.
sx_sort_pos/2: sort by top-left position row-major ascending.

5. Selection and Deduplication
sx_top_n/3: the N largest objects; if N > length, returns all sorted desc.
sx_dedup_form/2: keep first object of each distinct normalized shape.

All 41 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch166_SceneXf.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 156: ruleinfer Pack - Scene-Level Transformation Rule Inference (ri_*, Layer 185)

The ruleinfer pack infers which scene-level transformation was applied to a
Before scene to produce an After scene. Layer 185, WP 206.

Loading:
:- use_module(pack(ruleinfer/prolog/ruleinfer)).

Pairs are Before-After terms. A list of pairs: [B1-A1, B2-A2, ...].

14 exported predicates:

1. Inference (from one Before-After pair)
ri_infer_recolor/4: exactly one color changed (Old->New); count preserved.
ri_infer_recolor_all/3: all After objects have same color New.
ri_infer_color_map/3: full color substitution map from position-matched objects.
ri_infer_keep_color/3: After keeps only Color objects from Before.
ri_infer_remove_color/3: After is Before minus all Color objects.
ri_infer_shift/4: uniform shift DR/DC; verified via msort on shifted Before.
ri_infer_to_origin/2: After == Before shifted so scene bbox at r(0,0).

2. Consistency (verify rule across multiple pairs)
ri_consistent_recolor/3: recolor(Old,New) applies to all pairs.
ri_consistent_color_map/2: color map applies to all pairs.
ri_consistent_keep_color/2: keep_color(Color) applies to all pairs.
ri_consistent_remove_color/2: remove_color(Color) applies to all pairs.
ri_consistent_shift/3: shift(DR,DC) applies to all pairs.

3. Cross-pair queries
ri_all_same_n_objs/1: all pairs preserve object count.
ri_all_same_colors/1: all pairs preserve color sets.

All 41 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch167_RuleInfer.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 157: sceneapply Pack - Scene-Level Rule Term Evaluation Engine (sa_*, Layer 186)

The sceneapply pack applies symbolic rule terms to obj(Color, Cells) scene lists.
Companion to ruleinfer (ri_*): ruleinfer infers the rule, sceneapply executes it.
Layer 186, WP 207.

Loading:
:- use_module(pack(sceneapply/prolog/sceneapply)).

Rule terms: recolor(Old,New), recolor_all(New), color_map(Map), shift(DR,DC),
to_origin, reflect_h(Width), reflect_v(Height), remove_color(Color),
keep_color(Color), sort_size_desc, sort_size_asc, sort_pos, top_n(N), dedup_form.

14 exported predicates:

1. Application
sa_apply/3: dispatch on rule term; apply to scene.
sa_apply_seq/3: apply list of rules in order (recursive descent).

2. Verification
sa_verify/3: rule transforms Before to After (msort-insensitive).
sa_verify_seq/3: rule sequence transforms Before to After.
sa_verify_all/2: rule works on all Before-After pairs.
sa_verify_seq_all/2: rule sequence works on all pairs.

3. Classification
sa_rule_type/2: color|spatial|filter|order|dedup.
sa_is_color_rule/1, sa_is_spatial_rule/1, sa_is_filter_rule/1: type tests.

4. Utilities
sa_colors_affected/2: color atoms referenced in rule.
sa_compose/3: make two-step sequence [Rule1, Rule2].
sa_seq_len/2: length of rule sequence.
sa_rule_invertible/1: recolor, shift, reflect_h, reflect_v are invertible.

All 46 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch168_SceneApply.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 158: condxf Pack - Conditional and Selective Scene Transformation (xc_*, Layer 187)

The condxf pack applies transformations to a subset of objects in an
obj(Color, Cells) scene list - those satisfying a condition - while leaving
the remaining objects unchanged. Complements scenexf (uniform transforms).

:- use_module(pack(condxf/prolog/condxf)).

% Partition by color
xc_split_color([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], r, Reds, Others).
% Reds = [obj(r,[r(0,0)])], Others = [obj(b,[r(0,1)])]

% Recolor only matching objects
xc_recolor_matching([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], r, g, Out).
% Out = [obj(g,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])]

% Shift only red objects
xc_shift_color([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], r, 1, 0, Out).
% Out = [obj(r,[r(1,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])]

% Swap two colors throughout
xc_swap_colors([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], r, b, Out).
% Out = [obj(b,[r(0,0)]),obj(r,[r(0,1)])]

% Recolor by size comparison (gt/lt/eq/ge/le)
xc_recolor_by_size([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(r,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])], 1, gt, y, Out).
% Out = [obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(y,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])]

% Largest and smallest
xc_largest([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(r,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])], Obj).
% Obj = obj(r,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])

% Recolor the largest
xc_recolor_largest([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(r,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])], g, Out).
% Out = [obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(g,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])]

% Unique-size object
xc_unique_size([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)]),obj(p,[r(0,0),r(1,0),r(1,1)])], Obj).
% Obj = obj(p,[r(0,0),r(1,0),r(1,1)])

% Partition by size
xc_split_size([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(r,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])], 1, gt, Match, NoMatch).
% Match = [obj(r,[r(0,0),r(0,1),r(0,2)])], NoMatch = [obj(r,[r(0,0)])]

14 xc_* predicates. 41 tests pass. Layer 187.
All 41 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch169_CondXf.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 159: scenepair Pack - Holistic Before-After Scene Pair Analysis (ps_*, Layer 188)

The scenepair pack analyzes a Before and After scene pair holistically, computing
scene-level observables useful for transformation rule hypothesis ranking.

:- use_module(pack(scenepair/prolog/scenepair)).

% Count change
ps_same_n_objs([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], [obj(g,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])]).

% Added/removed objects (matched by color+form)
ps_added_objs([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], [obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], Added).
% Added = [obj(b,[r(0,1)])]

% Color set observables
ps_color_set_before([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], Colors).
% Colors = [b, r]
ps_same_color_set([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], [obj(r,[r(1,0)])]).

% Color delta (position-matched)
ps_color_delta([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], [obj(g,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], Delta).
% Delta = [r-g]

% Transformation detectors
ps_is_recolor([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], [obj(g,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])]).
ps_is_recolor_all([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], [obj(g,[r(0,0)])]).
ps_is_shift([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], [obj(r,[r(1,1)]),obj(b,[r(1,2)])], DR, DC).
% DR = 1, DC = 1
ps_is_to_origin([obj(r,[r(2,3)])], [obj(r,[r(0,0)])]).
ps_size_preserved([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], [obj(g,[r(0,0)])]).

14 ps_* predicates. 41 tests pass. Layer 188.
All 41 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch170_ScenePair.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 160: scenerank Pack - Rule Hypothesis Ranking for Scene Lists (rk_*, Layer 189)

The scenerank pack ranks candidate rule terms by coverage across Before-After
training pairs. A pair is the term Before-After where both are obj(Color,Cells) lists.

:- use_module(pack(scenerank/prolog/scenerank)).

Pairs = [[obj(r,[r(0,0)])]-[obj(g,[r(0,0)])], [obj(r,[r(1,0)])]-[obj(g,[r(1,0)])]].

% Coverage: how many pairs does this rule correctly transform?
rk_coverage(recolor(r,g), Pairs, N).    % N = 2
rk_coverage(recolor(r,b), Pairs, N).    % N = 0

% Consistency check
rk_consistent(recolor(r,g), Pairs).     % succeeds

% Inconsistent pairs
rk_inconsistent_pairs(recolor(r,b), Pairs, Bad).  % Bad = Pairs

% Rank candidates by coverage descending
rk_rank_rules([recolor(r,g), shift(1,0)], Pairs, Ranked).
% Ranked = [recolor(r,g), shift(1,0)]

% Best rule
rk_best_rule([recolor(r,b), recolor(r,g)], Pairs, Best).
% Best = recolor(r,g)

% Perfect rules (coverage == pair count)
rk_perfect_rules([recolor(r,g), shift(1,0)], Pairs, [recolor(r,g)]).

% Coverage ratio
rk_coverage_ratio(recolor(r,g), Pairs, Num/Den).  % 2/2

% Top 2 rules as Coverage-Rule pairs
rk_top_n_rules([recolor(r,g), shift(1,0), recolor(r,b)], Pairs, 2, TopN).
% TopN = [2-recolor(r,g), 0-shift(1,0)]  (order depends on pair content)

14 rk_* predicates. 41 tests pass. Layer 189.
All 41 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch171_SceneRank.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 161: colortable Pack - Color Substitution Table Learning and Application (ct_*, Layer 190)

The colortable pack learns and applies color substitution tables from obj(Color,
Cells) scene pairs. A table is a list of From-To color pairs.

:- use_module(pack(colortable/prolog/colortable)).

% Infer a color map from one Before-After scene pair
ct_infer_from_pair([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], [obj(b,[r(0,0)])], Map).
% Map contains r-b

% Learn a consistent map from multiple pairs
ct_learn_map(Pairs, Map, Stats).

% Apply a learned map to a new scene
ct_apply_map(Map, [obj(r,[r(0,0)])], Out).
% Out = [obj(b,[r(0,0)])]

14 ct_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 190.
All 44 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch172_ColorTable.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 162: gridsolve Pack - End-to-End Scene Puzzle Solver (gs_*, Layer 191)

The gridsolve pack integrates rule inference, ranking, and application into a
single end-to-end scene puzzle solver. Given a list of obj-list training pairs,
it selects and applies the best transformation rule.

:- use_module(pack(gridsolve/prolog/gridsolve)).

% Infer and apply the best rule to test input
gs_solve(TrainingPairs, TestScene, OutputScene, Rule).

% Check how many pairs a rule explains
gs_coverage(Rule, Pairs, N).

% Verify a rule is consistent with all training pairs
gs_consistent(Rule, Pairs).

14 gs_* predicates. 41 tests pass. Layer 191.
All 41 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch173_GridSolve.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 163: transformgen Pack - Scene Rule Candidate Generation (tg_*, Layer 192)

The transformgen pack systematically generates candidate transformation rule terms
from a scene. These candidates are then ranked and tested by gridsolve or scenerank.

:- use_module(pack(transformgen/prolog/transformgen)).

% Collect all distinct colors in a scene
tg_scene_colors([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(b,[r(1,0)])], Colors).
% Colors = [b, r]

% Generate recolor-all candidates
tg_recolor_candidates([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], Cands).

% Generate full candidate set (recolor, shift, etc.)
tg_candidates(Scene, Pairs, Cands).

14 tg_* predicates. 42 tests pass. Layer 192.
All 42 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch174_TransformGen.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 164: multicolor Pack - Multi-Color Scene Analysis (mc_*, Layer 193)

The multicolor pack provides frequency analysis, color partitioning, and
color-indexed queries over obj(Color,Cells) scene lists.

:- use_module(pack(multicolor/prolog/multicolor)).

% Count objects per color
mc_color_counts([obj(r,[r(0,0)]),obj(r,[r(1,0)]),obj(b,[r(0,1)])], Counts).
% Counts = [b-1, r-2]

% How many objects of a given color
mc_count_of_color([obj(r,[r(0,0)])], r, N).
% N = 1

% The color appearing most often
mc_majority_color(Scene, Color).

14 mc_* predicates. 42 tests pass. Layer 193.
All 42 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch175_MultiColor.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 165: sceneinv Pack - Scene Invariant Detection (si_*, Layer 194)

The sceneinv pack detects invariant properties of a scene across all training
Before-After pairs: properties that hold in every Before or every After.

:- use_module(pack(sceneinv/prolog/sceneinv)).

% Count of Before objects invariant across all pairs
si_n_before(Pairs, N).

% Count of After objects invariant across all pairs
si_n_after(Pairs, N).

% Colors invariant in all Before scenes
si_colors_before(Pairs, Colors).

14 si_* predicates. 46 tests pass. Layer 194.
All 46 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch176_SceneInv.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 166: seqinfer Pack - Sequential Rule Inference (sq_*, Layer 195)

The seqinfer pack searches for a sequence of transformation steps that maps each
Before scene to its corresponding After scene, supporting multi-step explanations.

:- use_module(pack(seqinfer/prolog/seqinfer)).

% Apply a sequence of rules to a scene
sq_apply(Scene, [recolor(r,b), shift(1,0)], OutScene).

% Verify a rule sequence explains all training pairs
sq_verify(Pairs, Seq, Stats).

% Search for a short rule sequence explaining all pairs
sq_infer(Pairs, Seq).

14 sq_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 195.
All 44 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch177_SeqInfer.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 167: gridquery Pack - Grid Query and Manipulation (gq_*, Layer 196)

The gridquery pack provides read and manipulation operations directly on raw grid
format (list of rows, each a list of color atoms).

:- use_module(pack(gridquery/prolog/gridquery)).

% Grid dimensions
gq_size([[r,b],[r,r],[r,r]], H, W).
% H = 3, W = 2

% Cell value at (R, C)
gq_at([[r,b],[r,r]], 0, 1, V).
% V = b

% Most frequent color
gq_majority([[r,b],[r,r]], C).
% C = r

14 gq_* predicates. 45 tests pass. Layer 196.
All 45 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch178_GridQuery.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 168: gridparse Pack - Grid Parse and Object Extraction (gp_*, Layer 197)

The gridparse pack converts between raw grid format and the obj(Color,Cells) scene
representation. It provides 4-connected flood fill for object segmentation.

:- use_module(pack(gridparse/prolog/gridparse)).

% Identify the background color
gp_bg_color([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], BgColor).
% BgColor = r

% Convert raw grid to scene (obj list)
gp_grid_to_scene([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], r, Scene).
% Scene = [obj(b, [r(1,1)])]

% Convert scene back to grid
gp_scene_to_grid([obj(b,[r(1,1)])], 3, 3, r, Grid).
% Grid = [[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]]

14 gp_* predicates. 43 tests pass. Layer 197.
All 43 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch179_GridParse.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 169: gridtask Pack - Grid Task End-to-End Solver (gt_*, Layer 198)

The gridtask pack infers and applies simple transformation rules directly on raw
grid format, without converting to obj terms first.

:- use_module(pack(gridtask/prolog/gridtask)).

% Count differing cells
gt_n_changed([[r,r],[r,r]], [[r,b],[r,r]], N).
% N = 1

% Infer strategy and apply to test grid
gt_solve([[[r,r]]-[[b,b]]], [[r,r]], Out, Rule).
% Rule = color_map([r-b]), Out = [[b,b]]

% Pixel accuracy of a rule on one pair
gt_pair_score(identity, [[r,r]], [[r,r]], Score).
% Score = 1.0

14 gt_* predicates. 51 tests pass. Layer 198.
All 51 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch180_GridTask.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 170: gridnbr Pack - Grid Neighbor Analysis (gn_*, Layer 199)

The gridnbr pack analyzes cell neighborhoods in raw grids: neighbor values,
color counts, morphological dilation and erosion, and Conway-like steps.

:- use_module(pack(gridnbr/prolog/gridnbr)).

% 4-connected neighbor values at (R,C)
gn_nbr4([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], 1, 1, Vals).
% Vals = [r,r,r,r]

% Count r-neighbors at center
gn_count4([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], 1, 1, r, N).
% N = 4

% Morphological dilation: grow b by one layer
gn_expand_color([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], b, E).
% b spreads to left, right, above, below center

% Morphological erosion: shrink a 3x3 b block
gn_shrink_color(G5x5, b, S).
% outer ring of b removed; inner center remains b

% Mark border cells (foreground touching background)
gn_mark_border([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], r, MG).
% MG[1][1] = border

% Conway step: kill isolated b (0 b-neighbors)
gn_conway_step([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], b, 0, NG).
% NG = all r

14 gn_* predicates. 51 tests pass. Layer 199.
All 51 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch181_GridNbr.txt for the full chapter.

Chapter 171: griddist Pack - Grid Distance Transform (gd_*, Layer 200)

The griddist pack computes spatial distances between cells in raw grids and
provides operations built on those distances: distance maps, BFS flood with
walls, Voronoi assignment, zone marking, morphological N-step expansion and
erosion, distance-based recoloring, equidistance detection, and farthest-cell
lookup.

:- use_module(pack(griddist/prolog/griddist)).

% Manhattan distance between (0,0) and (2,3): 2+3 = 5
gd_manhattan(0, 0, 2, 3, D).
% D = 5

% Chebyshev distance: max(2,3) = 3
gd_chebyshev(0, 0, 2, 3, D).
% D = 3

% Distance map: each cell holds min Manhattan dist to nearest b
gd_dist_map([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], b, DMap).
% DMap = [[2,1,2],[1,0,1],[2,1,2]]

% Voronoi: background cells assigned color of nearest foreground cell
gd_voronoi([[r,r,r],[b,r,g],[r,r,r]], VG).
% VG = [[b,b,g],[b,b,g],[b,b,g]]

% BFS flood: distances from (0,0) with b as wall
gd_bfs_flood([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], 0, 0, b, DG).
% DG[1][1] = 9999 (blocked); DG[1][2] = 3 (detour via top row)

% N-step expand: grow b outward by 1 step
gd_expand_n([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], b, 1, E).
% E = [[r,b,r],[b,b,b],[r,b,r]]

% N-step shrink: erode b by 1 step (removes b at center; it touches r)
gd_shrink_n([[r,r,r],[r,b,r],[r,r,r]], b, 1, S).
% S = [[r,r,r],[r,r,r],[r,r,r]]

14 gd_* predicates. 53 tests pass. Layer 200.
All 53 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch182_GridDist.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 172: gridperiod Pack - Grid Periodic Pattern Detection and Extension (gper_*, Layer 201)

The gridperiod pack detects and exploits periodic structure in raw grid format
(list-of-lists of color atoms). It finds the smallest repeating period along
rows and columns, extracts the fundamental tile, tiles small grids to larger
target sizes, measures autocorrelation, and applies wrap-around shifts.

:- use_module(pack(gridperiod/prolog/gridperiod)).

% Detect tile dimensions of a 4x4 repeating pattern:
gper_tile_dims([[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r],[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r]], PH, PW).
% PH = 2, PW = 2

% Extract the tile and reconstruct:
gper_extract_tile([[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r],[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r]], 2, 2, Tile).
% Tile = [[r,b],[b,r]]
gper_tile_grid([[r,b],[b,r]], 4, 4, G).
% G = [[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r],[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r]]

% Extend a small grid to a larger target size:
gper_extend([[r,b],[b,r]], 4, 4, E).
% E = [[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r],[r,b,r,b],[b,r,b,r]]

% Measure autocorrelation at lag 2 (score 1.0 = perfect period):
gper_autocorr_row([[r,b],[b,r],[r,b],[b,r]], 2, S).
% S = 1.0

% Shift rows down by 1 with wrap-around:
gper_shift_wrap_rows([[r,r,r],[b,b,b],[g,g,g]], 1, S).
% S = [[g,g,g],[r,r,r],[b,b,b]]

14 gper_* predicates. 51 tests pass. Layer 201.
All 51 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch183_GridPeriod.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 173: gridscale Pack - Grid Block-Pixel Scaling (gsc_*, Layer 202)

The gridscale pack provides integer block-pixel scaling operations on raw grid
format: upsample, downsample, majority-vote downscaling, scale-factor detection,
tile inference, nearest-neighbor resize, stride subsampling, border padding, and
factor-aligned cropping.

:- use_module(pack(gridscale/prolog/gridscale)).

% Upsample 2x2 checkerboard by 2 to 4x4:
gsc_upsample([[r,b],[b,r]], 2, S).
% S = [[r,r,b,b],[r,r,b,b],[b,b,r,r],[b,b,r,r]]

% Downsample back (fails if blocks are not uniform):
gsc_downsample([[r,r,b,b],[r,r,b,b],[b,b,r,r],[b,b,r,r]], 2, D).
% D = [[r,b],[b,r]]

% Detect scale factor between two grids:
gsc_scale_factor([[r,b],[b,r]], [[r,r,r,b,b,b],[r,r,r,b,b,b],[r,r,r,b,b,b],[b,b,b,r,r,r],[b,b,b,r,r,r],[b,b,b,r,r,r]], N).
% N = 3

% Infer the smallest tile of a repeated-tile grid:
gsc_infer_tile([[r,r,b,b],[r,r,b,b],[b,b,r,r],[b,b,r,r]], T).
% T = [[r,b],[b,r]]

% Pad with a border of color g:
gsc_pad([[r,b],[b,r]], 1, g, P).
% P = [[g,g,g,g],[g,r,b,g],[g,b,r,g],[g,g,g,g]]

14 gsc_* predicates. 60 tests pass. Layer 202.
All 60 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch184_GridScale.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 174: gridrun Pack - Grid Run-Length Encoding and Stripe Analysis (grl_*, Layer 203)

The gridrun pack provides run-length encoding and stripe analysis for raw grids.
A run is a maximal sequence of identical adjacent cells, encoded as Color-Len.

:- use_module(pack(gridrun/prolog/gridrun)).

% Encode row 0 of a 3-row grid:
grl_row_runs([[r,r,b,g,g],[b,b,b,r,r],[g,r,g,r,g]], 0, R).
% R = [r-2, b-1, g-2]

% Decode back:
grl_decode([r-2, b-1, g-2], L).
% L = [r,r,b,g,g]

% Detect horizontal stripes:
grl_is_striped_h([[r,r,r],[b,b,b],[g,g,g]]).  % succeeds

% Get stripe colors:
grl_stripe_colors_h([[r,r,r],[b,b,b],[g,g,g]], C).
% C = [r,b,g]

% Find longest run in row 1:
grl_max_run([[r,r,b,g,g],[b,b,b,r,r]], 1, C, L).
% C = b, L = 3

% Detect alternating pattern:
grl_alternating([[r,b,r,b,r,b]], 0).  % succeeds

14 grl_* predicates. 55 tests pass. Layer 203.
All 55 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch185_GridRun.txt for the full chapter.

gpa_wavefront([[p,p,p],[p,p,p],[p,p,p]], 1, 1, 2, w, Cells).
% Cells = [0-0, 0-2, 2-0, 2-2]  (4 corners)

Chapter 182: gridxform Pack - Rotations, Flips, Transpose, Crop, Pad, Scale, Tile, and Canonical Form (gx_*, Layer 207)

The gridxform pack (Layer 207) provides fourteen predicates for geometric
transformation of raw grids: three rotations (90/180/270 CW), horizontal and
vertical flips, main-diagonal transpose, anti-diagonal flip, crop to explicit
or auto-detected bounding box, pad with background color, integer upscale,

:- use_module(pack(gridxform/prolog/gridxform)).

% R = [[g,d,a],[h,e,b],[i,f,c]]

gx_transpose([[a,b,c],[d,e,f]], R).
% R = [[a,d],[b,e],[c,f]]

% R = [[r,r],[r,r]]

% Tile a 2x2 checkerboard to 4x4:
gx_tile([[r,x],[x,r]], 4, 4, R).
% R = [[r,x,r,x],[x,r,x,r],[r,x,r,x],[x,r,x,r]]

% Scale up by 2:
gx_scale([[r,x],[x,r]], 2, R).
% R = [[r,r,x,x],[r,r,x,x],[x,x,r,r],[x,x,r,r]]

Chapter 184: gridsymm Pack - Symmetry Detection, Completion, Violations, and Score (gsm_*, Layer 208)

The gridsymm pack (Layer 208) provides fourteen predicates for testing,
completing, and measuring symmetry in symbolic grids across six types:
top-bottom (h), left-right (v), main diagonal (d1), anti-diagonal (d2),
90-degree rotation (rot90), and 180-degree rotation (rot180).

:- use_module(pack(gridsymm/prolog/gridsymm)).

% Test top-bottom symmetry:
gsm_sym_h([[r,x,r],[x,b,x],[r,x,r]]).
% true

% Enumerate all symmetries:
gsm_symmetries([[r,x,r],[x,x,x],[r,x,r]], Syms).
% Syms = [h, v, d1, d2, rot90, rot180]

% Complete grid to be horizontally symmetric:
gsm_complete_h([[r,b],[x,x]], top, R).
% R = [[r,b],[r,b]]

% Find symmetry violations:
gsm_violations_h([[r,x],[b,x]], Cells).
% Cells = [0-0]  (row 0, col 0: r != b)

% Compute symmetry score (fraction of matching pairs):
gsm_score([[r,x,r],[r,x,b],[r,x,r]], v, S).
% S = 0.666...

14 gsm_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 208.
All 44 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch190_GridSymm.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 186: gridcolor Pack - Color Analysis, Recolor, Histogram, Threshold, and Dominant (gc_*, Layer 209)

The gridcolor pack (Layer 209) provides fourteen predicates for color analysis
and transformation of symbolic grids: counting, histograms, frequency ranking,
recoloring, color mapping, binary thresholding, and dominant color detection.

:- use_module(pack(gridcolor/prolog/gridcolor)).

gc_count([[r,r,x],[r,x,b],[x,x,r]], r, N). % N = 4

% Histogram:
gc_histogram([[r,x],[r,r]], Pairs). % Pairs = [r-3, x-1]

% Recolor x to background:
gc_recolor([[r,x],[x,r]], x, bg, R). % R = [[r,bg],[bg,r]]

% Color map (swap colors):
gc_color_map([[r,g],[g,r]], [r-b, g-r], R). % R = [[b,r],[r,b]]

% Threshold: only red cells selected:
gc_threshold([[r,x,b],[r,r,x]], [r], sel, bg, R).
% R = [[sel,bg,bg],[sel,sel,bg]]

% Most frequent non-background:
gc_dominant([[x,r,x],[x,r,r]], x, Color). % Color = r

Chapter 187: gridstitch Pack - Concatenation, Splitting, Tiling, Border, and Repetition (gst_*, Layer 210)

The gridstitch pack (Layer 210) provides fourteen predicates for assembling
and disassembling symbolic grids.

:- use_module(pack(gridstitch/prolog/gridstitch)).

Concatenation: gst_concat_h/3 places two grids side by side (matching row
counts); gst_concat_v/3 stacks two grids top to bottom (matching column
counts); gst_hstack/2 and gst_vstack/2 reduce a list of grids using the
two-grid concat predicates.

Splitting: gst_split_h/4 and gst_split_v/4 split at a given index;
gst_halves_h/3 and gst_halves_v/3 split at the midpoint; gst_quadrants/5
extracts all four even-size quadrants.

Tiling: gst_tile_grid/4 arranges NR*NC sub-grids in a tiled layout;
gst_repeat_h/3 and gst_repeat_v/3 repeat a grid N times in one direction.

Border: gst_add_border/4 wraps in an N-cell Color border; gst_strip_border/3
removes N cells from all sides (inverse of add_border).

14 gst_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 210.
All 44 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch192_GridStitch.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 188: gridedge Pack - Edge Detection, Boundary Analysis, Corners, and Transition Maps (ge_*, Layer 211)

The gridedge pack (Layer 211) provides fourteen predicates for detecting edges
and structural features of symbolic grids.

:- use_module(pack(gridedge/prolog/gridedge)).

Edge detection: ge_edge_cells/3 and ge_edge_cells8/3 collect FgColor cells
with at least one differently-colored 4/8-connected neighbor (using once/1 to
prevent duplicate entries when multiple neighbors qualify); ge_is_edge/3 tests
a single cell; ge_edge_count/3 counts edge cells.

Boundary analysis: ge_boundary/4 finds Color1 cells adjacent to Color2;
ge_inner_border/3 finds BgColor cells adjacent to non-BgColor; ge_outer_border/3
finds non-FgColor cells adjacent to FgColor.

Grid construction: ge_edge_grid/4 replaces edge cells with EdgeColor.

Structural features: ge_corners/3 finds cells with 2+ differing neighbors;
ge_endpoints/3 finds cells with exactly 1 same-color neighbor; ge_smooth_cells/3
finds cells with no differing neighbors; ge_neighbors_diff/4 lists positions
of 4-neighbors with different color.

Transition map: ge_transition_map/2 builds a grid where each cell value is the
count (0..4) of differently-colored 4-neighbors.

14 ge_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 211.
All 44 tests pass. See docs/tutorial/Ch193_GridEdge.txt for the full chapter.


Chapter 189: gridmorph Pack - Morphological Operations on Symbolic Grids (gmo_*, Layer 212)

The gridmorph pack (Layer 212) provides fourteen predicates for morphological
operations on symbolic grids: dilation, erosion, opening, closing, single-step
variants, inner and outer boundary extraction, morphological gradient, top-hat
and bottom-hat transforms, hole filling, border-reachable flood fill, and
connected-component size filtering.

:- use_module(pack(gridmorph/prolog/gridmorph)).

gmo_dilate/4 expands FgColor by N layers; gmo_erode/4 shrinks by N layers.
gmo_open/4 removes small FgColor protrusions; gmo_close/4 fills small holes.
gmo_boundary_inner/3 returns FgColor cells on the inner border;
gmo_boundary_outer/3 returns non-FgColor cells on the outer border;
gmo_gradient_cells/3 returns their union.
gmo_top_hat/4 highlights cells removed by opening;
gmo_bottom_hat/4 highlights cells added by closing.
gmo_fill_holes/4 fills enclosed background regions with FgColor.
gmo_connected_border/4 BFS-floods from the grid border through TargetColor cells.
gmo_remove_small/5 removes FgColor components below a size threshold.

14 gmo_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 212.


Chapter 190: gridconv Pack - Sliding Window, Pattern Matching, and Square Morphology (gcv_*, Layer 213)

The gridconv pack (Layer 213) provides fourteen predicates for grid convolution
operations: sliding window extraction, window statistics (majority, uniformity,
density), pattern (subgrid) matching, hot spot detection, and square structuring
element morphology (dilation, erosion, and first-match replacement).

:- use_module(pack(gridconv/prolog/gridconv)).

Window radius N gives a (2N+1) x (2N+1) neighborhood. gcv_window/6 extracts
it with out-of-bounds fill; gcv_count_in/6 counts a Color in the window;
gcv_density_map/4 builds a per-cell count grid; gcv_majority/5 returns the
most frequent color; gcv_majority_map/3 applies this over the whole grid;
gcv_uniform_at/5 tests window uniformity; gcv_uniform_cells/3 collects
uniform positions.

gcv_find_pattern/3 finds all subgrid matches; gcv_count_pattern/3 counts
them; gcv_has_pattern/2 tests for existence.
gcv_hot_spots/5 returns cells where Color density meets a threshold.
gcv_dilate_sq/4 and gcv_erode_sq/4 apply square SE morphology.
gcv_replace_pattern/4 replaces the first pattern match.

14 gcv_* predicates. 46 tests pass. Layer 213.


Chapter 191: gridgraph Pack - Region Adjacency Graph Analysis (ggr_*, Layer 214)

The gridgraph pack (Layer 214) provides fourteen predicates for region adjacency
graph (RAG) analysis. A "region" is all cells of a given color. The pack covers
color pair adjacency, per-color neighbor enumeration, full adjacency graph
construction, degree computation, directional shared border analysis, isolated
and enclosed color detection, horizontal/vertical spanning detection, color
merging, and 4-connected component counting and partitioning.

:- use_module(pack(gridgraph/prolog/gridgraph)).

ggr_adj_colors/2 collects sorted (C1-C2, C1@<C2) pairs of 4-adjacent colors;
ggr_adj_of/3 returns colors adjacent to a given color; ggr_adj_graph/2 builds
the full adjacency list; ggr_are_adj/3 tests adjacency. ggr_color_degree/3
counts adjacent colors; ggr_shared_border/4 collects the boundary cells;
ggr_border_length/4 counts them. ggr_isolated_colors/2 finds degree-0 colors;
ggr_enclosed_colors/3 finds colors fully surrounded by OuterColor with no
border cells. ggr_spanning_h/2 and ggr_spanning_v/2 find colors crossing
the grid. ggr_merge_colors/4 replaces one color with another. 
ggr_color_components/3 counts connected components; ggr_component_cells/3
partitions them.

14 ggr_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 214.


Chapter 192: griddiag Pack - Grid Diagonal Analysis (gdi_*, Layer 215)

The griddiag pack (Layer 215) provides symbolic analysis of both main
diagonals (cells where C - R = K) and anti-diagonals (cells where R + C = K)
of symbolic grids. Main diagonal K = 0 is the principal diagonal.

:- use_module(pack(griddiag/prolog/griddiag)).

gdi_main_diag/3 extracts values on main diagonal K in ascending R order;
gdi_anti_diag/3 extracts values on anti-diagonal K in ascending C order;
gdi_trace/2 returns the principal diagonal (K=0, length = min(H,W)).
gdi_all_main_diags/2 and gdi_all_anti_diags/2 return all H+W-1 diagonals
or anti-diagonals as lists of value lists.

gdi_main_count/4 and gdi_anti_count/4 count Color occurrences on a specific
diagonal. gdi_main_uniform/3 and gdi_anti_uniform/3 return yes/no for whether
all values on a diagonal are equal. gdi_uniform_main_diags/2 and
gdi_uniform_anti_diags/2 collect K values of uniform diagonals. 
gdi_diag_length/3 gives the cell count of main diagonal K.

gdi_set_main_diag/4 and gdi_set_anti_diag/4 return a modified grid with all
cells on the specified diagonal set to a new Color.

14 gdi_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 215.


Chapter 193: gridframe Pack - Grid Frame Analysis (gfr_*, Layer 216)

The gridframe pack (Layer 216) treats a grid as a set of concentric rings
(frames). Frame depth D is the set of cells (R,C) where the expression
min(R, H-1-R, C, W-1-C) equals D. Frame 0 is the outermost border.

:- use_module(pack(gridframe/prolog/gridframe)).

gfr_cell_depth/4 computes the depth of a specific cell; gfr_frame/3 returns
all cells at depth D as R-C pairs; gfr_max_depth/2 returns floor((min(H,W)-1)/2);
gfr_frame_count/2 returns max_depth+1. gfr_peel/2 returns the inner (H-2)x(W-2)
grid after stripping the outermost frame (fails for grids smaller than 3x3).
gfr_all_frames/2 lists all frame cell-lists in depth order.

gfr_frame_uniform/3 returns yes/no for frame uniformity; gfr_frame_colors/3
gives distinct colors in a frame; gfr_count_in_frame/4 counts a specific color.
gfr_set_frame/4 sets frame D to Color; gfr_fill_frames/3 assigns Colors[D] to
each frame. gfr_uniform_frames/2 collects depths of uniform frames.
gfr_innermost/2 returns cells at max depth; gfr_onion_layers/2 returns D-Color
pairs for all uniform frames, enabling onion/target pattern detection.

14 gfr_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 216.


Chapter 194: gridspiral Pack - Grid Spiral Traversal (gsp_*, Layer 217)

The gridspiral pack (Layer 217) provides clockwise spiral traversal: starting
at (0,0), moving right, down, left, up, then repeating for inner frames.

:- use_module(pack(gridspiral/prolog/gridspiral)).

gsp_spiral/2 returns R-C pairs in spiral order; gsp_read_spiral/2 reads values;
gsp_write_spiral/3 and gsp_fill_spiral/3 write values into spiral positions.
gsp_spiral_length/2 returns H*W; gsp_spiral_index/4 finds a cell's position;
gsp_nth_spiral/4 finds the cell at position N. gsp_frame_spiral/3 returns the
spiral for a single frame D; gsp_all_frame_spirals/2 lists all frames.
gsp_spiral_uniform/2 tests uniformity; gsp_spiral_reversed/2 reverses order;
gsp_spiral_count/3 counts a color. gsp_rotate_spiral/3 rotates values N steps
along the spiral; gsp_spiral_slice/4 returns spiral cells from Start to End.

14 gsp_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 217.


Chapter 195: griddelta Pack - Grid Delta Analysis (gdt_*, Layer 218)

The griddelta pack (Layer 218) compares two same-size grids, detecting changes
and mapping transitions. gdt_compatible/2 tests dimension compatibility;
gdt_diff_cells/3 finds changed cells; gdt_diff_count/3 counts them; gdt_same_cells/3
finds unchanged cells. gdt_changed_pairs/3 maps R-C-(Old->New) for each change;
gdt_color_changes/4 counts changes from a specific color; gdt_changed_colors/3
returns new colors. gdt_apply_delta/4 applies G1→G2 changes to a third grid;
gdt_invert_delta/3 reverses them. gdt_diff_rows/3 and gdt_diff_cols/3 find
changed rows and columns. gdt_agree_region/6 tests a rectangular sub-region.
gdt_overlay/4 overlays G2 on G1 with Marker as transparent. gdt_is_identity/2
tests if two grids are identical.

:- use_module(pack(griddelta/prolog/griddelta)).

14 gdt_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 218.


Chapter 196: gridrowcol Pack - Grid Row and Column Comparative Analysis (grc_*, Layer 219)

The gridrowcol pack (Layer 219) treats rows and columns as first-class list
objects for comparison, sorting, and lookup. grc_row/3 extracts a row; grc_col/3
extracts a column as a top-to-bottom list. grc_row_uniform/3 and grc_col_uniform/3
test uniformity and bind the shared color. grc_equal_rows/3 and grc_equal_cols/3
test whether two rows or columns are identical. grc_row_positions/3 and
grc_col_positions/3 return the indices of rows or columns matching a given pattern.
grc_matching_rows/2 and grc_matching_cols/2 return all ordered pairs of identical
rows or columns. grc_unique_rows/2 and grc_unique_cols/2 return indices of rows or
columns that appear exactly once. grc_row_sort/2 sorts rows; grc_col_sort/2
reorders columns by their lexicographic content.

:- use_module(pack(gridrowcol/prolog/gridrowcol)).

14 grc_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 219.


Chapter 197: gridseg Pack - Grid Segmentation by Separator Rows and Columns (gsg_*, Layer 220)

The gridseg pack (Layer 220) detects uniform-color separator rows and columns
and uses them to split, trim, and extract panels from a raw grid. gsg_is_sep_row/3
and gsg_is_sep_col/3 test uniformity and bind the separator color. gsg_sep_rows/3
and gsg_sep_cols/3 collect all separator indices. gsg_split_h/3 splits by
separator rows into sub-grids (consecutive separators = one boundary, no empty
segments). gsg_split_v/3 splits by separator columns. gsg_segment_count_h/3 and
gsg_segment_count_v/3 count segments. gsg_panels/3 splits by both separator kinds
and returns all rectangular panels as a flat row-major list. gsg_panel_count/3
counts panels. gsg_trim_h/3 removes border separator rows; gsg_trim_v/3 removes
border separator columns; gsg_trim/3 removes both. gsg_sep_color/2 finds the
unique separator color used in the grid.

:- use_module(pack(gridseg/prolog/gridseg)).

14 gsg_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 220.


Chapter 198: gridhist Pack - Grid Histogram Analysis (ghst_*, Layer 221)

The gridhist pack (Layer 221) provides per-row and per-column color frequency
histograms, modal color lookup, count queries, diversity measurement, and
row/column selection by frequency threshold. ghst_row_hist/3 returns the sorted
Color-Count histogram for row R. ghst_col_hist/3 does the same for column C.
ghst_all_row_hists/2 and ghst_all_col_hists/2 return histograms for all rows
or columns. ghst_modal_row/3 and ghst_modal_col/3 find the most frequent color.
ghst_row_count/4 and ghst_col_count/4 count a specific color. ghst_row_entropy/3
and ghst_col_entropy/3 return the number of distinct colors. ghst_max_row/3 finds
the row with the highest count of a color (ties: highest index). ghst_min_row/3
finds the row with the lowest non-zero count (ties: lowest index). ghst_rows_with/4
and ghst_cols_with/4 return indices where a color appears >= N times.

:- use_module(pack(gridhist/prolog/gridhist)).

14 ghst_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 221.

Chapter 199: gridpos Pack - Grid Positional Analysis (gps_*, Layer 222)

The gridpos pack (Layer 222) provides fourteen predicates for extracting sub-grids
and cell positions based on geometric position: halves, quadrants, even/odd rows
and columns, checkerboard patterns, center cell, corner cells, and center cross.
gps_top_half/2 returns rows [0, H//2); gps_bottom_half/2 returns rows [H//2, H).
gps_left_half/2 and gps_right_half/2 do the same for columns. gps_quadrant/3
takes tl/tr/bl/br and returns the corresponding rectangular sub-grid.
gps_even_rows/2 and gps_odd_rows/2 filter by row parity; gps_even_cols/2 and
gps_odd_cols/2 filter by column parity. gps_checkerboard/3 returns R-C pairs
where (R+C) mod 2 = Parity (Parity must be bound). gps_center_cell/3 returns
the center coordinates (H//2, W//2). gps_corners/2 returns four R-C-Value terms
for the corner cells. gps_cross_h/3 and gps_cross_v/3 return the center row and
center column respectively.

:- use_module(pack(gridpos/prolog/gridpos)).

14 gps_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 222.

Chapter 200: gridgrav Pack - Grid Gravity Simulation (gv_*, Layer 223)

The gridgrav pack (Layer 223) provides fourteen predicates for simulating gravity
within a raw grid. Non-background (non-bg) cells are those whose color differs
from a user-specified BgColor. gv_fall_down/3 settles non-bg cells to the bottom
of each column; gv_fall_up/3 to the top; gv_fall_left/3 to the left of each row;
gv_fall_right/3 to the right. All four preserve relative order within each row or
column. gv_col_pile_h/4 and gv_row_pile_w/4 count non-bg cells in a column or row.
gv_all_col_piles/3 and gv_all_row_piles/3 return these counts for all columns or
rows. gv_floating_cells/3 returns R-C pairs of non-bg cells with a bg cell directly
below (cells that would move under down gravity). gv_settled/2 succeeds if no
floating cells exist. gv_col_gap_above/4 counts bg cells at the top of a column
above the first non-bg cell. gv_max_col_pile/3 and gv_min_col_pile/3 find the
column with the most or fewest non-bg cells (ties: lowest index).
gv_non_bg_count/3 returns the total non-bg cell count.

:- use_module(pack(gridgrav/prolog/gridgrav)).

14 gv_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 223.

Chapter 201: gridtile Pack - Grid Tiling Pattern Analysis (gti_*, Layer 224)

The gridtile pack (Layer 224) provides fourteen predicates for detecting, extracting,
verifying, and constructing tiling patterns in raw grids. A grid has vertical period
P if H mod P = 0 and every row R equals row R mod P; horizontal period is defined
similarly for columns. gti_h_period/2 and gti_w_period/2 find the smallest periods.
gti_tile_size/3 returns both. gti_is_tiling/3 verifies a given (TH, TW) pair.
gti_extract_tile/4 extracts the top-left TH x TW sub-grid as the canonical tile.
gti_tile_to_grid/4 builds any H x W grid by repeating a tile (cell R,C = Tile[R mod
TH][C mod TW]). gti_row_is_periodic/3 and gti_col_is_periodic/3 check periodicity
of individual rows and columns. gti_tile_count_h/3 and gti_tile_count_w/3 count
complete tile rows and columns. gti_matches_tile/4 checks if a specific sub-grid
equals a tile. gti_tile_offset/5 finds the tile-relative position of a cell.
gti_all_tiles/4 lists all tile sub-grids in row-major order. gti_crop_to_tile/2
extracts the minimal tile.

:- use_module(pack(gridtile/prolog/gridtile)).

14 gti_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 224.

Chapter 202: gridmark Pack - Grid Marking and Annotation (gmk_*, Layer 225)

The gridmark pack (Layer 225) provides fourteen predicates for creating annotated
copies of a grid with specific cells overwritten by a mark color. All operations
are non-destructive. gmk_mark_cell/5 marks a single cell. gmk_mark_cells/4 marks
a list of R-C pairs. gmk_mark_row/4 and gmk_mark_col/4 mark entire rows or
columns. gmk_mark_rect/7 marks a rectangular region. gmk_mark_border/3 marks the
outer ring. gmk_mark_diagonal/3 marks cells where R=C. gmk_mark_anti_diagonal/3
marks cells where R+C=W-1. gmk_mark_corners/3 marks the four corners.
gmk_mark_cross/3 marks the center row and column. gmk_mark_checkerboard/3 marks
cells where (R+C) mod 2 = 0. gmk_marked_cells/3 returns R-C pairs of marked
cells. gmk_is_marked/4 checks a single cell. gmk_erase/4 replaces mark color
with a background color.

:- use_module(pack(gridmark/prolog/gridmark)).

14 gmk_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 225.

Chapter 203: gridcrop Pack - Grid Cropping and Padding (gcr_*, Layer 226)

The gridcrop pack (Layer 226) provides fourteen predicates for resizing grids by
cropping, trimming, padding, centering, and expanding. All operations are
non-destructive. gcr_bbox/3 returns the bounding box R0-C0-R1-C1 of all
non-background cells. gcr_crop_bbox/3 and gcr_trim/3 crop the grid to that box.
gcr_trim_rows/3 removes leading and trailing uniform-background rows only;
gcr_trim_cols/3 removes uniform-background columns only. gcr_crop/6 extracts an
explicit rectangle. gcr_pad_to/5 extends the grid to at least a target size.
gcr_center_in/5 places the grid inside a background frame. gcr_add_border/4 and
gcr_remove_border/4 add or remove an N-cell color border. gcr_expand_down/4 and
gcr_expand_right/4 append rows or columns. gcr_content_h/3 and gcr_content_w/3
return the height and width of the non-background bounding box.

:- use_module(pack(gridcrop/prolog/gridcrop)).

14 gcr_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 226.

Chapter 204: gridpatch Pack - Grid Patch Operations (gpt_*, Layer 227)

The gridpatch pack (Layer 227) provides fourteen predicates for working with
sub-grid regions called patches. A patch is itself a grid: a list of rows of
color atoms. gpt_extract/6 copies a rectangular region from a grid. gpt_place/5
pastes a patch at a position, overwriting cells. gpt_overlay/6 pastes with
transparency: patch cells matching a transparency color are skipped. gpt_match_at/4
checks if a patch matches at a specific position. gpt_find_all/3 returns all
positions where a patch matches. gpt_count/3 counts them. gpt_replace_first/4
replaces the first (top-left) match with a new patch. gpt_scatter/4 places a patch
at each of a list of positions. gpt_tile_fill/4 builds an H x W grid by repeating
a tile. gpt_h/2, gpt_w/2, and gpt_size/3 return dimensions. gpt_eq/2 checks
equality. gpt_inpaint/7 fills a rectangular region with a color.

:- use_module(pack(gridpatch/prolog/gridpatch)).

14 gpt_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 227.
