Chapter 205: gridscan Pack - Grid Ray Scanning (gsn_*, Layer 228)

The gridscan pack (Layer 228) provides fourteen predicates for scanning a grid in
four directions (right, left, down, up) to find non-background cells. gsn_scan_row/4
collects all non-background cells in a row as C-V pairs. gsn_scan_col/4 does the
same for a column as R-V pairs. gsn_first_right/5, gsn_first_left/5,
gsn_first_down/5, and gsn_first_up/5 each return the nearest non-background cell
in the named direction, failing if none exists. gsn_dist_right/5, gsn_dist_left/5,
gsn_dist_down/5, and gsn_dist_up/5 return the step count to the nearest
non-background cell, also failing if none. gsn_blocked_right/4, gsn_blocked_left/4,
gsn_blocked_down/4, and gsn_blocked_up/4 succeed if any non-background cell exists
in the named direction from the given position.

:- use_module(pack(gridscan/prolog/gridscan)).

14 gsn_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 228.

Chapter 211: gridwave - Grid Wave Propagation (gwv_*, Layer 229)

The gridwave pack provides fourteen predicates for color wave propagation,
frontier detection, single-color expansion, directional shadow casting,
and interior contraction. Wave expansion models BFS-style multi-color flooding:
each background cell adjacent to exactly one non-background color takes that
color; cells adjacent to two or more different colors remain background (conflict).

:- use_module(pack(gridwave/prolog/gridwave)).

gwv_step/3       - one wave-expansion step (all colors, conflict = stays bg)
gwv_fill/3       - expand to fixed point (multi-color Voronoi with conflict)
gwv_fill_n/4     - exactly N wave-expansion steps
gwv_frontier/3   - bg cells adjacent to any non-bg (R-C list)
gwv_color_frontier/4   - bg cells adjacent to a specific color
gwv_equidistant_front/3 - bg cells adjacent to two or more different colors
gwv_color_expand/4     - expand one color one step
gwv_color_expand_n/5   - expand one color N steps
gwv_shadow_right/4     - cast shadow rightward from non-bg cells
gwv_shadow_left/4      - cast shadow leftward
gwv_shadow_down/4      - cast shadow downward
gwv_shadow_up/4        - cast shadow upward
gwv_contract/3   - remove non-bg cells touching in-bounds bg (interior erosion)
gwv_contract_n/4 - N contraction steps

14 gwv_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 229.

Chapter 212: gridshift - Grid Shifting and Cyclic Rolling (gsh_*, Layer 230)

The gridshift pack provides fourteen predicates for shifting and rolling grids.
Four directional shifts (gsh_shift_right, gsh_shift_left, gsh_shift_down,
gsh_shift_up) move cells with bg fill for vacated positions. Four cyclic rolls
(gsh_roll_right, gsh_roll_left, gsh_roll_down, gsh_roll_up) wrap cells toroidally.
Per-row/column operations (gsh_roll_row, gsh_roll_col, gsh_shift_row, gsh_shift_col)
modify individual rows or columns. gsh_shift_color shifts cells of one color,
and gsh_offset shifts the entire grid by (DR, DC).

:- use_module(pack(gridshift/prolog/gridshift)).

gsh_shift_right/4  - shift each row right by N (bg fill left)
gsh_shift_left/4   - shift each row left by N (bg fill right)
gsh_shift_down/4   - shift grid down by N rows (bg rows at top)
gsh_shift_up/4     - shift grid up by N rows (bg rows at bottom)
gsh_roll_right/3   - cyclic roll each row right (toroidal)
gsh_roll_left/3    - cyclic roll each row left (toroidal)
gsh_roll_down/3    - cyclic roll grid down N rows (toroidal)
gsh_roll_up/3      - cyclic roll grid up N rows (toroidal)
gsh_roll_row/4     - cyclic roll single row right by N
gsh_roll_col/4     - cyclic roll single column down by N
gsh_shift_color/6  - shift cells of specific Color by (DR, DC)
gsh_offset/5       - shift entire grid by (DR, DC) with bg fill
gsh_shift_row/5    - shift single row by N (pos=right, neg=left)
gsh_shift_col/5    - shift single column by N (pos=down, neg=up)

14 gsh_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 230.

Chapter 213: gridmap - Grid Color Mapping (gmp_*, Layer 231)

The gridmap pack provides fourteen predicates for color mapping and substitution
on symbolic grids: applying From-To substitution lists, swapping/replacing/merging
colors, normalizing to integer labels, extracting palettes, masking, palette
inversion and cycling, deriving mappings from aligned grids, and composing maps.

:- use_module(pack(gridmap/prolog/gridmap)).

G = [[r,g,r],[g,b,g],[r,g,r]].
gmp_remap(G, [r-x, g-y], R).
% R = [[x,y,x],[y,b,y],[x,y,x]]

gmp_normalize(G, b, N).
% N = [[1,2,1],[2,b,2],[1,2,1]]

gmp_palette(G, b, P).
% P = [r, g]

gmp_build_map([[r,g],[b,r]], [[x,y],[z,x]], q, M).
% M = [r-x, g-y, b-z]

gmp_remap/3   - apply From-To mapping list; unmapped cells unchanged
gmp_swap/4    - swap two colors (bidirectional remap)
gmp_replace/4 - replace one color with another
gmp_merge/4   - replace list of colors with single target
gmp_normalize/3 - assign integer rank labels in row-major order
gmp_palette/3   - ordered distinct non-bg colors in first-occurrence order
gmp_recolor_fg/4  - replace all non-bg cells with FgColor
gmp_mask_color/4  - keep one color; replace all others with bg
gmp_invert/3    - reverse palette assignments (c_i -> c_(n+1-i))
gmp_cycle/4     - cyclic palette index shift by N
gmp_build_map/4 - derive mapping from two aligned grids
gmp_invert_map/2 - reverse all From-To pairs
gmp_compose_maps/3 - chain two maps (From-Mid, Mid-To -> From-To)
gmp_map_count/3  - count cells with a From key in the mapping

14 gmp_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 231.

Chapter 214: gridrefl - Grid Reflection and Rotation (grf_*, Layer 232)

The gridrefl pack provides fourteen predicates for geometric reflection and
rotation of symbolic grids: horizontal and vertical flips, 90/180/270 degree
rotations, main-diagonal and anti-diagonal transpositions, symmetry detection
on h/v/d/a axes, axis enumeration, and symmetry completion.

:- use_module(pack(gridrefl/prolog/gridrefl)).

G = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]].
grf_rot90(G, R).
% R = [[7,4,1],[8,5,2],[9,6,3]]

grf_rot180(G, R).
% R = [[9,8,7],[6,5,4],[3,2,1]]

grf_transpose(G, T).
% T = [[1,4,7],[2,5,8],[3,6,9]]

grf_sym_axes([[1,2,1],[3,4,3],[1,2,1]], Axes).
% Axes = [h,v]

grf_make_sym_h([[r,b,b],[b,b,g]], b, R).
% R = [[r,b,r],[g,b,g]]

grf_flip_h/2    - reverse each row (left-right mirror)
grf_flip_v/2    - reverse list of rows (top-bottom mirror)
grf_rot90/2     - rotate 90 degrees clockwise
grf_rot180/2    - rotate 180 degrees
grf_rot270/2    - rotate 270 degrees clockwise (90 counter-clockwise)
grf_transpose/2 - swap rows and columns
grf_antidiag/2  - reflect across anti-diagonal (rot180 then transpose)
grf_is_sym_h/1  - succeed if grid equals its horizontal flip
grf_is_sym_v/1  - succeed if grid equals its vertical flip
grf_is_sym_d/1  - succeed if grid equals its own transpose
grf_is_sym_a/1  - succeed if grid equals its own anti-diagonal reflection
grf_sym_axes/2  - return subset of [h,v,d,a] for satisfied symmetry axes
grf_make_sym_h/3 - make horizontally symmetric by merging row with its reverse
grf_make_sym_v/3 - make vertically symmetric by merging row R with row H-1-R

14 grf_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 232.

Chapter 215: gridlogic - Grid Logical Operations (ggl_*, Layer 233)

The gridlogic pack provides fourteen predicates for cell-wise logical and set
operations on symbolic grids. BgColor acts as false; all other colors are true.
Covers pairwise and/or/xor/not/subtract/common/differ, list-wide any/all/majority/
unanimous, and conditional mask/if/filter.

:- use_module(pack(gridlogic/prolog/gridlogic)).

A = [[r,b],[b,b]], B = [[r,b],[r,b]].
ggl_and(A, B, b, R).
% R = [[r,b],[b,b]]  (only (0,0) non-bg in both)

ggl_or([[r,b],[b,b]], [[b,g],[b,b]], b, R).
% R = [[r,g],[b,b]]

ggl_not([[r,b],[b,g]], b, x, R).
% R = [[b,x],[x,b]]

ggl_any([A, B, C], b, R).  % first non-bg across list wins
ggl_majority([G, G, H], b, R).  % 2/3 majority wins

ggl_and/4       - both non-bg → Grid1; else bg
ggl_or/4        - first non-bg wins (Grid1 over Grid2)
ggl_xor/4       - exactly one non-bg → that; else bg
ggl_not/4       - bg → FgColor; non-bg → bg
ggl_subtract/4  - Grid1 non-bg AND Grid2 bg → Grid1; else bg
ggl_common/4    - same non-bg value in both → value; else bg
ggl_differ/4    - both non-bg but different → Grid1; else bg
ggl_any/3       - list-wide OR (first non-bg wins)
ggl_all/3       - list-wide AND (unanimous non-bg agreement)
ggl_majority/3  - plurality vote per cell (>N/2 threshold)
ggl_unanimous/3 - strict all-grids agreement including bg
ggl_mask/5      - keep Grid where Mask = MaskColor; else bg
ggl_if/5        - CondColor → ThenGrid; else ElseGrid
ggl_filter/4    - keep cells in Colors list; else bg

14 ggl_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 233.

Chapter 216: gridchain - Grid Sequence Utilities (gch_*, Layer 234)

The gridchain pack provides fourteen predicates for structural manipulation of
sequences (lists) of grids. Covers consecutive pairing, sliding windows, zip,
take, drop, 0-indexed access, sameness testing, deduplication, repetition,
interleaving, splitting, reversal, pairwise change counts, and first-vs-last
change masks.

:- use_module(pack(gridchain/prolog/gridchain)).

gch_pairs/2       - consecutive two-element pairs: [[G1,G2],[G2,G3],...]
gch_window/3      - sliding windows of W consecutive grids
gch_zip/3         - element-wise zip of two sequences into pairs
gch_take/3        - first N grids (clamps to length)
gch_drop/3        - drop first N grids (clamps to length)
gch_nth/3         - 0-indexed element access
gch_all_same/1    - succeed if all grids identical
gch_dedup/2       - remove duplicate grids, preserve first occurrence
gch_cycle/3       - N copies of a grid
gch_interleave/3  - interleave two sequences element by element
gch_split_at/4    - split at index N into Before and After
gch_reverse/2     - reverse the sequence
gch_diff_counts/3 - per-pair count of differing cell positions
gch_changes_mask/4 - first-vs-last change mask with MarkColor

14 gch_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 234.

Chapter 217: gridblend - Grid Blending and Layered Composition (gbld_*, Layer 235)

The gridblend pack provides ten predicates for compositing grids in layers,
applying spatial blend patterns, and reducing lists of grids to a single composite.
All operations treat one designated BgColor as transparent.

:- use_module(pack(gridblend/prolog/gridblend)).

gbld_overlay/4           - Top over Bottom: non-bg in Top wins; bg reveals Bottom
gbld_underlay/4          - Bottom over Top: non-bg in Bottom wins; bg reveals Top
gbld_stencil/4           - keep Source only at StencilColor positions; else SC
gbld_priority/3          - reduce list: first grid topmost (first non-bg wins)
gbld_checker_blend/5     - blend by checkerboard parity (R+C) mod 2
gbld_stripe_blend/5      - blend by horizontal row stripes of width StripeW
gbld_threshold_replace/5 - replace non-bg cells in rows meeting a count threshold
gbld_merge_many/3        - reduce list: last grid topmost (accumulate from bottom)
gbld_dominant/3          - per-cell plurality vote across grid list
gbld_composite/4         - mode-dispatched composite: overlay | underlay | dominant

10 gbld_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 235.

Chapter 218: gridextract - Grid Object Extraction (gxt_*, Layer 236)

The gridextract pack provides fourteen predicates for collecting cells, computing
bounding boxes, extracting crops, counting regions, and building object registries.

:- use_module(pack(gridextract/prolog/gridextract)).

gxt_nonbg_cells/3    - all non-bg cells as r(R,C,V) triples in row-major order
gxt_color_cells/3    - all cells of a specific color as r(R,C,Color) triples
gxt_bbox/4           - tight bounding box of all non-bg cells + cell list
gxt_crop_bbox/5      - extract sub-grid for a bounding box + offset
gxt_object_at/5      - BFS flood bbox of 4-connected same-color region from (R,C)
gxt_all_colors/3     - sorted unique list of non-bg colors in the grid
gxt_color_count/4    - count occurrences of a specific color
gxt_largest_color/3  - non-bg color with the highest cell count
gxt_smallest_color/3 - non-bg color with the lowest cell count
gxt_centered_crop/5  - crop of given size centered on centroid of non-bg cells
gxt_row_cells/4      - non-bg cells in a specific 0-indexed row
gxt_col_cells/4      - non-bg cells in a specific 0-indexed column
gxt_region_count/4   - count distinct 4-connected regions of a given color
gxt_registry/3       - obj(Color,Count,BBox) for each unique non-bg color

14 gxt_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 236.

Chapter 219: gridgrav - Grid Gravity and Sliding (gra_*, Layer 237)

The gridgrav pack provides fourteen predicates for making non-bg cells fall or
slide toward the edges of their rows and columns, with support for all four
directions, blocked gravity, individual setters, and a settled-state test.

:- use_module(pack(gridgrav/prolog/gridgrav)).

gra_fall_down/3      - non-bg values sink to bottom of each column
gra_fall_up/3        - non-bg values rise to top of each column
gra_fall_left/3      - non-bg values slide to left of each row
gra_fall_right/3     - non-bg values slide to right of each row
gra_fall/4           - direction dispatch: down | up | left | right
gra_col_nonbg/4      - collect non-bg values from a column, top to bottom
gra_row_nonbg/4      - collect non-bg values from a row, left to right
gra_set_col_bottom/5 - place values at bottom of column; bg fills above
gra_set_col_top/5    - place values at top of column; bg fills below
gra_set_row_left/5   - place values at left of row; bg fills to right
gra_set_row_right/5  - place values at right of row; bg fills to left
gra_blocked_fall/5   - gravity with BlockColor as immovable walls
gra_is_settled/2     - true if fall_down leaves the grid unchanged
gra_gravity_score/3  - count of cell positions displaced by fall_down

14 gra_* predicates. 44 tests pass. Layer 237.

Chapter 220: gridstamp - Grid Stamping and Canvas Operations (gst_*, Layer 238)

The gridstamp pack adds fourteen predicates for placing patterns on grids,
finding pattern matches, resizing grids, and creating blank canvases.

Predicates: gst_canvas/4, gst_stamp/6, gst_stamp_all/5, gst_scatter/5,
gst_find_matches/4, gst_stamp_count/4, gst_pad/5, gst_unpad/3,
gst_replicate_h/4, gst_replicate_v/4, gst_border/5, gst_center/5,
gst_extract/6, gst_replace/5.

Key distinction: gst_stamp is transparent (Bg cells in Stamp show through
to Grid); gst_scatter and gst_replace are opaque (every target cell is
overwritten regardless of color).

See docs/tutorial/Ch220_GridStamp.txt for full predicate descriptions,
design notes, example session, and test coverage summary.

Chapter 221: gridalign - Grid Alignment and Shift Matching (gal_*, Layer 239)

The gridalign pack adds fourteen predicates for computing alignment properties
of symbolic grids: center of mass, bounding box, translation, overlap scoring,
IoU, offset search, center alignment, placement, anchor-based placement, and
any-overlap offset enumeration.

Predicates: gal_center_of_mass/3, gal_nonbg_bbox/3, gal_bbox_center/3,
gal_translate/5, gal_overlap_count/4, gal_overlap_score/4, gal_iou/4,
gal_find_offset/5, gal_align_centers/4, gal_align_bbox_centers/4,
gal_nonbg_offset/4, gal_place_at/6, gal_anchor_to/8, gal_match_any_offset/4.

Key design: gal_iou uses \+(V1=Bg, V2=Bg) for the union to avoid double-counting.
gal_find_offset searches cell sets, not full translated grids, for efficiency.
gal_place_at is transparent (same model as gst_stamp).

See docs/tutorial/Ch221_GridAlign.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 222: gridcolorop - Grid Color Operations (gco_*, Layer 240)

The gridcolorop pack adds fourteen predicates for querying and transforming
colors in symbolic grids: counting, ranking, swapping, replacing, masking,
cycling, palette application, and binary inversion.

Predicates: gco_color_counts/3, gco_distinct_colors/3, gco_nth_color/4,
gco_count_distinct/3, gco_swap/5, gco_replace/5, gco_apply_map/4,
gco_keep_only/4, gco_remove_color/4, gco_cycle/5, gco_rank_grid/3,
gco_apply_palette/4, gco_most_least/4, gco_invert/4.

Key design: gco_sort_by_count_desc_ uses Neg is -N (arithmetic integer) not
the term -N (compound -(N)) to ensure msort produces descending count order.
gco_cycle uses ((Idx+N) mod PLen + PLen) mod PLen to handle negative N.
gco_invert is binary: all non-bg becomes Bg, all Bg becomes FgColor.

See docs/tutorial/Ch222_GridColorOp.txt for full predicate descriptions,
design notes, example session, and test coverage summary.

Chapter 223: gridresize - Grid Resize and Scale Operations (grs_*, Layer 241)

The gridresize pack adds fourteen predicates for scaling, resizing, tiling,
cropping, and fitting symbolic grids: integer scale-up/down, mode downsample,
double and halve shortcuts, nearest-neighbor resize, modular tiling, rectangular
crop, border crop, letterbox fit, canvas centering, dimension query, aspect ratio,
and square test.

Predicates: grs_scale_up/3, grs_scale_down/4, grs_scale_down_mode/4,
grs_double/2, grs_halve/3, grs_resize/5, grs_tile_to/4, grs_crop/6,
grs_border_crop/4, grs_fit_in/5, grs_embed_in/5, grs_grid_size/3,
grs_aspect_ratio/4, grs_is_square/1.

Key design: all if-then-else blocks following grs_dims_ must be wrapped in
outer parentheses; without them SWI-Prolog parses grs_dims_ as part of the
condition and H, W are unbound in the else branch. grs_mode_ uses arithmetic
negation (Neg is -N) for sort keys. grs_fit_in uses round/1 for pixel rounding.

See docs/tutorial/Ch223_GridResize.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 224: gridobj - Grid Object Operations (gob_*, Layer 242)

The gridobj pack adds fourteen predicates for 4-connected component analysis
and manipulation: BFS cell enumeration, color/size/bbox query, binary mask,
bounding-box extraction, full object inventory, count, largest/smallest
selection, flood fill, enclosed-region fill, object removal, and movement.

Predicates: gob_object_cells/4, gob_object_color/4, gob_object_size/4,
gob_object_bbox/4, gob_object_mask/5, gob_extract_object/5, gob_all_objects/3,
gob_object_count/3, gob_largest_object/3, gob_smallest_object/3,
gob_flood_fill/5, gob_fill_enclosed/4, gob_remove_object/5, gob_move_object/7.

Key design: gob_bfs_ checks both Visited and the frontier tail (Rest) to prevent
double-enqueueing. gob_fill_enclosed BFS-floods from all border Bg cells;
unreachable Bg cells are enclosed. gob_move_object clips out-of-bounds cells.

See docs/tutorial/Ch224_GridObj.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 225: gridgroupby - Grid Group-By Operations (ggb_*, Layer 243)

The gridgroupby pack adds fourteen predicates for grouping, filtering, sorting,
pairing, and counting ob(Color,Cells,BBox) object terms produced by gob_all_objects.

Predicates: ggb_group_by_color/2, ggb_group_by_size/2, ggb_group_by_row/2,
ggb_group_by_col/2, ggb_filter_by_color/3, ggb_filter_by_size/3,
ggb_filter_larger/3, ggb_filter_smaller/3, ggb_sort_by_size_asc/2,
ggb_sort_by_size_desc/2, ggb_sort_by_row/2, ggb_sort_by_col/2,
ggb_pair_by_color/2, ggb_count_per_color/2.

Key design: all group-by predicates share ggb_group_by_key_/3 which collects
k(Key,Obj) pairs, msort-s by key, then collects same-key runs into grp terms.
Descending sort uses neg(Neg,Obj) keys (Neg is -Size) so msort produces largest
first. ggb_pair_by_color emits pair/3 only for colors with exactly two objects.

See docs/tutorial/Ch225_GridGroupBy.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 226: gridrelation - Grid Object Spatial Relations (grl_*, Layer 244)

The gridrelation pack adds fourteen predicates for computing spatial relations
between ob(Color,Cells,BBox) object terms from gob_all_objects.

Predicates: grl_touching/2, grl_adjacent/3, grl_min_distance/3, grl_above/2,
grl_below/2, grl_left_of/2, grl_right_of/2, grl_bbox_contains/2,
grl_bbox_overlap/2, grl_cells_overlap/2, grl_same_rows/2, grl_same_cols/2,
grl_direction/3, grl_all_relations/3.

Key design: grl_touching cuts after first match. grl_direction uses doubled
centroid (R0+R1, C0+C1) for exact integer arithmetic; tie favors vertical axis.
grl_bbox_overlap uses standard interval test; adjacent single-cell bboxes do not
overlap. grl_all_relations uses a findall over a 10-element Rel-Goal association.

See docs/tutorial/Ch226_GridRelation.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 227: gridtransform - Grid Transformation Detection and Application (gtr_*, Layer 245)

The gridtransform pack adds fourteen predicates for detecting color mappings between
two grids, computing differences, and applying transformations.

Predicates: gtr_color_map/3, gtr_apply_color_map/3, gtr_diff_cells/3,
gtr_diff_count/3, gtr_same_cells/3, gtr_changed_colors/3, gtr_invert_map/2,
gtr_compose_maps/3, gtr_is_identity_map/1, gtr_apply_changes/3, gtr_delta_grid/4,
gtr_overlay/4, gtr_common_grid/4, gtr_is_color_permutation/2.

Key design: color maps are cm(Old,New) sorted lists. gtr_color_map uses sort+
consistency check. gtr_delta_grid and gtr_overlay compose: overlay(A,delta(A,B,Bg),Bg)=B.
gtr_is_color_permutation requires both consistency (no ambiguous mapping) and
injectivity (no two sources map to same target).

See docs/tutorial/Ch227_GridTransform.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 228: gridobjmatch - Object Matching and Change Detection (gom_*, Layer 246)

The gridobjmatch pack (Work Package 267, Layer 246) provides fourteen predicates for
matching ob(Color,Cells,BBox) object terms between two lists and detecting what
changed from one list to the other. Objects are produced by gob_all_objects/3 from
the gridobj pack.

Predicates: gom_match_color/3, gom_match_nearest/3, gom_match_size/3,
gom_unmatched_a/3, gom_unmatched_b/3, gom_color_diff/3, gom_move_vector/2,
gom_move_vectors/2, gom_constant_move/2, gom_infer_color_map/2, gom_appeared/3,
gom_disappeared/3, gom_same_structure/2, gom_count_change/3.

Key design: match(O1,O2) pairing terms; mv(DR,DC) movement vectors; cm(Old,New)
color maps. gom_match_nearest uses doubled centroid (R0+R1, C0+C1) for exact
integer arithmetic. gom_constant_move fails for empty list. gom_appeared and
gom_disappeared operate on color classes across the two lists.

See docs/tutorial/Ch228_GridObjMatch.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 229: symtab - Symbol Table Learning from Input-Output Pairs (st_*, Layer 247)

The symtab pack (Work Package 268, Layer 247) provides fourteen predicates for
learning symbol-to-value mappings from training pairs. A symbol table is a list
of sym(Feature, Value) terms that encode what each observable feature of an input
object means in the output.

Predicates: st_build_table/2, st_identify_symbols/2, st_contrastive_learn/2,
st_apply_table/3, st_hole_count/2, st_lookup/3, st_entry_consistent/2,
st_color_feature/2, st_size_feature/2, st_position_feature/3, st_is_symbol/2,
st_candidate_symbols/2, st_score_table/3, st_best_table/3.

Key design: symbol tables are sym(Feature,Value) lists. Features are color(C),
size(N), holes(N), or pos(Q). st_contrastive_learn intersects candidate sym
entries across all training pairs, keeping only entries consistent with every pair.
st_hole_count uses bbox-area minus cell-count approximation. st_best_table
selects the highest-scoring candidate table.

See docs/tutorial/Ch229_SymTab.txt for full details and examples.

Chapter 230: invariant - Cross-Pair Invariant Extraction (iv_*, Layer 248)

The invariant pack (Work Package 269, Layer 248) provides fourteen predicates for
finding what stays constant across all training pairs. Invariants reduce the rule
search space by eliminating entire rule classes before any search begins.

Predicates: iv_grid_invariants/2, iv_output_invariants/2, iv_object_invariants/2,
iv_variant_features/2, iv_consistent_delta/2, iv_all_grids/2, iv_no_grids/2,
iv_color_set/3, iv_same_color_sets/2, iv_same_dims/1, iv_preserves_dims/1,
iv_preserves_colors/1, iv_preserves_count/2, iv_stable_color_map/2.

Key design: invariants are inv(Property) terms; variant features are feature(Property)
terms. Training pairs are pair(InputGrid, OutputGrid). iv_stable_color_map intersects
per-pair cell change maps to find consistent color mappings. iv_all_grids/iv_no_grids
are meta_predicate declarations for correct module-qualified dispatch.

See docs/tutorial/Ch230_Invariant.txt for full details and examples.


Chapter 231: Contrastive Pair Analysis - The contrast Pack (ca_*, Layer 249)

The contrast pack (Work Package 270, Layer 249) provides fourteen predicates for
contrastive pair analysis. It finds which input features co-vary with output changes
across all training pairs - the key causal signal for identifying transformation rules.

Predicates: ca_pairwise_delta/2, ca_covarying_features/2, ca_context_gate/2,
ca_discriminating_pair/4, ca_correlated_features/3, ca_change_count/3,
ca_common_context/3, ca_separates/4, ca_minimal_features/2, ca_feature_profile/2,
ca_profile_diff/3, ca_stable_features/2, ca_unstable_features/2, ca_rank_features/2.

Key design: deltas are delta(I, Changes) terms where Changes is a list of
chg(R,C,OldV,NewV). Features are feat(Name, Value) terms. ca_covarying_features
returns features present in ALL changing pairs but absent from stable pairs.
ca_rank_features scores features by change_count / total_changing and returns
them in descending correlation order. ca_context_gate returns the top-ranked feature.

See docs/tutorial/Ch231_Contrast.txt for full details and examples.


Chapter 232: Legend and Key Region Detection - The legend Pack (lg_*, Layer 250)

The legend pack (Work Package 271, Layer 250) provides fourteen predicates for
detecting and parsing "legend" or "key" regions in grid tasks. A legend region is
a small, isolated part of the grid that acts as a code key.

Predicates: lg_detect_legend/3, lg_is_legend/2, lg_legend_entries/2,
lg_consistent_legend/2, lg_region_bbox/3, lg_separated/2, lg_region_area/2,
lg_all_regions/3, lg_region_color/3, lg_is_small/3, lg_same_shape/2,
lg_entry_boundaries/2, lg_color_map/3, lg_position/3.

Key design: regions are lists of r(Row,Col) cells. BFS 4-connected flood-fill.
Legend detection requires area <= TotalArea/4 and not touching other regions.
lg_color_map/3 takes the source grid to look up actual cell colors by row band.

See docs/tutorial/Ch232_Legend.txt for full details and examples.


Chapter 233: Multi-Pair Object Tracking - The multipair Pack (mp_*, Layer 251)

The multipair pack (Work Package 272, Layer 251) provides fourteen predicates for
tracking object identity and correspondence across multiple training pairs. Objects
are identified by color (bg=0). Training pairs are pair(InputGrid, OutputGrid) terms.

Predicates: mp_track_objects/2, mp_invariant_objects/2, mp_role_objects/3,
mp_cross_pair_match/2, mp_all_input_objects/2, mp_color_frequency/3,
mp_universal_colors/2, mp_variable_colors/2, mp_disappeared_objects/2,
mp_appeared_objects/2, mp_stable_color_objects/2, mp_modal_object_count/2,
mp_consistent_count/1, mp_singleton_color/2.

Key design: tracked(Idx, Color, PairIdxList) identity terms; po(PairIdx, Color)
input objects; match(I, J, CommonColors) correspondence terms.
mp_universal_colors and mp_variable_colors are guaranteed disjoint.

See docs/tutorial/Ch233_MultiPair.txt for full details and examples.


Chapter 234: Task Type Classification - The taskcat Pack (tc_*, Layer 252)

The taskcat pack (Work Package 273, Layer 252) provides fourteen predicates for
classifying grid transformation tasks by type and selecting solving strategies.
The four task categories are: single_rule, multi_step, context_gated, symbol_table.

Predicates: tc_categorize/2, tc_is_single_rule/1, tc_is_multi_step/1,
tc_has_context_gate/1, tc_has_symbol_table/1, tc_suggest_strategies/2,
tc_preserves_dims/1, tc_preserves_colors/1, tc_consistent_change_count/1,
tc_max_change_count/2, tc_distinct_change_patterns/2, tc_is_fill_task/1,
tc_is_deletion_task/1, tc_confidence/2.

Key design: Chain of Responsibility ordering in tc_categorize (simplest wins).
tc_suggest_strategies returns ordered fallback strategy lists per category.
tc_confidence provides a 0.0-1.0 score using dims, count, and pattern uniformity.

See docs/tutorial/Ch234_TaskCat.txt for full details and examples.


Chapter 235: seqinfer Enhancement - ARC-AGI-2 Candidates (WP-274)

sq_arc2_candidates/1 returns a 66-entry list of integer-color scene-level rule
candidates for use with sq_infer_2step and sq_infer_3step on harder tasks.

    sq_arc2_candidates(Rules),
    sq_infer_2step(Rules, TrainingPairs, BestSeq).

Uses integer colors 0-9 (standard ARC encoding). Extends sq_default_rules with
more recolor pairs (colors 1-6), larger shift deltas, and top_n operators.


Chapter 236: hyp Enhancement - Spatial, Structural, Sequence Hypotheses (WP-275)

Three new hypothesis types extend the hyp pack beyond color substitutions:

hy_spatial_hyp(+Pairs, +Moves, -Hyp): find the first DR-DC shift that explains all pairs.
    hy_spatial_hyp(Pairs, [0-1, 1-0, 0-(-1), (-1)-0], shift(DR, DC)).

hy_structural_hyp(+Pairs, +Patterns, -Hyp): find the first structural pattern consistent
with all pairs.
    hy_structural_hyp(Pairs, [dims_preserved, colors_preserved], structural(Pat)).

hy_sequence_hyp(+Pairs, +Maps1, +Maps2, -Hyp): find a two-step color-map hypothesis.
    hy_sequence_hyp(Pairs, [[1-2]], [[2-3]], seq([1-2], [2-3])).


Chapter 237: condxf Enhancement - Context Gate Inference (WP-276)

xc_infer_gate/3 infers which scene-object color acts as a context gate:

    xc_infer_gate(Pairs, [], gate_color(GateColor)).

For each sorted candidate color, the predicate partitions pairs into those that
contain the color in their input scene vs those that do not, then checks whether
the two groups produce different change signatures (lost/gained color sets).
Returns gate_color(C) for the first color that successfully separates the groups.


Chapter 238: induction Enhancement - Cross-Pair Property Aggregation (WP-277)

Two new predicates aggregate grid-pair properties across a list of training pairs:

id_cross_pair_invariants(+Pairs, -Invariants):
    Returns properties that hold for every training pair (e.g. dims_preserved).
    id_cross_pair_invariants(Pairs, Inv), member(dims_preserved, Inv).

id_cross_pair_variants(+Pairs, -Variants):
    Returns properties that hold for SOME but not ALL pairs.
    id_cross_pair_variants(Pairs, Var), member(colors_preserved, Var).

Properties tested: dims_preserved, colors_preserved, total_nonzero_preserved,
monotone_input, monotone_output, bg_preserved, scale_preserved.


Chapter 239: Periodic Pattern Repair - The periodfix Pack (ppf_*, Layer 253)

Author: D. R. Dison, Founder of AIU, Creator and Owner of PrologAI and Mentova, ORCID 0009-0001-9246-5758, linkedin.com/in/d-r-dison/

The periodfix pack (Work Package 278, Layer 253) provides fourteen predicates for detecting
majority-vote periodic tiles, finding violations in periodic patterns, and repairing
corrupted cells in both 1D lists and 2D grids.

A list has period P if List[i] = List[i mod P] for all i. A majority-vote tile is built
by taking the modal value at each phase index. A violation is a position where the value
differs from the tile at that phase.

Core predicates:

    ppf_list_period(+List, -P)           % smallest exact period with no violations
    ppf_majority(+List, -Mode)           % most common element (mode)
    ppf_tile_from_list(+List, +P, -Tile) % majority-vote tile of length P
    ppf_violations_list(+L, +P, +Tile, -Violations) % viol(Idx,Actual,Expected) list
    ppf_repair_list(+L, +P, +Tile, -Repaired)        % replace all violations
    ppf_best_period_list(+L, -P, -NViol) % period 1..N//2 minimizing violations
    ppf_tile_2d(+Grid, +PH, +PW, -Tile)  % PH-by-PW majority-vote tile for a grid
    ppf_violations_2d(+Grid, +Tile, +PH, +PW, -Violations) % 2D violations
    ppf_repair_grid(+Grid, +Tile, +PH, +PW, -Repaired)     % replace 2D violations
    ppf_best_periods(+Grid, -PH, -PW)    % (PH,PW) minimizing total 2D violations

Single-violation repair (for exactly one corrupted cell):

    ppf_single_violation_list(+L, +P, +Tile, -Violation) % true iff exactly 1 mismatch
    ppf_repair_single_list(+L, +P, +Tile, -Repaired)     % repair the unique mismatch
    ppf_single_violation_2d(+Grid, +Tile, +PH, +PW, -V)  % true iff exactly 1 2D mismatch
    ppf_repair_single_grid(+Grid, +Tile, +PH, +PW, -Rep) % repair the unique 2D mismatch

Example:

    :- use_module(library(periodfix)).

    ?- ppf_best_period_list([1,2,1,2,1,3], P, NViol).
    P = 2, NViol = 1.

    ?- ppf_tile_from_list([1,2,1,2,1,3], 2, Tile).
    Tile = [1,2].

    ?- ppf_repair_list([1,2,1,2,1,3], 2, [1,2], Repaired).
    Repaired = [1,2,1,2,1,2].

See docs/tutorial/Ch239_Periodfix.txt for full details and examples.


Chapter 240: I/O Channel Integration - The iochan Pack (ic_*, Layer 254)

Author: D. R. Dison, Founder of AIU, Creator and Owner of PrologAI and Mentova, ORCID 0009-0001-9246-5758, linkedin.com/in/d-r-dison/

The iochan pack (Work Package 279, Layer 254) provides fourteen predicates for dispatching
information to and from concrete I/O channels. PrologAI can speak aloud, listen to a
microphone, send and receive text and images through multiple channels, and print output
to a physical printer.

CHANNEL TYPES AND CONFIGURATIONS

A channel is a named configuration registered in the runtime registry. Channel types:
  text_out, text_in, image_out, image_in, audio_out, audio_in, printer

Configuration terms for each type:
  console_config                  - system stdout (text) or stdin (text)
  email_config(Addr, Subject)     - SMTP email via sendmail or mutt
  screen_config(WinId)            - X11 window via xdotool
  app_config(AppId, Endpoint)     - HTTP POST/GET to an application endpoint
  speaker_config(DevId)           - espeak-ng TTS; '' = default device
  mic_config(DevId)               - arecord + whisper-cli; '' = default device
  file_config(Path)               - local file path (image input)
  webcam_config(DevId)            - fswebcam camera capture
  url_config(URL)                 - wget URL download (image input)
  printer_config(Name, Opts)      - CUPS lp; '' = default printer

REGISTERING CHANNELS

    :- use_module(library(iochan)).

    % Register a custom email channel.
    ?- ic_register_channel(text_out, email_config('user@example.com', 'Alert'), ChanId).
    ChanId = chan_6.

    % Check its status.
    ?- ic_channel_status(chan_6, Status).
    Status = ready.

    % List all text-output channels.
    ?- ic_list_channels(text_out, Channels).
    Channels = [chan(chan_1, console_config), chan(chan_6, email_config(...))].

TEXT OUTPUT

    % Write to the console (pre-registered as chan_1).
    ?- ic_text_out(console_config, 'Hello from PrologAI!', Status).
    Hello from PrologAI!
    Status = ok.

    % Write to an email channel.
    ?- ic_text_out(chan_6, 'Alert: reasoning complete.', Status).
    Status = ok.    % (if sendmail is installed)

TEXT INPUT

    % Read a line from the console.
    ?- ic_text_in(console_config, Text).
    |: Hello
    Text = "Hello".

SPEAKER OUTPUT

    % Speak text aloud using espeak-ng (festival as fallback).
    ?- ic_speak('PrologAI is now speaking.', Status).
    Status = ok.    % (if espeak-ng or festival is installed)

    % Speak to a named speaker channel.
    ?- ic_register_channel(audio_out, speaker_config('hw:0,0'), SpkId),
       ic_speak_to(SpkId, 'Channel test.', Status).

MICROPHONE INPUT

    % Record 5 seconds from the default mic and transcribe.
    ?- ic_listen(5, Transcript).
    Transcript = "what I just said".    % (if arecord + whisper-cli installed)

IMAGE OUTPUT

    % Display an image in the terminal (requires img2txt).
    ?- ic_image_out(console_config, '/path/to/image.png', Status).

    % Email an image attachment.
    ?- ic_image_out(email_config('user@example.com', 'Photo'), '/path/to/photo.jpg', S).

IMAGE INPUT

    % Capture from a webcam.
    ?- ic_register_channel(image_in, webcam_config('/dev/video0'), CamId),
       ic_image_in(CamId, ImagePath).

PRINTER OUTPUT

    % Print text to the default CUPS printer.
    ?- ic_register_channel(printer, printer_config('', []), PrintId),
       ic_print_text(PrintId, 'PrologAI report.', Status).

    % Print an image.
    ?- ic_print_image(PrintId, '/path/to/diagram.png', Status).

All hardware-dependent predicates degrade gracefully: if the required external program
(espeak-ng, festival, arecord, whisper-cli, sendmail, mutt, xdotool, img2txt,
display, fswebcam, wget, lp) is absent, Status = error(program_unavailable) is
returned rather than an exception being thrown.

See docs/tutorial/Ch240_Iochan.txt for full details and exercises.


Chapter 241: Wave 23 - period_extend (WP-281, Layer 256)

Wave 23 adds the period_extend named rule to arc_benchmark_2.pl, solving
ARC-AGI-2 task 16de56c4. This rule detects periodic patterns in rows or
columns of a grid and extends them to fill the line.

The core idea is simple: if a row contains two 1s separated by 3 columns,
the rule fills every third column of that row with 1s. If a different color
appears at one of those pattern positions, it acts as a stop-and-recolor
marker: the pattern fills from the anchor end to the stop marker using the
marker's color, and does not extend past the stop.

Direction (row-wise vs column-wise) is chosen automatically: whichever
dimension has more "active lines" (lines with two or more cells of the same
non-background color) wins.

Example (row mode):

    Input row:  [9, 0, 9, 0, 0, 0, 6, 0, 0]
                    ^ 9s at cols 0,2  step=2  stop: 6 at col 6
    Output row: [6, 0, 6, 0, 6, 0, 6, 0, 0]
                    ^ fills cols 0,2,4,6 with 6 (the stop/recolor color)

Key predicates (all arc2_pe_* namespace):

    arc2_pe_count_active_/5  count active rows and cols
    arc2_pe_find_anchor_/4   find the anchor pair and step
    arc2_pe_pat_pos_/4       generate all in-bounds pattern positions
    arc2_pe_out_pairs_/5     compute output cells with stop/recolor logic

The period_extend rule is registered via arc2_named_rule(period_extend) and
dispatched by the standard arc2_transform/3 interface used by all waves.

Task solved: 16de56c4. Benchmark score: 23/120 = 19.17%.


Chapter 242: Wave 24 - shape_sort (WP-282, Layer 257)

Wave 24 adds the shape_sort named rule, solving ARC-AGI-2 task 2ba387bc.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

The input grid contains several 4x4 colored blocks on a zero background.
Every block is one of two types:

- HOLLOW: a one-cell-wide outer ring of a single color surrounds a 2x2
  interior of zeros. The 4x4 bounding box contains at least one 0.

- SOLID: all 16 cells are the same color. The 4x4 bounding box has no 0.

The rule reads every block, notes its top-left corner, and sorts all blocks
by reading order (row first, column second). It then splits the sorted list
into a HOLLOW list and a SOLID list while preserving reading order within
each. It pairs position-i hollow with position-i solid, placing the hollow
on the left half and the solid on the right half of 4 output rows. If one
list is shorter, a 4x4 block of zeros fills the missing side. All pairs are
stacked vertically to form the output grid.

EXAMPLE

Input pair 1 (23 x 24): 7 blocks scattered on a zero canvas.

Sorted by reading order and classified:
  pos 1: color=1 (1,2)   SOLID
  pos 2: color=2 (4,9)   SOLID
  pos 3: color=8 (6,17)  HOLLOW
  pos 4: color=7 (12,1)  SOLID
  pos 5: color=3 (13,8)  HOLLOW
  pos 6: color=6 (15,17) SOLID
  pos 7: color=4 (18,3)  HOLLOW

Hollow list: [8, 3, 4]   Solid list: [1, 2, 7, 6]

Output (16 x 8), four pairs stacked:
  pair 1: hollow=8 | solid=1
  pair 2: hollow=3 | solid=2
  pair 3: hollow=4 | solid=7
  pair 4: hollow=zero  | solid=6

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_ss_shapes_/2
      Collect all non-BG colors; for each find min row and min col; extract
      4x4 sub; sort all by R0-C0 reading order via msort.

  arc2_ss_hollow_/1
      Succeed if the 4x4 subgrid contains any 0 (used with include/exclude).

  arc2_ss_zip_/3
      Zip hollow and solid lists into pairs; pad the shorter with zero blocks.

  arc2_ss_pair_rows_/2
      Concatenate left 4-wide rows with right 4-wide rows to make 8-wide rows.

Task solved: 2ba387bc. Benchmark score: 24/120 = 20.00%.

Chapter 243: Wave 25 - bar_extend (WP-283, Layer 258)

Wave 25 adds the bar_extend named rule, solving ARC-AGI-2 task 1ae2feb7.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

A vertical divider column separates the grid into a left bar region and a
right empty region. Each row with a bar carries 1 or 2 non-background
colors. The rightmost non-background cell is the primary color P with count
CP. Any other non-background color is secondary S with count CS (default 1).

Period = CP times CS.

Within one period:
- P at every multiple of CP.
- S at every multiple of CS not already P.
- Background (0) everywhere else.

The right side is filled by repeating this period cyclically.

EXAMPLE

Bar [3,3,3,4,4]: P=4 (count 2), S=3 (count 3). Period=6.
One period [4,0,4,3,4,0]: 4 at 0,2,4 (multiples of 2); 3 at 3 (multiple
of 3, not already 4); BG at 1,5.
Right side: [4,0,4,3,4,0,4,0,4,3,...].

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_be_divider_(Grid, D)
      Finds the unique column D where all non-BG values are the same.

  arc2_be_primary_(Bar, PC, CP)
      Finds the rightmost non-BG cell (primary color) and counts it.

  arc2_be_secondary_(Bar, PC, SC, CS)
      Finds any other non-BG color (secondary); defaults CS=1 if absent.

  arc2_be_cell_(PC, CP, SC, CS, Idx, Val)
      Computes the cell value at period position Idx.

Task solved: 1ae2feb7. Benchmark score: 24/120 = 20.00% -> 25/120 = 20.83%.

Chapter 244: Wave 26 - straighten_diag (WP-284, Layer 259)

Wave 26 adds the straighten_diag named rule, solving ARC-AGI-2 task 7b80bb43.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

A two-color grid (background BG and one line color LV) contains an orthogonal
tree of line segments with diagonal shortcuts: non-BG cells that have no
4-connected non-BG neighbor but do have a diagonal non-BG neighbor. These
diagonal-only cells corrupt the orthogonal structure. The rule removes them
and bridges the gaps they span.

Phase 1 - Gap fill:
Find 8-connected components of diagonal cells. For each component, identify
anchor cells: non-diagonal non-BG cells 8-adjacent to the component. For each
anchor, find the farthest diagonal tip (max Manhattan distance). Determine the
anchor's axis (horizontal if it has a 4-connected non-diagonal non-BG neighbor
in the same row; vertical if in the same column). Scan from the anchor toward
the tip along the anchor's axis: collect BG cells (gap) until a non-BG
non-diagonal terminus is found. If found, the gap cells are filled with LV.

Phase 2 - Diagonal removal:
Remove all diagonal cells (set to BG). Apply all gap fills.

Phase 3 - Stub pruning:
Track DiagNbr: non-diagonal non-BG cells that were 8-adjacent to any diagonal.
Iteratively: find a DiagNbr cell with exactly one 4-connected non-BG neighbor
(its anchor) whose direction to the anchor is perpendicular to the anchor's
line axis. Remove it. Repeat until no such stub exists.

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_sd_find_diag_(Grid, BG, Diag)
      Finds all diagonal-only cells: non-BG, no 4-conn non-BG, has 8-conn
      (diagonal) non-BG neighbor.

  arc2_sd_diag_nbr_(Grid, BG, Diag, DiagNbr)
      Finds non-diagonal non-BG cells 8-adjacent to any diagonal cell.

  arc2_sd_comps8_(Cells, Comps)
      Partitions Cells into 8-connected components using DFS flood-fill.

  arc2_sd_anchor_fill_(Grid, BG, Diag, Comp, NRows, NCols, AR-AC, Acc, Acc1)
      For one anchor, computes fill cells in H/V direction toward farthest tip.

  arc2_sd_scan_(Grid, BG, Diag, AR, AC, FDR, FDC, NRows, NCols, Gap)
      Scans from anchor in direction (FDR,FDC), skipping diagonal cells,
      collecting BG cells until a non-BG non-diagonal terminus is found.

  arc2_sd_prune_loop_(Grid, BG, DiagNbr, Out)
      Iteratively removes perpendicular degree-1 stubs from DiagNbr.

  arc2_sd_perp_check_(ANeighbors, AR, AC, DDR, _DDC)
      True when stub direction is perpendicular to anchor's line axis.

Task solved: 7b80bb43. Benchmark score: 25/120 = 20.83% -> 26/120 = 21.67%.

Chapter 245: Wave 27 - stream_extend (WP-285, Layer 260)

Wave 27 adds the stream_extend named rule, solving ARC-AGI-2 task 53fb4810.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

The grid has BG = 0 and A-marker cells of value 1 forming 4-connected blobs.
Adjacent to each blob, in each cardinal direction, a seed chain of colored
non-BG non-marker cells may appear. If the chain is followed by a BG gap
before the grid edge, the chain's period-P pattern is tiled outward to the
boundary. Extension formula: fill value at distance D from chain anchor =
Chain[D mod P]. Parallel chains in the same direction extend independently.

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_sx_comps4_(ACells, Comps)
      Partitions A-marker cells into 4-connected components via flood-fill.

  arc2_sx_try_dir_(Dir, BG, AVal, NRows, NCols, Comp, G0, G1)
      Finds seed chains in direction Dir; applies extension fills via foldl.

  arc2_sx_collect_(Grid, BG, AVal, NRows, NCols, R, C, Dir, Acc, Chain)
      Walks from (R,C) in Dir. Returns chain when BG is found (gap exists).
      Fails when grid boundary is reached or another marker is hit.

  arc2_sx_ext_fill_(Chain, Dir, NRows, NCols, R-C-Val)
      Generates each fill cell in the extension region. Computes
      Offset = distance from anchor; Val = Chain[Offset mod P].

  arc2_sx_step_(Dir, DR, DC)
      Maps direction to row/column delta.

Task solved: 53fb4810. Benchmark score: 26/120 = 21.67% -> 27/120 = 22.50%.

Chapter 246: Wave 28 - slide_open (WP-286, Layer 261)

Wave 28 adds the slide_open named rule to arc_benchmark_2.pl, solving
ARC-AGI-2 task 6e453dd6. This rule recognizes that 0-cell objects left of a
vertical 5-divider should be slid rightward to touch the divider, and that
rows left open by the slide should be filled right of the divider with 2.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

The grid contains a vertical column where every cell holds value 5 (the
divider at column DC). All 0-cells strictly left of DC form one or more
4-connected components (the objects). Each object is shifted rightward so
its rightmost cell occupies column DC-1, placing it immediately against the
divider. After all objects have been slid, the rule scans each row. If a row
satisfies all three open-row conditions:
  (1) cell(R, DC-1) = 0  (a gap exists at the divider edge in this row),
  (2) cell(R, DC-2) != 0 (the gap is isolated; the slid object is not here),
  (3) at least one 0 exists in columns 0..DC-3 (another object is present),
then all cells right of the divider (columns DC+1 to NCols-1) are filled
with value 2 in that row.

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_so_divider_(Grid, NRows, DC)
      Finds the column DC where all NRows cells hold value 5.
      Uses between/3 to generate candidate columns and forall to verify.
      Critical ordering: NRowsM is computed before numlist/3 is called,
      because numlist/3 requires an instantiated upper bound.

  arc2_so_comps4_(Cells, Comps)
      Partitions a flat list of R-C pairs into 4-connected components
      using iterative DFS flood-fill (arc2_so_flood4_/6).

  arc2_so_slide_(DC, _NRows, _NCols, Comp, G0, G1)
      Slides one component rightward. Two foldl passes:
      Pass 1 - erase all component cells (set to placeholder 6).
      Pass 2 - place each cell at C + Shift, where Shift = (DC-1) - MaxCol.
      The two-pass design prevents a shifted component from overwriting
      its own source cells in a single pass.

  arc2_so_fill_open_(Grid, NRows, NCols, DC, Out)
      Fills open rows with 2 right of the divider. Iterates over all rows
      via foldl, checking all three open-row conditions per row.
      Open rows receive value 2 in columns DC+1 through NCols-1.

ALGORITHM TRACE (task 6e453dd6)

The input grid has a vertical column of 5s at column DC. Several 0-cell
objects appear left of the divider, scattered across rows. Each object is a
4-connected component.

Slide phase: each component is identified. Its rightmost column MaxCol is
found. Shift = DC-1 - MaxCol. The component is first erased (set to 6), then
re-placed at the shifted positions (0s). After all slides, every component
touches the divider from the left.

Fill phase: the rule scans each row. Rows where the slid object placed its
rightmost cell at DC-1 show a 0 at DC-1 and a non-zero at DC-2 (the object
body). If another object (0) exists further left (cols 0..DC-3), the row is
open, and all cells right of the divider are set to 2.

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: 6e453dd6.
Score: 27/120 = 22.50% to 28/120 = 23.33%.

Chapter 247: Wave 29 - diag_beam (WP-287, Layer 262)

Wave 29 adds the diag_beam named rule to arc_benchmark_2.pl, solving
ARC-AGI-2 task db695cfb. This rule recognizes that 1-cells placed exactly
45 degrees apart from each other define diagonal beams, and that 6-cells
on a beam path act as reflectors that send perpendicular 6-trails outward.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

The grid contains only three types of cells: background, 1-cells, and
6-cells. Two 1-cells are diagonally aligned when |row_diff| = |col_diff| > 0.
Each aligned pair defines a 45-degree beam. The beam fills intermediate
background cells with 1. When the beam hits a 6-cell, it leaves the 6
unchanged and fires two perpendicular 45-degree rays from that point. Each
ray fills background cells with 6 until it hits the grid boundary or
another existing 6-cell.

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(diag_beam, Grid, Out)
      Top-level guard: reads background from Grid[0][0], verifies all
      non-background cells are 1 or 6, collects Ones and Sixes, finds
      all diagonal 1-pairs, and applies each beam via foldl.

  arc2_db_march_(R, C, DR, DC, R2, C2, Sixes, NRows, NCols, G0, G1)
      Base clause stops at the destination (R2, C2). Recursive clause
      checks memberchk(R-C, Sixes): if true, bounces; otherwise fills
      the cell with 1 and steps to (R+DR, C+DC).

  arc2_db_bounce_(R, C, DR, DC, Sixes, NRows, NCols, G0, G1)
      Fires two perpendicular rays: directions (DR, -DC) and (-DR, DC).

  arc2_db_ray_(R, C, DR, DC, Sixes, NRows, NCols, G0, G1)
      Extends the ray one step at a time. Stops at boundary or existing 6.
      Fills background cells with 6 and recurses.

ALGORITHM TRACE (task db695cfb, training pair P0)

Background 5. 1s at (3,1) and (9,7). Direction (+1,+1). 6s at (5,3) and
(8,6) lie on the beam path.

March: (4,2)=bg -> 1; (5,3)=6 -> bounce; (6,4)=bg -> 1; (7,5)=bg -> 1;
(8,6)=6 -> bounce; destination (9,7) reached.

Bounce at (5,3) with direction (+1,+1):
  Ray (-1,+1): (4,4),(3,5),(2,6),(1,7),(0,8) -> all become 6.
  Ray (+1,-1): (6,2),(7,1),(8,0) -> all become 6.

Bounce at (8,6) with direction (+1,+1):
  Ray (-1,+1): (7,7),(6,8),(5,9),(4,10),(3,11) -> all become 6.
  Ray (+1,-1): (9,5),(10,4),(11,3),(12,2),(13,1),(14,0) -> all become 6.

Result: 3 new 1s, 19 new 6s, 22 total changes. Output matches.

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: db695cfb.
Score: 28/120 = 23.33% to 29/120 = 24.17%.


Chapter 248: Wave 30 - frame_absorb (WP-288, Layer 263)

Wave 30 adds the frame_absorb named rule to arc_benchmark_2.pl, solving
ARC-AGI-2 task d35bdbdc. This rule recognizes frame blocks (rectangular
3x3 squares or cross-shaped clusters) and builds an absorption graph from
their center-color and arm-color relationships.

WHAT THE RULE DOES

Each grid in task d35bdbdc contains:
- A snake path of 5-valued cells that is always preserved unchanged.
- Frame blocks: either 3x3 squares (8 border cells + 1 center cell) or
  cross shapes (4 arm cells + 1 center cell).
- Each frame block has a center color CV and an arm color AV.
  For monochrome blocks, all cells share one color, so CV = AV.

The transformation: build a directed absorption graph where an edge X -> Y
means X.CV = Y.AV. Process the graph using this priority order:
1. Find any monochrome block Y (CV = AV). If a predecessor X exists
   (X.CV = Y.AV), X absorbs Y: X's center cell is repainted Y.CV, and
   Y's cells are all erased. If no predecessor, Y is erased.
2. After all monochrome blocks are handled: find a chain-start block X
   (no block Z has Z.CV = X.AV). X absorbs its successor Y if one exists;
   otherwise X is erased.
3. Any remaining blocks (forming a cycle) are all erased.

KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(frame_absorb, Grid, Out)
      Collects non-0, non-5 positions; partitions into 4-connected
      components; classifies each as a frame block; solves the absorption
      graph; applies actions via foldl.

  arc2_fa_comps_(Positions, Components)
      BFS partition of cell positions into 4-connected components.

  arc2_fa_block_(Grid, Cells, block(CR,CC,CV,AV,Cells))
      Classifies one component as a frame block. Two-color: the singleton
      color is CV, the majority color is AV, and the center cell is the
      unique cell whose grid value equals CV. Monochrome: CV = AV, and the
      center is the most 4-connected cell in the component.

  arc2_fa_solve_(Blocks, Actions)
      Recursive priority-ordered solver. Returns a list of absorb(X,Y) and
      erase(B) actions.

  arc2_fa_exec_(Action, G0, G1)
      Applies one action to the grid: absorb erases Y and repaints X's
      center; erase zeroes all cells of B.

KEY BUG AND FIX

The snake path (5-valued cells) forms a large 4-connected component.
If 5-cells are included in the position collection, they form a monochrome
block with no predecessor and are incorrectly erased, corrupting the grid.
Fix: exclude 5-values cells from the findall goal with V \= 5.

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: d35bdbdc.
Score: 29/120 = 24.17% to 30/120 = 25.00%.

Chapter 249: Wave 31 - shape_beam (WP-289, Layer 264)

WHAT IT DOES

shape_beam solves ARC-AGI-2 task 5961cc34. A rod (a single value-4 cell
adjacent to a trail of value-2 cells) fires a beam in the direction opposite
its trail. The beam travels through connected shapes made of value-1 body
cells and value-3 exit-marker cells. Each shape the beam enters is converted
entirely to value 2, and the beam is redirected toward the open (background)
side of the exit markers. This relay continues until the beam exits the grid.
Shapes not reached by any beam are erased; the output contains only value 2
on a background.

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(shape_beam, +Grid, -Out)
      Entry point. Guards: exactly one value-4 cell; at least one value-1
      cell. Identifies background BG, rod cell (RR, RC) and beam direction
      (BeamDir), collects shape components, classifies each shape, and runs
      the beam relay before painting the result.

  arc2_sb_bg_(+Grid, -BG)
      Identifies the background as the most-frequent cell value.

  arc2_sb_rod_(+Grid, -RR, -RC, -BeamDir)
      Finds the value-4 cell and its adjacent value-2 trail. BeamDir is
      opposite the trail direction.

  arc2_sb_rod_cells_(+Grid, +RR, +RC, +BeamDir, -RodCells)
      Collects the value-4 cell and all value-2 trail cells as R-C pairs.

  arc2_sb_comps_(+Positions, -Components)
      Partitions a list of R-C positions into 4-connected components using
      BFS seed expansion.

  arc2_sb_classify_(+Grid, +BG, +Comp, -shape(AllCells,ExitDir,Cross,ExAP))
      Classifies one component. Separates value-1 and value-3 cells, calls
      arc2_sb_exit_dir_ to determine the exit direction, and computes the
      cross positions (cols for vertical beams, rows for horizontal beams)
      and exit axis position (row or col of the 3-markers).

  arc2_sb_exit_dir_(+Grid, +BG, +Cells3, -ExitDir)
      Determines the exit direction by the open-face method. The value-3
      cells are always collinear. If they share a row R3, check row R3-1
      (up) and row R3+1 (down) at the marker columns: the side that is all
      BG is the exit direction. If they share a column C3, check columns
      C3-1 (left) and C3+1 (right) at the marker rows similarly.

  arc2_sb_beam_(+Dir, +Cross, +AxisStart, +Shapes, +NR, +NC, +Acc, -Out)
      Recursive beam relay. Finds the closest shape in direction Dir that
      overlaps Cross positions. Fills the gap between AxisStart and the hit,
      adds the shape's cells, redirects the beam with the shape's exit
      parameters, and recurses. Base case fills the remaining gap to the
      grid boundary.

  arc2_sb_hit_(+Dir, +Cross, +AxisStart, +Shapes, -HitShape, -HitPos)
      Finds the shape closest to AxisStart in direction Dir whose cells
      include positions in Cross. Returns HitShape and HitPos (the row or
      column of the closest cell).

  arc2_sb_rect_(+Cross, +From, +To, +Mode, -Cells)
      Generates a rectangle of R-C positions. Mode v: rows vary from From
      to To, columns fixed to Cross list. Mode h: columns vary, rows fixed.

  arc2_sb_blank_(+NR, +NC, +BG, -Blank)
      Creates an NR x NC grid filled with BG.

KEY BUG AND FIX

Naive exit direction detection checks which value-1 cell is adjacent to a
value-3 cell. This fails at corner positions where a value-3 cell is adjacent
to value-1 cells in two directions simultaneously. The correct method: the
value-3 cells in every shape are always collinear (same row OR same column).
Check which adjacent row or column is all-background at the marker positions.
That is the open face, and it is the exit direction.

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: 5961cc34.
Score: 30/120 = 25.00% to 31/120 = 25.83%.

Chapter 250: Wave 32 - shape_classify (WP-290, Layer 265)

WHAT IT DOES

shape_classify solves ARC-AGI-2 task aa4ec2a5. The grid contains exactly two
unique cell values: a background color (the most frequent) and a shape color.
The rule performs three operations on every shape (4-connected component of
non-background cells):
(1) HOLES: background cells that cannot reach the grid boundary via a
4-connected path through background cells are enclosed holes. Holes are
painted 6.
(2) BORDER: outside-reachable background cells that are 8-connected (Chebyshev
distance 1) adjacent to any shape cell are border cells. Border cells are
painted 2.
(3) SHAPE CLASSIFICATION: each shape component is checked against the holes.
If any cell of the component is 4-adjacent to a hole, all cells of that
component are painted 8. Otherwise the component cells remain at their
original color (1).

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(shape_classify, +Grid, -Out)
      Entry point. Guard: exactly 2 unique values. Finds BG, runs the
      outside flood-fill, identifies holes and border cells, partitions
      non-BG cells into components, classifies each component, then
      paints holes 6, border 2, and hole-adjacent components 8.

  arc2_scc_bg_(+Flat, -BG)
      Identifies the background as the most-frequent value in the flat
      cell list using run-length encoding and max_member/2.

  arc2_scc_runs_(+SortedList, -Pairs)
      Run-length encodes a sorted list into Count-Value pairs.

  arc2_scc_run_count_(+List, +Val, +Acc, -Count, -Remainder)
      Counts the leading run of Val in List.

  arc2_scc_outside_(+Grid, +NR, +NC, +BG, -Outside)
      BFS flood-fill from all boundary BG cells through BG cells
      (4-connected), returning the set of all outside-reachable BG cells.

  arc2_scc_bfs_(+Queue, +Visited, +Grid, +NR, +NC, +BG, -All)
      BFS worker: expands the queue through unvisited in-bounds BG cells
      using 4-connectivity, accumulating all visited cells.

  arc2_scc_nonbg_nbr_(+Grid, +BG, +R, +C)
      Succeeds if cell (R,C) has at least one non-BG cell among its 8
      Chebyshev neighbors (4 cardinal + 4 diagonal).

  arc2_scc_comps_(+Positions, -Components)
      Partitions a list of R-C positions into 4-connected components.
      Calls arc2_scc_grow_/5 with BFS seed expansion.

  arc2_scc_grow_(+Queue, +Pool, +Acc, -Comp, -Remaining)
      BFS component expansion. For the head cell, finds all Pool members
      that are 4-adjacent, removes them from Pool immediately to prevent
      re-discovery, adds them to the queue and accumulator, then recurses.

  arc2_scc_touches_hole_(+Holes, +Comp)
      Succeeds if any cell in Comp has a 4-adjacent cell in Holes.
      Used with include/3 to collect hole-touching components.

KEY BUGS AND FIXES

Bug 1: Border used 4-connected adjacency to detect non-BG neighbors.
Fix: The expected border includes cells diagonally adjacent to shape cells.
Changed arc2_scc_nonbg_nbr_ to check all 8 Chebyshev neighbors instead of
only the 4 cardinal neighbors.

Bug 2: foldl lambda variable name clash. The three painting foldls originally
all used the same variable G1 as both the lambda output parameter and the
chained accumulator. After the first foldl bound G1, the second foldl's
lambda received G1 as an already-bound value from copy_term, causing it to
try to unify the expected output with the existing grid and fail.
Fix: Use distinct accumulator names (GA, GB, Out) for each foldl and
decompose elements explicitly (RC6 = R6-C6 etc.).

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: aa4ec2a5.
Score: 31/120 = 25.83% to 32/120 = 26.67%.

Chapter 251: Wave 33 - tile_stamp (WP-291, Layer 266)

WHAT IT DOES

tile_stamp solves ARC-AGI-2 task bf45cf4b. Every input grid contains exactly
two non-background objects:

(1) KERNEL: a compact rectangular tile that uses more than one distinct
non-background color. This tile is the stamp reproduced in the output.

(2) INDICATOR PATTERN: scattered cells sharing a single non-background color.
Their bounding box defines an NR x NC binary grid where each position is T
(non-background = stamp here) or F (background = leave empty).

The output has NR*KR rows and NC*KC cols (KR and KC are the kernel dimensions).
Each tile position (r, c) in the NR x NC grid receives a copy of the kernel if
the corresponding indicator cell is non-background, or background cells otherwise.

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(tile_stamp, +Grid, -Out)
      Entry point. Finds BG, collects non-BG cells, partitions into 4-connected
      components, identifies the kernel component (>1 distinct value), extracts
      the kernel sub-grid, computes indicator pattern bounding box, builds output.

  arc2_stamp_nonbg_(+Grid, +BG, -Cells)
      Collects all R-C positions where the grid cell value differs from BG.

  arc2_stamp_kern_(+Comps, +Grid, -KR1, -KC1, -KR, -KC, -PatCells)
      Separates the kernel component (>1 distinct value) from pattern components.
      Returns kernel bounding box parameters and merged pattern cell list.

  arc2_stamp_multi_val_(+Comp, +Grid)
      Succeeds if the component cells yield more than one distinct value in Grid.

  arc2_stamp_bbox_(+Cells, -R1, -C1, -R2, -C2)
      Computes bounding box of a list of R-C pairs via min/max operations.

  arc2_stamp_subgrid_(+Grid, +R1, +C1, +KR, +KC, -SubGrid)
      Extracts a KR x KC sub-grid starting at (R1, C1), keeping interior BG cells.

  arc2_stamp_out_(+PatCells, +PR1, +PC1, +PNR, +PNC, +BG, +Kern, +KR, +KC, -Out)
      Builds the full output grid row by row using arc2_stamp_row_.

Chapter 252: Wave 34 - rail_fill (WP-292, Layer 267)

WHAT IT DOES

rail_fill solves ARC-AGI-2 task 271d71e2. Every input grid contains one or
more sub-objects. Each sub-object is a 0-bordered rectangular box with cells
valued 5 or 7 inside, plus two parallel 9-rails on one side (the arm). The
two rails are the inner rail (closer to the box) and the outer rail.

arm_gap = distance between inner and outer rail. The box gains
min(arm_gap, free_interior_cells) new 7-cells, filling from the arm side in
a direction-specific traversal order. The box shifts by the same step count
toward the outer rail. The inner rail merges into the new box. New arm_gap =
arm_gap - step count; if 0 only the outer rail remains.

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(rail_fill, +Grid, -Out)
      Entry point. Background = Grid[0][0]. Finds 0-bordered boxes with 5/7
      interiors, computes transformation plans, and builds output grid.

  arc2_rf_arm_(+Grid,+NR,+NC,+R1,+C1,+R2,+C2,-Dir,-Inner,-Outer)
      Scans each cardinal direction from box boundary; first 9-rail = inner,
      second 9-rail = outer. Sets Dir to arm_left/right/top/bottom.

  arc2_rf_fill_order_(+Dir,+H,+W,-Order)
      Produces (R-C) pairs in arm-side-outward traversal order.

  arc2_rf_make_interior_(+H,+W,+N7,+Order,-Interior)
      First N7 positions in Order get 7; rest get 5.

  arc2_rf_box_transform_(+Grid,+NR,+NC,+Box,-Plan)
      Computes arm, gap, fill, new box position, and new rail positions.

  arc2_rf_cell_in_plan_(+R,+C,+Plan,-V)
      Returns 9 (rail), 0 (border), or interior value for (R,C) in plan.

BENCHMARK RESULT

Chapter 253: Wave 35 - bbox_fill (ARC-AGI-2 task 9385bd28)

WHAT IT DOES

bbox_fill reads a 2-column legend in the bottom-left of the grid. Each row
of the legend maps object color K to fill color V. For each mapping the rule
finds all K-cells outside the legend, computes their bounding box, and fills
that box with V. Smaller bounding boxes override larger ones where they
overlap. The K-cells themselves are preserved unless the bbox is dense (all
cells are K-cells), in which case they become V.

LEGEND DETECTION

Legend = leftmost column pair (LC, LC+1) in the bottom half where a
contiguous block of rows has Grid[R][LC] != BG. The right column may be BG,
encoding "fill bbox with BG" (a punch-through that blocks larger fills).

FILL MODES

V != 0 or V == BG : fill bbox with V; re-paint K-cells (sparse) or convert
them to V (dense).
V == 0 and BG != 0 : erase mode - no fill drawn; K-cells treated like BG.

OVERRIDE ORDER

Bboxes sorted ascending by area. arc2_bxf_fill_for_ returns the first
(smallest) matching bbox's fill color for a given cell.

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(bbox_fill, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_bxf_legend_(+Grid, +BG, -LC, -LegRows, -LegMap)
  arc2_bxf_scan_up_(+Grid, +BG, +R, +C, -Block)
  arc2_bxf_bbox_(+Cells, -R1, -C1, -R2, -C2)
  arc2_bxf_is_erase_(+V, +BG)
  arc2_bxf_fill_for_(+R, +C, +InfosAsc, -V)
  arc2_bxf_cell_val_(+R, +C, +OrigV, +BG, +LegCells, +LegMap, +InfosAsc, -V)
  arc2_bxf_build_row_(+Grid, +R, +NC, +BG, +LegCells, +LegMap, +InfosAsc, -Row)
  arc2_bxf_update_bbox_(+RC, +Acc, -NewAcc)

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: 9385bd28 (4 training pairs).
Score: 34/120 = 28.33% to 35/120 = 29.17%.

Chapter 254: Wave 36 - slide_void (ARC-AGI-2 task 332f06d7)

WHAT IT DOES

slide_void solves maze-navigation tasks where an H x W void (0-cell block)
must slide through a river of 1-cells to reach the farthest corner of the
maze. The grid contains an H x W block of 0-cells (the void), an H x W block
of 2-cells (the marker), and river cells (1) on a non-0/1 background. The
void slides to the FARTHEST reachable dead-end in the block-movement graph.
If that dead-end is adjacent to the 2-marker, the void enters the marker
pocket instead.

BLOCK-MOVEMENT BFS

The void moves as a rigid H x W block. A slide in one direction is valid if
the new cells entering the block's leading face are all passable (value 0 or 1
in the original grid). BFS explores block positions (top-left coordinates),
not individual cells. This is critical: cell-level BFS gives wrong distances
because the block moves as a whole.

DEAD-END DETECTION

A dead-end is a block position with exactly 1 slide neighbour in the full
block-movement graph (not just the BFS tree). arc2_svo_is_deadend_ counts
slide neighbours via findall and checks length == 1.

FARTHEST DEAD-END

Among all reachable non-start dead-ends, the rule picks the one with MAXIMUM
BFS distance from the void's starting position. This is the far end of the
river maze. If that dead-end is adjacent to the 2-marker (any face cell == 2),
the target becomes the marker's top-left position.

OUTPUT

Old void cells (original 0-cells) become river (1). Target cells become
void (0). All other cells remain unchanged.

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(slide_void, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_svo_void_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, -VR, -VC, -H, -W)
  arc2_svo_marker_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, -MR, -MC, -H, -W)
  arc2_svo_one_nbr_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +R, +C, +H, +W, -NR2, -NC2)
  arc2_svo_row_passable_(+Grid, +FR, +C, +W)
  arc2_svo_col_passable_(+Grid, +R, +FC, +H)
  arc2_svo_block_bfs_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +VR, +VC, +H, +W, -Reached)
  arc2_svo_is_deadend_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +R, +C, +H, +W)
  arc2_svo_farthest_end_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +H, +W, +VR, +VC, +Reached, -FR, -FC)
  arc2_svo_adj_marker_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +R, +C, +H, +W)
  arc2_svo_cell_out_(+OldCells, +NewCells, +R, +C, +Orig, -Out)

BENCHMARK RESULT

Task solved: 332f06d7 (4 training pairs).
Score: 35/120 = 29.17% to 36/120 = 30.00%.


Chapter 255: Wave 37 - concentric_rings (WP-295, Layer 270)

WHAT IT DOES

concentric_rings solves ARC-AGI-2 task 13e47133. Every input grid contains a
background color BG (most frequent value), a divider color Div (most frequent
non-BG value), and a sparse set of single-cell marker cells (non-BG, non-Div).
The Div cells form walls that partition the remaining cells into one or more
4-connected components. Each component is filled with concentric rectangular
rings, where ring 0 is the outermost layer and ring numbers increase inward.
Marker cells encode a color cycle: the marker color at ring R becomes the fill
color for all cells at ring R in that component, repeating cyclically.

COMPONENT AND RING DETECTION

arc2_cri_bg_ finds BG by counting occurrences (most frequent). arc2_cri_div_
finds Div by the same method excluding BG. arc2_cri_comps_ collects all non-Div
cells and partitions them into 4-connected components via arc2_cri_flood_. For
each component, arc2_cri_is_bnd_ marks ring-0 cells (grid boundary or
8-connected adjacent to any Div cell). arc2_cri_bfs_ runs multi-source
4-connected BFS from all ring-0 cells to compute ring distances.

PREDICATES

  arc2_transform(concentric_rings, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_cri_bg_(+Grid, -BG)
  arc2_cri_div_(+Grid, +BG, -Div)
  arc2_cri_nbrs_(+NR1, +NC1, +R, +C, -Nbrs)
  arc2_cri_nbrs8_(+NR1, +NC1, +R, +C, -Nbrs)
  arc2_cri_is_bnd_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +Div, +R, +C)
  arc2_cri_flood_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +Div, +Queue, +Vis, -Comp)
  arc2_cri_comps_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +Div, +NDCs, +Seen, -Comps)
  arc2_cri_bfs_(+Grid, +NR1, +NC1, +Div, +Frontier, +Acc, -Res)
  arc2_cri_build_cycle_(+RCPairs, +BG, -Cycle)

Chapter 257: Wave 39 - room_outline (WP-297, Layer 272)

WHAT IT DOES

room_outline solves ARC-AGI-2 task 8f3a5a89. Every input grid contains
background cells (value 8), wall cells (value 1), and a unique seed cell
(value 6). The seed sits inside an accessible room: a 4-connected region of
background cells reachable from the seed without crossing any wall. The rule
traces the room perimeter with value 7, preserves boundary-connected wall
cells, and erases isolated floating wall clusters.

ACCESSIBLE ROOM AND PERIMETER

arc2_ro_flood_ runs 4-connected BFS from the seed through value-8 cells to
build the accessible set Vis. Wall components touching Vis are preserved
(WallKeep). Among WallKeep, only components that include a grid-edge cell
(BdryWalls) generate perimeter cells. Accessible BG cells on the grid boundary
or 8-adjacent to BdryWalls become perimeter (7). Accessible BG cells not
adjacent to any BdryWall remain interior (8).

Chapter 258: Wave 40 - grid_tile (WP-298, Layer 273)

WHAT IT DOES

grid_tile solves ARC-AGI-2 task eee78d87. Every input is a 6x6 grid with
background value 7 and a small colored shape whose non-background cells fit
within a 3x3 bounding box. The rule reads the nine cells of that bounding box,
decodes four tiling constants from their positions, tiles a 16x16 output grid
with a period-3 repeating pattern, and highlights the central 6x6 region of
the output by replacing all background-zero cells with 9.

BOUNDING BOX AND TILE DECODING

arc2_gt_bbox_ finds the minimum row and column of all non-7 cells. The bounding
box top-left (Rmin, Cmin) anchors the 3x3 sub-grid. arc2_gt_tile_ reads all
nine values and derives four tiling constants by position:

  corners (0,0)(0,2)(2,0)(2,2)    -> block  = 7 if all four are 7, else 0
  top+bottom midpoints (0,1)(2,1)  -> v_edge = 7 if both are 7, else 0
  left+right midpoints (1,0)(1,2)  -> h_edge = 7 if both are 7, else 0
  center (1,1)                     -> corner = 7 if it is 7, else 0

PERIOD-3 TILING AND ACTIVE REGION

arc2_gt_cell_ maps each (R,C) to a position type by (R mod 3, C mod 3):
corner(0,0), h_edge(0,!=0), v_edge(!=0,0), or block(!=0,!=0). The cell emits
the corresponding constant. Within rows 5-10, cols 5-10 (the central 6x6), any
0-value cell emits 9 instead.

  arc2_transform(grid_tile, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_gt_bbox_(+Grid, -Rmin, -Cmin)
  arc2_gt_tile_(+Grid, +R0, +C0, -Block, -HEdge, -VEdge, -Corner)
  arc2_gt_gv_(+Grid, +R, +C, -V)
  arc2_gt_build_(+Block, +HEdge, +VEdge, +Corner, -Out)
  arc2_gt_cell_(+R, +C, +Block, +HEdge, +VEdge, +Corner, -V)

Task solved: eee78d87 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 37/120 = 30.83% to 38/120 = 31.67%.

Chapter 260: Wave 42 - section_sort (WP-300, Layer 275)

section_sort solves ARC-AGI-2 task 78332cb0. All-6 divider rows and/or columns
partition the input grid into rectangular sections. The sections are sorted in
ascending order of their non-background, non-6 cell count. The output direction
is the transpose of the input direction (a vertical stack becomes a horizontal
row; a horizontal row becomes a vertical stack). For 2-dimensional inputs (both
row-dividers and column-dividers present), output direction is determined by
comparing the sum of section bounding-box heights against the sum of widths.
Single-cell 6-dividers are re-inserted between sections in the output.

Chapter 261: Wave 43 - frame_stamp (WP-301, Layer 276)

frame_stamp solves ARC-AGI-2 task 247ef758. A bordered room occupies the
right portion of the grid (columns 4 and beyond). The room's outer frame has
a dominant color (BdrBG). Anomalous values in the top row of the frame encode
a stamp column per color (col K encodes color C). Anomalous values in the
rightmost column of the frame encode a stamp row per color (row R encodes
color C). Colored shapes occupy the left side (columns 0-3) of the grid.

For each color C that has both a top-row column anomaly and a right-column
row anomaly, and also has a left-side shape: the shape is stamped inside the
room centered at the (R, K) intersection. Multiple placements per color are
supported when multiple anomalies exist. Stamps are applied in descending
cell-count order so smaller shapes overwrite larger ones where they overlap.
Left-side shapes that were stamped are erased to BG. Left-side shapes without
any frame anomalies are preserved unchanged.

Chapter 262: Wave 44 - jigsaw_fill (WP-302, Layer 277)

jigsaw_fill solves ARC-AGI-2 task 5dbc8537. The input grid splits at a row or
column boundary into two non-empty regions. One region is a 2-color template
(solid color S, hole color H). The other region is a multi-color objects area
(background B plus shaped pieces). Every non-B 4-connected component in the
objects region is a "piece" that exactly tiles one contiguous sub-region of
H-cells in the template.

The corner cell (top-left of the template) always holds the solid color S.
This corner-color heuristic identifies S unambiguously even when the count of
S-cells equals the count of H-cells. Each piece is normalized to a (0,0)
bounding-box origin by subtracting the minimum row and column offsets from all
cell positions. Multi-color pieces (where different cells in the same BFS
component carry different colors) preserve per-cell colors through the
normalization and placement steps.

Pieces are sorted by ascending number of valid placements (fewest-first) to
guide backtracking. Backtracking exact cover then assigns each piece to a
non-overlapping sub-region of H-cells. The filled template is the output.

Key predicates:
  arc2_jf_find_split_(+Grid, -Tmpl, -Hole, -ObjGrid, -ObjBg)
  arc2_jf_check_tmpl_obj_(+Tmpl, +Obj, -Hole, -ObjBg)
  arc2_jf_mode_(+List, -Mode)
  arc2_jf_holes_(+Grid, +Hole, -Pairs)
  arc2_jf_pieces_(+Grid, +Bg, -Pieces)
  arc2_jf_bfs_(+Queue, +Grid, +Bg, +Rows, +Cols, +Vis0, +Acc0, -Comp, -Vis)
  arc2_jf_normalize_(+Piece, -Norm)
  arc2_jf_valid_placements_(+Norm, +Holes, +TR, +TC, -Placements)
  arc2_jf_augment_(+Pieces, +Holes, +TR, +TC, -PiecesWP)
  arc2_jf_cover_(+Sorted, +RemHoles, +Map0, -Map)
  arc2_jf_apply_(+Tmpl, +ColorMap, -Out)
  arc2_transform(jigsaw_fill, +Grid, -Out)

Chapter 263: Wave 45 - stair_fill (WP-303, Layer 278)

stair_fill solves ARC-AGI-2 task 28a6681f. The input grid contains staircase
wall cells (any non-0, non-1 color forming L-shaped step outlines) and exactly N
misplaced 1-cells. The output places exactly N 1-cells into the interior step
pocket recesses and clears all others. The 1-cell count is exactly conserved.

The key insight is that step pockets fall into two categories. Doubly-bounded
pockets have staircase wall cells on both the left and right sides of an open
horizontal run. Single-bounded pockets have a staircase wall only on the left and
reach the grid right boundary. A two-phase bottom-to-top algorithm fills
doubly-bounded pockets first (Phase 1), then single-bounded pockets with any
remaining budget (Phase 2). Within each row, smaller gaps fill before wider ones.

  arc2_sf_count1_(+Grid, -N)
  arc2_sf_is_wall_(+Cell)
  arc2_sf_open_(+Cell)
  arc2_sf_find_ranges_(+Row, +NC, -Ranges)
  arc2_sf_scan_ranges_(+Row, +C, +NC, +Start, +InRun, -Ranges)
  arc2_sf_run_end_(+Start, +C, -Range)
  arc2_sf_below_ok_(+Grid, +R, +C_L, +C_R, +NR, +Pockets)
  arc2_sf_phase1_(+Grid, +NR, +NC, +LR, +LC, +N, +P0, -P, -N1)
  arc2_sf_phase1_rows_(+Grid, +NR, +NC, +LR, +LC, +R, +N, +P0, -P, -N1)
  arc2_sf_db_ranges_(+Row, +Grid, +R, +NR, +NC, +LC, +Pockets, -DB)
  arc2_sf_phase2_(+Grid, +NR, +NC, +LR, +LC, +N, +P0, -P)
  arc2_sf_phase2_rows_(+Grid, +NR, +NC, +LR, +LC, +R, +N, +P0, -P)
  arc2_sf_sb_ranges_(+Row, +Grid, +R, +NR, +NC, +LC, +Pockets, -SB)
  arc2_sf_fill_ranges_(+Ranges, +R, +N, +P0, -P, -N1)
  arc2_sf_fill_range_(+C_L, +C_R, +R, +N, +P0, -P, -N1)
  arc2_sf_build_out_(+Grid, +Pockets, -Out)

Chapter 264: Wave 46 - two_join (WP-304, Layer 279)

two_join solves ARC-AGI-2 task 20270e3b. The input contains two connected
4-cell objects, each with a cluster of 7-cells marking its connection point.
The output merges the objects by shifting one so its 7-cluster aligns
adjacent to the other's 7-cluster, replacing all 7s with 4s.


PATTERN

Each input has exactly two 7-clusters (groups of 7-cells connected by
4-adjacency). For each candidate ordering (base 7-cluster and shift 7-cluster):
  Object A = all non-1 cells reachable from base_7 via BFS through non-1 cells
  Object B = all remaining non-1 cells (includes isolated pieces)
  dr = min_row(base_7) - min_row(shift_7) - 1
  dc = min_col(base_7) - min_col(shift_7)
  Shifted B = Object B shifted by (dr, dc)
If all shifted positions have row >= 0 and col >= 0, compute merged bbox area.
Pick the ordering with the smaller area. Output: merged positions -> 4, rest -> 1.


KEY PREDICATES

  arc2_tj_nonbg_pos_(+Grid, +R, +NR, +NC, -Pos)
  arc2_tj_all_sevens_(+Grid, +R, +NR, +NC, -Pos)
  arc2_tj_seven_clusters_(+Sevens, -G1, -G2)
  arc2_tj_bfs_obj_(+Seeds, +NR, +NC, +AllPos, -ObjA)
  arc2_tj_subtract_pos_(+All, +Remove, -Result)

Chapter 265: Wave 47 - shape_count (WP-305, Layer 280)

shape_count solves ARC-AGI-2 task 58490d8a. The input has a rectangular
0-cell template (odd rows have color markers at col TC0+1) and a game board
with identical small shape instances. Count instances per color using iterative
bounding-box expansion; output repeats each color marker Count times at odd
output columns.

Instance detection: iterative bbox expansion groups all same-color cells within

handles diamond-cross shapes (C _ C / _ C _ / C _ C) that are NOT 4-connected.
