markers; the helpers tw_background_ and tw_components_ from the
twin_lift pack are reused unchanged. A motif rectangle is two cells
wide, at least four cells long, fills its bounding box completely,
and holds color-2 cells on exactly one long side; its anchor end is
the long-axis end farther from that 2-run. A marker on the left or
right grid edge aligns with the vertical motif whose row span holds
the marker row, and a marker on the top or bottom edge aligns with
the horizontal motif whose column span holds the marker column; the
marker teaches the lesson pairing its axis offset from the anchor
end with its color, and votes on the polarity by comparing its side
of the motif with the motif's 2-run side. All votes must agree.
Every motif then casts one ray per deduplicated lesson at the
lesson offset from its own anchor end, from its exit side out to
the grid edge, and the rebuild gives a background cell under a ray
the ray color while every other cell keeps its input value. The
induce clause dispatches early, directly after stripe_cycle,
requiring preserved dimensions, 10 to 200 foreground cells, at
least two parsing motif rectangles, and exactly three singleton
markers before verifying every training pair.

The lesson: the exit direction is a grid parameter, not a motif
parameter. In the first training grid every motif exits on the same
side as its color-2 run, in the second training grid and the test
grid every motif exits on the opposite side, and no structural
feature of the motifs separates the cases - even their palettes
change from grid to grid. The markers decide it: each marker lies
on the ray of its own color, so its side of the aligned motif
reveals the polarity of the whole grid. What looked like an
inconsistent rule became a single parameter read off the input at
answer time, and the rule passed the single-task test and the full
regression on the first compile.

Key predicates:
  ms_classify_/3       - split components into motifs and markers
  ms_motif_/2          - parse one motif rectangle with its anchor
  ms_lesson_/7         - one marker's offset-color-polarity lesson
  ms_ray_/6            - the ray cells cast by one motif
  ms_row_/5            - rebuild one output row under the rays
  arc2_transform(motif_shadow, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, motif_shadow)

Task solved: 64efde09 (2 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 80/120 = 66.67% to 81/120 = 67.50%.


Chapter 303: Wave 85 - dial_rotor (WP-343, Layer 318)

dial_rotor solves ARC-AGI-2 task 6ffbe589. The task gives three
training pairs and one test case, all on 20x20 inputs over
background 0, with square outputs of 13x13, 10x10, 10x10, and
13x13. Each input holds one main figure - a framed square wrapped
in a dotted ring - plus two or three small straight pieces lying
far away from the figure. The palettes vary per grid: ring 3 with
core colors 8 and 6 in the first training input, ring 5 with core
colors 3 and 4 in the second, ring 4 with core colors 1 and 2 in
the third, and ring 5 with core colors 3 and 4 again in the test.

Rule: the main figure is cropped together with every component
within Chebyshev distance 3 of its bounding box; each far-away
piece is a dial - a straight single-color run of color C with
length L commanding "rotate color C's layer by L quarter turns
clockwise" (L mod 4); the ring color is the unique color on the
crop's outermost layer and the ring thickness is the minimum inset
of any non-ring-colored cell; the ring zone spins by the ring
color's dial (staying put without one) and the core block spins by
the largest dial among its own colors.

Algorithm:

The non-zero cells are grouped into 8-connected components with the
reused helper tw_components_ from the twin_lift pack, and the
component with the largest bounding box is the main figure. Every
component with a cell within Chebyshev distance 3 of that bounding
box merges into the crop box - this pulls in the detached dotted-
ring pieces of the test grid - and every farther component is a
dial piece parsed by dr_run_ as a straight single-color run. The
crop must be square. The ring color is read from the crop's
outermost layer, the thickness K is the minimum inset of any
non-ring-colored cell, and the core block is the central region
inset by K. The ring zone rotates by the ring color's dial and the
core block by the largest dial among the core's colors; dr_rotn_
delegates the quarter turns to the existing rotate_90_cw,
rotate_180, and rotate_90_ccw named rules, and dr_row_ pastes the
rotated core back into the center of the spun crop. The induce
clause dispatches early, directly after motif_shadow, requiring a
strictly smaller square output, 10 to 300 foreground cells, and at
least two components before verifying every training pair.

The lesson: the palette does not pick the rotation. The second
training grid and the test grid share the exact same colors - ring
5 with core 3 and 4 - yet one needs a core quarter turn and the
other a core half turn, so any variant-by-palette reading is a red
herring. The debris pieces that look like noise are the actual
instruction stream: their lengths are dials, and the test's
five-cell ring dial proves the modulo-4 reading, one full
revolution plus a quarter. The decisive method was to brute-force
the ring and core transform pairs per training pair first and hunt
for the selecting input feature second: length-of-piece matched all
four grids with zero contradictions, and the rule passed the
single-task test and the full regression on the first compile.

Key predicates:
  dr_maxbbox_/2        - component with the largest bounding box
  dr_near_box_/5       - component within distance 3 of the box
  dr_run_/3            - parse one dial piece as a straight run
  dr_rotn_/3           - rotate a grid N quarter turns clockwise
  dr_row_/8            - rebuild one output row from ring and core
  arc2_transform(dial_rotor, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, dial_rotor)

Task solved: 6ffbe589 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 81/120 = 67.50% to 82/120 = 68.33%.


Chapter 304: Wave 86 - xray_frame (WP-344, Layer 319)

xray_frame solves ARC-AGI-2 task a6f40cea. The task gives three
training pairs and one test case, on inputs of 20x20, 30x30, 22x22,
and 24x24 with small cropped outputs of 4x7, 13x14, 7x8, and 8x10.
Each input holds one large rectangular frame of a single color
whose interior is uniformly filled - with the background color in
two grids and with a distinct fill color in the others - plus
several partial rectangle outlines drawn outside the frame. The
outlines visibly slide under the frame: their edges break off at
the frame boundary and, in one grid, re-emerge on the far side.
Two grids include outlines whose edges alternate two colors cell
by cell.

Rule: the output is the frame interior with every shape's hidden
cells restored, as if the frame were transparent - an x-ray of the
occluded region; each shape's bounding-box perimeter is completed
through the frame, single-color shapes restore their own color,
and two-color alternating shapes restore the color-swapped mirror
(vertical first, then horizontal) of the visible cell inside the
shape's own bounding box.

Algorithm:

The majority color is the background. Candidate frames are found by
xr_ring_: single-color 8-connected components (via the reused
helper tw_components_ from the twin_lift pack) whose cells form
exactly the perimeter of their bounding box, at least 3x3, with a
uniformly single-colored interior; xr_frame_ keeps the largest
candidate by bounding-box area, so the small perfect boxes
elsewhere in the test grid remain ordinary shapes. xr_shapes_
collects every non-background cell strictly outside the frame's
bounding box, groups the cells into 8-connected components, and
merges components sharing an identical color set - reuniting
outline halves that the frame split apart. xr_hidden_ walks each
shape's bounding-box perimeter and keeps the cells strictly inside
the frame interior, and xr_restore_ paints them: a single-color
shape paints its own color, while a two-color shape reflects the
hidden cell to the opposite edge of its own bounding box (vertical
mirror first, horizontal as fallback), reads the visible color
there with xr_visible_, and paints the swapped color with xr_swap_.
xr_out_cell_ assembles the interior crop with the restored cells
overlaid. The induce clause dispatches early, directly after
dial_rotor, requiring a strictly smaller output whose dimensions
equal the frame interior dimensions before verifying every
training pair.

The lesson: the two-color alternating outlines are a trap for every
positional-parity reading. Every visible cell of every two-color
shape in all four grids obeys a plain (row + column) checkerboard,
and the checkerboard correctly restores three of the four
alternating shapes - but the 9/8 shape in the third training pair
demands the exact anti-checkerboard in its hidden cells. Plain
alternation continuation, perimeter-path alternation,
border-skipping alternation, 180-degree rotation, and periodic
translation all fail on at least one shape. Only the color-swapped
mirror within the shape's own bounding box explains every hidden
cell of every shape, and writing out every hidden cell of every
training pair by hand before coding is what exposed both the trap
and its escape. The rule passed the single-task test and the full
regression on the first compile.

Key predicates:
  xr_frame_/6          - largest perfect ring with uniform interior
  xr_ring_/7           - one candidate single-color ring
  xr_shapes_/7         - outside shapes merged by color set
  xr_hidden_/9         - hidden perimeter cells inside the interior
  xr_restore_/13       - restored color via the swap-mirror rule
  xr_out_cell_/5       - interior crop cell with overlay
  arc2_transform(xray_frame, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, xray_frame)

Task solved: a6f40cea (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 82/120 = 68.33% to 83/120 = 69.17%.


Chapter 305: Wave 87 - twin_toggle (WP-345, Layer 320)

twin_toggle solves ARC-AGI-2 task c7f57c3e. The task gives two
training pairs and one test case, on grids of 18x18, 18x18, and
28x28, with every output the same size as its input. Each grid
holds several shapes on a uniform majority-color background. In the
first pair the shapes are small plants - a flower of 1s with a 2
center above a stem and a base - and the stem-and-base region is
drawn either in colors 2 and 3 or entirely in color 8. In the
second pair the shapes are X-bodies of 1s with a 2 center carrying
either a single 4 attached below or a single 6 attached above. The
test mixes 3-center and 6-center bodies with a 2-decoration above
or a 4-decoration below, and some shapes are exact scaled-up copies
of the small ones: scale 2 in training and scale 3 in the test.

Rule: every grid contains integer-scaled copies of exactly two
variant templates that share an identical body of same-colored
cells under a best-overlap alignment; the output redraws every
shape as the other variant at its own scale, positioned so the
shared body cells stay exactly where they were.

Algorithm:

The majority color is the background, and the foreground cells
group into 8-connected components with the reused helpers
tw_background_ and tw_components_ from the twin_lift pack.
tt_shape_ crops each component to its bounding box on a clean local
canvas holding only its own cells, and tt_reduce_ fully reduces the
crop by the largest integer scale whose square blocks are all
uniform - tt_blocky_ checks the blocks and tt_shrink_ samples them
- yielding a canonical template and a scale factor, so the scale-2
and scale-3 copies collapse onto the same canonical templates as
their small twins. The grid must present exactly two distinct
canonical templates. tt_align_ scores every translation offset of
the second template against the first by the count of overlapping
cells agreeing in color and keeps the maximum; those agreeing cells
are the shared body. tt_draw_ then redraws every shape as the OTHER
template, upscaled by the shape's own scale factor and positioned
through the alignment offset so the shared body stays fixed, and
tt_render_ paints the toggled cells onto a fresh background canvas
after a bounds check. The induce clause dispatches early, directly
after xray_frame, requiring preserved dimensions, a sparse
foreground of 8 to 400 cells, at least two components, and exactly
two canonical templates before verifying every training pair.

The lesson: the human-steps description frames this task as three
color roles - a base, a mid, and a pivot - with an adjacency test
choosing between a color swap and a color promotion. That reading
is hard to code and does not generalize: the first pair recolors a
stem and base, the second pair moves a marker from below one shape
to above another while changing 4 to 6, and the test does both at
once. Dumping all six grids and writing the complete rule in plain
English before coding revealed the far simpler twin-toggle
invariant, and one uniform mechanism - canonicalize, pair, align,
toggle - covers all three decoration styles with no per-color
reasoning at all. The rule passed both training pairs and the test
case on the first prototype run and the full regression on the
first compile.

Key predicates:
  tt_shape_/3          - canonical template, scale, and origin
  tt_reduce_/5         - maximal uniform block reduction
  tt_blocky_/4         - every SxS block is uniform
  tt_shrink_/5         - sample block colors into the template
  tt_align_/5          - best same-colored overlap offset
  tt_draw_/7           - one toggled twin cell
  tt_render_/5         - fresh canvas with the toggled shapes
  arc2_transform(twin_toggle, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, twin_toggle)

Task solved: c7f57c3e (2 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 83/120 = 69.17% to 84/120 = 70.00%.


Chapter 306: Wave 88 - band_jigsaw (WP-346, Layer 321)

band_jigsaw solves ARC-AGI-2 task 16b78196. The task gives two
training pairs and one test case, all on 30x30 grids, with every
output the same size as its input. Each grid holds one wide band
component whose bounding box spans the full grid width - or, in the
test case, the full grid height - with ragged, notched edges, plus
three to five small single-color shapes scattered around it. The
output keeps the band exactly where it is, erases the shapes from
their original positions, and restacks every shape flush against
the band so the pieces interlock like a jigsaw puzzle.

Rule: every shape is translated - never rotated, flipped, or
recolored - so that the shapes on each side of the band exactly
tile the region between the band's ragged edge profile and a
perfectly straight outer edge, plugging the band's notches on the
way; the side split, the intervals, and every placement are found
by search.

Algorithm:

The majority color is the background, and the foreground cells
group into 8-connected components with the reused helpers
tw_background_ and tw_components_ from the twin_lift pack. bj_span_
selects the unique component whose bounding box spans a full grid
dimension as the band, and bj_canon_dims_ with bj_canon_cells_
transposes a vertical band into the canonical horizontal frame.
bj_profile_ reads the band's bottom profile as the maximum band row
per column and its top profile as the minimum, and bj_flipnum_
reflects the top profile vertically so both sides solve as a
bottom-side chain. bj_partition_ assigns each shape to a side by
backtracking search, bj_norm_ normalizes each shape to a sorted
cell list at its bounding-box corner, and bj_flipnorm_ does the
same after a vertical flip for the far side. bj_chain_ picks a
column interval at least as wide as the widest assigned shape and
forces the straight outer edge row from the exact-area equation -
interval width times outer row minus profile sum equals total shape
area - then bj_tile_ exact-tiles the region by anchored exact
cover: the row-major first uncovered cell must host some unused
shape's row-major first cell. bj_unflip_ and bj_uncanon_cell_ map
the placements back, and the reused tt_render_ paints band plus
placements onto a fresh background canvas. The induce clause
dispatches early, directly after twin_toggle, requiring preserved
dimensions, 20 to 500 foreground cells, exactly one full-span
component, and two to eight movable shapes of at most 40 cells
before verifying every training pair.

The lesson: the human-steps description splits the shapes into wide
ones above the band and narrow ones below, but the second training
pair stacks all three shapes below the band, so the side split must
be searched rather than assumed. Hand-tracing all six grids before
coding exposed the real invariant - a straight outer edge - and
that edge turns out to be a computed quantity, not a searched one:
given an interval, arithmetic forces it, collapsing the packing
search to a handful of exact-cover calls. The test grid rotates the
whole scene ninety degrees, with a vertical band and chains on its
left and right, so the orientation-general canonicalization by
transposition plus vertical flip was mandatory, exactly as the
Wave 85-87 surveys predicted. The rule passed both training pairs
and the test case on the first prototype run and the full
regression on the first compile.

Key predicates:
  bj_span_/4           - full-span band selection and orientation
  bj_canon_dims_/5     - canonical dimensions under transposition
  bj_canon_cells_/3    - cells into the canonical frame
  bj_profile_/4        - per-column band edge profile
  bj_partition_/3      - side assignment by search
  bj_norm_/2           - row-major normalized shape cells
  bj_flipnorm_/3       - normalize after a vertical flip
  bj_chain_/5          - interval choice and forced outer edge
  bj_tile_/3           - anchored exact-cover tiling
  bj_unflip_/3         - placements back out of the flipped frame
  bj_uncanon_cell_/3   - placements back to the original frame
  arc2_transform(band_jigsaw, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, band_jigsaw)

Task solved: 16b78196 (2 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 84/120 = 70.00% to 85/120 = 70.83%.


Chapter 307: Wave 89 - wire_wrap (WP-347, Layer 322)

wire_wrap solves ARC-AGI-2 task 88bcf3b4. The task gives five
training pairs and one test case, all with same-size inputs and
outputs, on grids from 7x7 up to 22x22. Each grid holds one or more
three-color systems on a uniform background: a straight orthogonal
line segment, a crumpled meandering wire attached orthogonally to
one endpoint of the line, and a separate marker shape - a single
cell, a short bar, a zigzag, or a staircase blob. The output keeps
the line and the marker exactly where they are, erases the crumpled
wire, and re-lays it as a clean path that leaves the line end, runs
diagonally to the marker, bends snugly around the marker's near
face, and departs on the mirrored diagonal.

Rule: the re-laid wire is pure geometry - a diagonal approach from
the attachment cell toward the marker, clamped to the marker's near
lane; a hug laying one cell beyond each marker row's or column's
near edge from the contact side to the far side; and a mirrored
45-degree departure ray - truncated at the input wire's exact cell
count or the grid edge, whichever comes first.

Algorithm:

The majority color is the background with the reused helper
tw_background_, and ww_groups_ collects one component per remaining
color, each verified as a single 8-connected set by ww_connected_
over the reused tw_components_. ww_straight_ recognizes lines as
straight orthogonal segments of at least three cells, ww_ends_
yields their endpoints, and ww_orth_ pairs each line with the
crumpled wire whose cell touches an endpoint orthogonally; the
leftover components are markers, and ww_nearest_ gives each system
the marker nearest to its attachment endpoint by Chebyshev
distance. ww_sign_ takes, per axis, the sign of the marker centroid
minus the line endpoint - a zero component is tie-broken by the
side where the input wire's mass lies - and ww_approach_ walks that
diagonal from the attachment cell, each axis clamped to the lane
one cell outside the marker's bounding box, until the path is
8-adjacent to the marker. ww_ctype_ classifies the contact as a row
contact (marker cell ahead along the row axis, directly or across
the diagonal) or a column contact (marker cell aside along the
column axis), and ww_face_ lays the hug cells one column beyond
each marker row's near edge or one row beyond each marker column's
near edge, ordered from the contact side to the far side. ww_ray_
casts the departure ray with the lateral component of the approach
diagonal reversed, ww_ingrid_prefix_ cuts the chained path at the
first off-grid cell, the path is truncated to the input wire's cell
count, and the reused tt_render_ paints lines, markers, and re-laid
wires onto a fresh background canvas. The induce clause dispatches
early, directly after band_jigsaw, requiring preserved dimensions,
6 to 250 foreground cells, and a distinct foreground color count
that is a positive multiple of three of at most nine before
verifying every training pair.

The lesson: the wire's length is not always conserved - two of the
five training pairs lose wire cells because the re-laid path hits
the grid edge before the length is spent, which falsifies every
rigid-transform, rotation, and length-preserving reading of the
task. The wire is not moved; it is recomputed, and the input wire
contributes only its color, its cell count, its attachment cell,
and a tie-break side. Hand-tracing all seven line-wire-marker
systems (five training, two in the test) before coding exposed the
same four phases every time, and the face-based hug follows zigzag
and staircase contours with no special cases where contour-walk and
taut-string formulations kept failing. The rule passed all five
training pairs and the test case on the first prototype run and the
full regression on the first compile.

Key predicates:
  ww_groups_/2         - one component per foreground color
  ww_connected_/1      - single 8-connected set check
  ww_straight_/1       - straight orthogonal segment check
  ww_ends_/3           - bounding-box endpoints of a segment
  ww_orth_/2           - orthogonal adjacency of two cells
  ww_nearest_/3        - nearest-marker assignment per system
  ww_path_/7           - four-phase wire path construction
  ww_sign_/3           - approach diagonal sign with tie-break
  ww_approach_/10      - clamped diagonal walk to first adjacency
  ww_ctype_/7          - row-contact or column-contact decision
  ww_face_/9           - near-face hug cells in walk order
  ww_ray_/6            - mirrored 45-degree departure ray
  ww_ingrid_prefix_/4  - cut the path at the grid edge
  arc2_transform(wire_wrap, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, wire_wrap)

Task solved: 88bcf3b4 (5 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 85/120 = 70.83% to 86/120 = 71.67%.


Chapter 308: Wave 90 - compartment_tally (WP-348, Layer 323)

compartment_tally solves ARC-AGI-2 task 89565ca0. The task gives
three training pairs and one test case, with inputs from 22x20 up
to 30x28 and tiny census outputs from 3x4 up to 5x6. Each input
holds one scattered noise color plus three to five box colors on a
majority background. Every box color draws exactly one rectangular
outline, possibly subdivided by internal partition lines - full or
partial - into table-like compartments, one to six per box. The
boxes nest inside each other, overlap, and cross each other's
lines, and the scattered noise cells land everywhere, including on
top of box-line cells. The output is a census: one row per box,
sorted by compartment count ascending, each row painting the box
color once per compartment and padding with the noise color out to
the maximum count.

Rule: the census digits are structural - each box's number is the
count of compartments its partition lines enclose, found by flood
fill with guarded single-cell wall repair, and the boxes are listed
from fewest to most compartments with ties broken by the leftmost
bounding-box column.

Algorithm:

The majority color is the background with the reused helper
tw_background_, and ww_groups_ collects one cell set per remaining
color. ct_comps_ counts the 4-connected fragments of every color's
cell set, and the color with the most fragments is the scattered
noise color, because noise cells are mostly isolated singletons
while box outlines cohere into a few large fragments. For each
remaining color, ct_box_ frames the box with the reused ww_ends_
bounding box and collects every open cell strictly inside it. The
wall test ct_wall_ marks a cell as blocking when it carries the box
color itself, or - the guarded repair - when it is non-background
and both its vertical neighbors or both its horizontal neighbors
carry the box color, which restores partition cells that noise or
another box's crossing line overwrote while keeping genuine
single-row and single-column compartments open. ct_comps_ then
counts the 4-connected open regions with the ct_grow_ flood fill,
and that region count is the box's compartment count. Nested and
crossing boxes need no special handling, because their cells are
not walls for the box under analysis and closed outlines passing
through do not split compartments. Finally the boxes sort by
compartment count with msort - the b(N, C1, R1, V) term order gives
the leftmost-column tie-break for free - and ct_row_ emits one
census row per box, the box color repeated once per compartment,
padded with the noise color to the maximum count. The induce clause
dispatches early, directly after wire_wrap, requiring the first
pair's output strictly smaller than its input in both dimensions,
census bounds of two to nine, and a distinct foreground color count
equal to the output height plus one, then verifies every training
pair.

The lesson of the wave is the guarded gap repair. A naive flood
fill with walls of the box color only leaks through every partition
cell that noise or a crossing box overwrote, undercounting the
compartments. Repairing every flanked cell fixes the leaks but
walls off genuine single-row and single-column compartments, whose
interior background cells are also flanked by two parallel lines.
Guarding the repair to non-background cells fixes both failure
modes at once: real gaps are always noise or crossing cells, never
background.

Key predicates:
  ct_box_/5            - one tallied box term per box color
  ct_wall_/5           - guarded wall test for the flood fill
  ct_wall_x_/6         - wall classification of one cell
  ct_comps_/2          - 4-connected component count of a cell set
  ct_comps_s_/2        - component count of an ordered cell set
  ct_grow_/3           - absorb one component's cells
  ct_row_/5            - one census row for one box
  arc2_transform(compartment_tally, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, compartment_tally)

Task solved: 89565ca0 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 86/120 = 71.67% to 87/120 = 72.50%.


Chapter 309: Wave 91 - catalog_query (WP-349, Layer 324)

catalog_query solves ARC-AGI-2 task d8e07eb2. The task gives five
training pairs and one test case, and all twelve grids are 30x22
with dimensions preserved. Every input carries the same fixed body:
a four-by-four catalog of sixteen three-by-three colored shapes,
laid out at a five-cell pitch between two solid separator rows of
color 6 at rows 5 and 27. Above the upper separator sits the header
band, showing up to four query shapes in slots at the same column
pitch; below the lower separator sits a two-row footer band of
blank background. Each query shape is an exact cell-for-cell copy
of one catalog block, colors included. The output highlights every
matched catalog block by turning the background 8 cells of its
five-by-five frame box - the block plus a one-cell border - to
color 3, keeping all shape cells; when all matched blocks share one
catalog row or one catalog column, the header band is highlighted
the same way and the footer rows fill with color 3, and otherwise
the header stays untouched and the footer rows fill with color 2.

Rule: the header queries the catalog - every query shape looks up
its exact match in the fixed shape table and lights up that entry's
frame box, and the aligned-or-scattered verdict over all matches
decides the header highlight and the footer color.

Algorithm:

The transform first checks the fixed frame: exactly thirty rows,
exactly twenty-two columns, and solid color-6 separator rows at
rows 5 and 27. cq_block_ cuts a three-by-three block out of the
grid with three cq_seg_ row segments, and a findall over the row
and column block indices collects the sixteen catalog blocks,
block (i, j) at rows 8 + 5i to 10 + 5i and columns 2 + 5j to
4 + 5j. A second findall cuts the header slots out of rows 1 to 3
at the same column pitch and keeps the slots that fail cq_blank_,
so a slot counts as a query only when it holds at least one non-8
cell - the training pairs show both three-query and four-query
headers. Matching is a plain member unification of each query
block against the catalog terms: because the blocks are ground
lists of color codes, cell-for-cell equality including colors
falls out of unification, and same_length over slots and matches
guards that every query matched exactly once. The color exactness
matters, because the catalog carries same-shape different-color
decoys, such as a ring-of-eight shape that appears once in color 1
and once in color 2. The alignment verdict then asks whether all
matched positions share one catalog row index or one catalog
column index. Rendering is a per-cell walk with cq_paint_: footer
background cells fill with 3 when aligned and 2 otherwise, header
background cells promote to 3 only when aligned, background cells
inside any matched block's five-by-five frame box promote to 3,
and every other cell keeps its color. Adjacent frame boxes merge
into one continuous band with no dedicated banding logic, because
only background cells are promoted. The induce clause dispatches
early, directly after compartment_tally, requiring the fixed
30x22 frame and the two solid separator rows on the first pair,
then verifies every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that the verdict is global, not local.
Two training pairs highlight the header and paint the footer 3,
three leave the header alone and paint the footer 2, and no single
match explains the split - only the survey of all matches against
a shared catalog row or column does. The second lesson is fixed
scenery: when every training input carries the same body, the body
is a lookup table, and all the variation lives in the small query
region.

Key predicates:
  cq_block_/4          - cut one three-by-three block at (R1, C1)
  cq_seg_/4            - cut one three-cell row segment
  cq_blank_/1          - test a block for background-only content
  cq_paint_/6          - paint one output cell by region and verdict
  arc2_transform(catalog_query, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, catalog_query)

Task solved: d8e07eb2 (5 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 87/120 = 72.50% to 88/120 = 73.33%.


Chapter 310: Wave 92 - stencil_carve (WP-350, Layer 325)

stencil_carve solves ARC-AGI-2 task 58f5dbd5. The task gives three
training pairs and one test case. The inputs are 19x19, 19x19,
23x18, and 23x18, and the outputs are 19x7, 7x19, 13x13, and
19x13 - the output shape is not fixed but follows the scene. Every
input holds several solid five-by-five monochrome square blocks
standing in a regular grid arrangement with one-cell background
gaps - one column of three blocks, one row of three blocks, a
two-by-two arrangement, and a three-by-two arrangement across the
four grids - and each block carries a distinct non-background
color. Elsewhere in the scene lie small three-by-three sketch
motifs of various colors, including decoy sketches whose colors
have no matching block. The output keeps the block arrangement on
a fresh background canvas and carves each block's central
three-by-three region with the negative of its same-colored
sketch: wherever the sketch shows the block color the output cell
turns background, and wherever the sketch shows background the
cell keeps the block color.

Rule: each solid block pairs with the detached sketch of its own
color, and the sketch acts as a stencil whose colored cells are
the holes - the block interior is the sketch's punched-out
negative, not its copy.

Algorithm:

The transform first takes the background as the most common color
with tw_background_. stc_block_ then scans for solid five-by-five
squares of one non-background color, anchoring each block at a
top-left corner whose upper and left neighbors differ from the
block color, so a larger same-colored region can never yield
duplicate placements and no overlap bookkeeping is needed. The
distinct block starting rows and starting columns are sorted into
the arrangement axes, and the block count must equal the product
of the row count and the column count, so the blocks fill a
complete row-by-column arrangement. For each block color,
stc_sketch_ collects every cell of that color outside all blocks -
stc_in_block_ supplies the membership test - and requires the
bounding box of the collection to be exactly three by three,
recording the offsets of the colored cells inside the box. The
sketches may be disconnected within their bounding boxes, so
collecting all same-colored cells beats connected-component
analysis, and decoy colors without a block are simply never
visited. stc_tile_ carves each block's five-by-five tile with
stc_tcell_: central cells at sketch offsets turn background, and
every other tile cell keeps the block color. Finally the output
canvas - height six times the block rows plus one, width six times
the block columns plus one - is assembled cell by cell with
stc_cell_, which uses six-cell band arithmetic (row and column
modulo six) to decide whether a cell falls inside a block tile and
reads the tile or paints background accordingly. The induce clause
dispatches early, directly after catalog_query, requiring the
first output's height and width to both be one more than a
multiple of six with a background top-left corner, then verifying
every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is the carving inversion. The naive reading
copies the sketch into the block interior and fails every pair;
the correct reading punches the sketch out of the solid block, so
a sketch cell of the block color becomes a background hole. A
second lesson is that the output dimensions are derived, not
assumed: the four grids exercise four different block
arrangements, and the canvas resizes to six cells per block row or
column plus one border cell. The test grid presents the largest
arrangement - three block rows by two block columns of colors 4,
8, 2, 1, 6, and 9 - carved from six sketches scattered in the left
third of the scene.

Key predicates:
  stc_block_/5         - find one anchored solid five-by-five block
  stc_in_block_/3      - test a cell for membership in any block
  stc_sketch_/4        - collect a color's sketch offsets
  stc_tile_/4          - carve one block's five-by-five tile
  stc_tcell_/6         - paint one tile cell from the sketch
  stc_cell_/5          - paint one canvas cell from the tiles
  arc2_transform(stencil_carve, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, stencil_carve)

Task solved: 58f5dbd5 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 88/120 = 73.33% to 89/120 = 74.17%.

Chapter 311: Wave 93 - demo_transfer (WP-351, Layer 326)

demo_transfer solves ARC-AGI-2 task 4c7dc4dd. The task gives two
training pairs and one test case. All three inputs are 30x30
scenes filled with a noisy diagonal color pattern, and the outputs
are 5x5, 4x4, and 5x5 - the output size follows the ring boxes
found in the scene. Every input holds four square ring boxes:
complete one-color rectangle outlines sharing one frame color and
one side length, each interior resting on a zero background with
sparse colored marks. One interior is entirely empty and marks the
answer slot. Two interiors share the same set of nonzero colors
and form a worked demonstration - the before and the after of a
transformation. The fourth interior, carrying its own distinct
colors, is the question. The output is the demonstrated
transformation applied to the question interior.

Rule: the grid itself teaches the transformation. The first
training scene demonstrates connect (markers joined by straight
filled segments), the second demonstrates complement (zero and
the mark color swap places), and the test scene demonstrates
ripple (a radial pattern around the center moves outward and
repeats) - an operation that appears in no training output, so
the transformation must be read off the scene at answer time.

Algorithm:

The transform first scans the scene with dt_ring_ for complete
square rings of one nonzero color with side five to nine whose
interiors hold at most two distinct nonzero colors and at least
four zero cells. dt_boxes_ groups the candidate rings by frame
color and side length and keeps the group holding exactly four
rings, slicing out their interiors with dt_interior_.
dt_classify_ then sets aside the all-zero interior as the answer
slot, pairs the two interiors sharing one nonzero color set as
the demonstration, and takes the remaining interior as the
question - color grouping alone separates demo from question, so
the noisy region frames of the scene never need to be parsed.
dt_learn_ checks a library of three operations against the
demonstration pair in both directions through dt_op_ and
dt_apply_: complement swaps zero with the single mark color;
connect fills every straight run of zeros between two collinear
marks with the non-corner mark color, trying each mark color as
the corner and scanning columns by transposing the grid;
ripple reads a Manhattan-distance color profile around the center
of the odd-sided square with dt_ring_val_, requires leading mark
rings followed by zero rings, and pushes the pattern outward by
its own extent, repeating it periodically with dt_ripple_val_.
When both directions match - complement is its own inverse -
dt_learn_ prefers the direction whose source box has a
nonzero-cell count closest to the question box. Finally
dt_apply_ runs the learned operation on the question interior
with the question's own colors: the corner color learned from
the demonstration persists, and the fill color is the question's
other mark. The induce clause dispatches early, directly after
stencil_carve, requiring a first input of height at least twenty,
a first output no larger than eight by eight, and four ring boxes
in the first input, then verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is the in-grid worked example. Any solver
that memorizes what the training pairs did fails the test by
construction, because the test demands an operation no training
output contains; the rule must understand the demonstration
inside each scene and transfer it to new colors. A second lesson
is that direction must be earned: position-based pairing of demo
and question boxes gives the wrong direction on the test grid,
while matching the question's nonzero-cell count against each
demonstration box picks the correct source in all three scenes.

Key predicates:
  dt_boxes_/2          - group rings and slice the four interiors
  dt_ring_/7           - find one complete square ring candidate
  dt_interior_/5       - cut a ring interior out of the scene
  dt_classify_/4       - split answer slot, demo pair, question
  dt_learn_/4          - pick the operation and its direction
  dt_op_/3             - check one library operation on the demo
  dt_apply_/3          - run complement, connect, or ripple
  dt_ring_val_/4       - read one radial profile color
  arc2_transform(demo_transfer, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, demo_transfer)

Task solved: 4c7dc4dd (2 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 89/120 = 74.17% to 90/120 = 75.00%.

Chapter 312: Wave 94 - seam_weld (WP-352, Layer 327)

seam_weld solves ARC-AGI-2 task 4e34c42c. The task gives two
training pairs and one test case. The inputs are 23x25, 30x20,
and 26x20 scenes, and the outputs are 5x12, 5x22, and 14x19 - the
output size follows the welded assembly, not the input. Every
scene scatters several multi-color pieces on a uniform
background, and the pieces carry duplicated connector stubs: a
same-color cell cluster drawn identically on two different
pieces. In the first training pair the stub is a 9,1,4,1,9
column shared by two pieces; in the second it is a 5-3
checkerboard corner and a three-cell bar of 8s; in the test the
stubs include half-rings capped with a 6 or a 7, a corner of 8s,
and a lone pair of 2 marker cells. The output is the single
assembly obtained by welding the pieces so that every duplicated
stub sits exactly on top of its twin.

Rule: weld the pieces stub on stub. The largest piece seeds a
canvas; every remaining piece is placed so that all landed cells
agree exactly with the written canvas and at least one whole
same-color component of the piece coincides with a whole
same-color component of the canvas; the union bounding box of
the finished weld is the answer.

Algorithm:

The transform first takes the most common color as the
background with arc2_bg_color_ and groups the non-background
cells into side-connected multi-color pieces with sw_pieces_,
each carried as its full bounding-box subgrid cut by
sw_subgrid_. sw_records_ counts the cells of each piece and
splits it into whole same-color components under eight-way
adjacency with sw_parts_ and sw_flood_ - these components are
the candidate connector stubs. The piece holding the most
non-background cells is written onto an empty canvas at the
origin by sw_write_, background cells of the bounding box
included. sw_assemble_ then welds the remaining pieces one per
round: sw_placement_ enumerates the unplaced pieces largest
first and every offset touching the written canvas, row offsets
before column offsets; sw_agree_ demands that every bounding-box
cell landing on already-written canvas content agree exactly,
background included, and counts the agreeing non-background
cells as the score; sw_stub_ demands that at least one whole
same-color component of the piece land entirely on written cells
and coincide exactly with one whole same-color component of the
canvas, recomputed each round by sw_canvas_comps_; sw_better_
keeps the earliest placement with the strictly highest score.
When every piece is placed, sw_render_ reads the union bounding
box of the canvas back as the output with the background filling
unwritten cells. The induce clause dispatches early, directly
after demo_transfer, requiring a first input at least fifteen
rows tall and a first output at least two rows tall and shorter
than the input, then verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is component totality. A prototype using
plain greedy overlap welding - exact agreement plus maximum
overlap count - picked spurious placements in two of the three
grids: a large 1-frame brushed a 1-ring through three
accidentally agreeing cells, and a 4-body glued itself onto a
4-cap. The correct weld always superimposes a complete
same-color component of one piece onto a complete same-color
component of the other, while spurious welds only ever cover
components partially - and because the smallest legitimate weld
in the task joins just two marker cells, no overlap-size
threshold can replace the totality condition. A second lesson is
that the key-piece pattern needs no special handling: the small
piece duplicating one end of a larger piece simply welds on top
of that end and adds nothing to the union bounding box.

Key predicates:
  sw_pieces_/3         - extract pieces, largest first
  sw_records_/4        - bounding box, size, and stubs per piece
  sw_parts_/5          - whole same-color stub components
  sw_flood_/5          - flood fill one connected part
  sw_write_/5          - write a full bounding box to the canvas
  sw_assemble_/4       - weld loop, one piece per round
  sw_placement_/12     - enumerate one valid weld placement
  sw_agree_/8          - exact-agreement check with weld score
  sw_stub_/5           - whole stub onto whole canvas stub
  sw_render_/3         - union bounding box readback
  arc2_transform(seam_weld, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, seam_weld)

Task solved: 4e34c42c (2 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 90/120 = 75.00% to 91/120 = 75.83%.

Chapter 313: Wave 95 - short_circuit (WP-353, Layer 328)

short_circuit solves ARC-AGI-2 task 7b0280bc. The task gives
three training pairs and one test case. The grids are 18x18,
15x15, and 20x20 in training and 30x30 in the test, and the
output always keeps the input size. Every scene draws a wiring
network on a uniform background using exactly three foreground
colors: a terminal color forming exactly two blocks, a marker
color forming small junction blocks, and a body color forming
the wire segments that bend, run diagonally, and branch between
them. In the first training pair the terminals are 1-blocks and
the junctions 2-blocks on 0-wires; in the third pair the roles
flip, with 2-block terminals and 1-block junctions on 6-wires;
in the test the terminals are 3x3 blocks of 0s, the junctions
blocks of 9s, and the wires runs of 1s. The output recolors
exactly one route through the network - the route with the
fewest stops between the two terminals - and leaves everything
else untouched.

Rule: find the fewest-node route between the two terminal
blocks through the graph of same-color components and recolor
its intermediate nodes - wire body cells to 5 and junction
marker cells to 3 - keeping the terminals, the dead-end spurs,
and every longer alternative route unchanged.

Algorithm:

The transform first takes the most common color as the
background with arc2_bg_color_ and ranks the exactly three
foreground colors by ascending cell count with scc_palette_ and
its run-length encoder scc_runs_: the rarest color is the
terminals, the middle one the junction markers, and the most
frequent the wire body. scc_fgcells_ lists every foreground cell
keyed by position, and the Wave 94 helpers sw_parts_ and
sw_flood_ - reused verbatim - group those cells into whole
same-color components under eight-way adjacency. scc_tag_
attaches its color to every component, and the terminal color
must form exactly two components. scc_index_ maps every cell to
its component number, and scc_adj_ builds the adjacency list of
the component graph through scc_neigh_, joining two components
whenever any pair of their cells is eight-adjacent. scc_bfs_
runs a breadth-first search from one terminal component to the
other, expanding whole paths and marking components visited on
enqueue, which yields the route with the fewest nodes. Finally
scc_recolor_ strips the two terminals off the route ends and
paints every cell of every intermediate component with
scc_paint_ and arc2_set_cell_ - 3 when the component carries the
junction marker color, 5 otherwise. The induce clause dispatches
early, directly after seam_weld, requiring equal first-pair
dimensions and exactly four distinct colors in the first input,
then verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that the network is a genuine graph,
not a pair of disjoint routes. Junction blocks carry three or
more attached segments, dead-end spurs hang directly off the
terminals, and in the test grid one junction block serves both
the shortest route and a long alternative - so no split into
candidate routes can work, and only a breadth-first search over
the component graph picks the right path. Counting nodes is the
right metric: in every pair the recolored route is the unique
fewest-node path between the two terminals, with no cell-count
or Euclidean tie-break needed. A second lesson is that color
roles must be derived per grid: the same color draws the wires
in one pair and the junctions in another, so the rule ranks the
three foreground colors by cell count instead of memorizing
identities.

Key predicates:
  scc_palette_/5       - rank foreground colors by cell count
  scc_runs_/2          - run-length encode a sorted cell list
  scc_fgcells_/3       - list foreground cells keyed by position
  scc_tag_/3           - attach its color to every component
  scc_index_/2         - map every cell to its component number
  scc_adj_/3           - adjacency list of the component graph
  scc_neigh_/4         - components eight-adjacent to one node
  scc_bfs_/5           - fewest-node route between terminals
  scc_recolor_/5       - recolor the intermediate route nodes
  scc_paint_/4         - write one color over listed cells
  arc2_transform(short_circuit, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, short_circuit)

Task solved: 7b0280bc (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 91/120 = 75.83% to 92/120 = 76.67%.

Chapter 314: Wave 96 - junction_hub (WP-354, Layer 329)

junction_hub solves ARC-AGI-2 task 800d221b. The task gives
three training pairs and one test case. The grids are 16x16,
10x10, and 20x20 in training and 30x30 in the test, and the
output always keeps the input size. Every scene draws a wire
network on a uniform background using exactly three foreground
colors: one wire color forming the network and two content
colors mixed together inside the small blocks that the wires
join. In the first training pair the wires are 8s joining
blocks of mixed 3s and 4s on a 9 background; in the second the
wires are 5s joining blocks of 2s and 1s on a 0 background; in
the third the wires are 7s joining blocks of 6s and 5s on an 8
background; and in the test the wires are 4s joining blocks of
3s and 2s on a 6 background. The output repaints every wire
cell with the majority content color of the block its branch of
the network reaches, except at the crossing point: there a
three-by-three ring keeps the wire color and the single cell at
its center takes the color of the winning side.

Rule: find the hub as the only cell whose full three-by-three
neighborhood holds the wire color, keep its eight ring cells
wire colored, split the remaining wire into four-connected
branches, repaint each branch with the majority content color
of the blocks it touches, and paint the hub with the majority
color of the branch cells touching the ring.

Algorithm:

The transform first takes the most common color as the
background with arc2_bg_color_ and requires exactly four
distinct colors in the grid. jh_centers_ and jh_solid_ then
census, for each foreground color, the cells whose full
three-by-three window holds that color: the wire color is the
only one owning exactly one such cell - the content colors own
none, because their blocks are small and mixed, or many,
because their solid regions are large - and that cell is the
hub. The eight cells around the hub form the ring; the hub and
ring leave the wire set and the ring keeps the wire color in
the output. jh_cells_ lists the content cells and the wire
cells keyed by position, and jh_parts_ with jh_flood_ splits
each set into four-connected components over a position assoc -
the content cells into mixed-color blocks and the ring-free
wire cells into branches. jh_branch_color_ repaints every
branch: it collects the blocks holding a cell four-adjacent to
the branch with jh_meets_ and takes the majority color over all
cells of those blocks with jh_majority_, which run-length
encodes the sorted colors through the Wave 95 helper scc_runs_.
Every branch cell four-adjacent to a ring cell then votes its
branch color once, and the hub takes the majority vote. Finally
jh_paint_branch_ writes every branch through scc_paint_ and
arc2_set_cell_ paints the hub. The induce clause dispatches
early, directly after short_circuit, requiring equal first-pair
dimensions and exactly four distinct colors in the first input,
then verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that the hub verdict is a cell-level
election, not a branch-level one. In the third training pair
the losing color owns three branches against two, yet the
winning color takes the hub because its two branches each wrap
onto the ring at two separate places and outvote the others
four cells to three - a branch touching the ring twice carries
double weight. A second lesson extends Wave 95: the four colors
permute freely across pairs, and this time no cell-count
ranking can name the wire, so the rule recognizes it by a
purely structural signature - the unique solid three-by-three
window - derived fresh from every grid. A third lesson is that
adjacency arity matters: the arms of the network run side by
side one background cell apart, so the branches separate only
under four-way adjacency, and the eight-way component helpers
of the earlier waves would fuse them diagonally.

Key predicates:
  jh_centers_/5        - solid three-by-three centers of a color
  jh_solid_/4          - test one full three-by-three window
  jh_cells_/3          - list cells of given colors by position
  jh_step_/4           - one four-connected neighbour of a cell
  jh_parts_/4          - four-connected components of a cell set
  jh_flood_/4          - four-connected flood fill over an assoc
  jh_branch_color_/6   - majority block color touching a branch
  jh_meets_/2          - does an adjacency set enter a block
  jh_majority_/2       - most frequent value of a list
  jh_paint_branch_/3   - paint one branch onto the grid
  arc2_transform(junction_hub, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, junction_hub)

Task solved: 800d221b (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 92/120 = 76.67% to 93/120 = 77.50%.

Chapter 315: Wave 97 - hub_dock (WP-355, Layer 330)

hub_dock solves ARC-AGI-2 task 2c181942. The task gives three
training pairs and one test case. The grids are 23x20, 18x21,
and 20x25 in training and 24x26 in the test, and the output
always keeps the input size. Every scene shows one hub piece -
an eight-cell ring in a four-by-four bounding box whose four
sides carry two centered cells each, every side in its own
color - plus loose single-color shapes scattered on the
background. In the first training pair the hub sides are 6 on
top, 3 on the left, 2 on the right, and 1 on the bottom, and
the loose shapes are 2s, 3s, and 4s; in the third the sides are
1, 2, 7, and 3, and the shapes are 1s, 2s, 3s, and 7s; in the
test the sides are 1, 4, 2, and 6, and the shapes are 1s, 2s,
3s, 4s, and 6s. The output rebuilds the scene from a fresh
background canvas: the hub stays exactly where it was, every
shape whose color matches a hub side is rotated and parked
flush against that side, and every shape whose color matches no
side vanishes - the 4 shape in the first pair and the 3 shape
in the test.

Rule: find the hub as the only eight-connected component mixing
four colors, read its four sides as two adjacent same-colored
cells each, and dock every matching shape by the unique quarter
turn that puts a bounding-box side holding exactly two adjacent
centered cells toward its hub side, parked flush so the two
contact cells sit directly next to the two hub edge cells.

Algorithm:

The transform first takes the most common color as the
background with arc2_bg_color_ and groups the foreground cells
into eight-connected components with the Wave 82 helper
tw_components_. hd_hub_ keeps the components mixing exactly
four distinct colors, and exactly one may exist: the hub, an
eight-cell ring in a four-by-four bounding box. hd_edge_ reads
each side of the box - top row, bottom row, left column, right
column - as exactly two adjacent cells of one color and records
the color, the direction, and the start of the pair; the four
side colors must all differ. arc2_sb_blank_ builds an
all-background canvas of the input size and hd_put_ paints the
hub back unchanged. hd_dock_ then walks every other component:
the shape must be single-colored, and its color is looked up
among the sides - a shape owning no side is dropped. For a
matching shape, hd_orient_ normalizes the cells to the
bounding-box corner and tries the four quarter-turn rotations
in order through hd_rot_, which maps (R, C) to (C, H-1-R) per
turn; hd_face_ collects the side line facing the hub and
hd_centered_ accepts it only when the side length is even and
the line holds exactly the two centered positions. hd_place_
computes the corner that parks the rotated bounding box flush
against the hub side - one cell above, below, left, or right -
with the first contact cell aligned to the edge start, and
scc_paint_ writes the docked cells after a bounds check. The
induce clause dispatches early, directly after junction_hub,
requiring equal first-pair dimensions and five to eight
distinct colors in the first input, then verifying every
training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that centered contact is what makes
the docking unique. The second training pair's left shape also
owns a three-cell side whose two cells would touch the hub edge
rows, but only its bottom side holds exactly the two centered
positions, so a plain adjacency check would accept an off-center
fit that the centered-contact condition rejects - and the same
condition throws out the full-length sides too, pinning both
the rotation and the translation uniquely. A second lesson is
that the rotations are pure quarter turns with no reflection,
which the first training pair proves in both directions: the
same shape family docks left by a clockwise turn and right by a
counterclockwise turn. A third lesson extends Waves 95 and 96:
the side colors permute freely across pairs, so each side's
role is derived from its own two edge cells at answer time,
never memorized from color identities.

Key predicates:
  hd_hub_/1            - component mixing exactly four colors
  hd_edge_/5           - one hub side: two adjacent same cells
  hd_put_/3            - write one colored cell onto the grid
  hd_dock_/7           - dock or drop one loose shape
  hd_orient_/7         - first quarter turn with centered contact
  hd_rot_/7            - rotate cells K quarter turns clockwise
  hd_face_/4           - side line facing the hub
  hd_centered_/2       - exactly the two centered positions
  hd_place_/7          - flush corner against the hub side
  arc2_transform(hub_dock, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, hub_dock)

Task solved: 2c181942 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 93/120 = 77.50% to 94/120 = 78.33%.

Chapter 316: Wave 98 - twin_beam (WP-356, Layer 331)

twin_beam solves ARC-AGI-2 task 9bbf930d. The task gives three
training pairs and one test case. The grids are 17x16, 19x14,
and 17x16 in training and 23x21 in the test, and the output
always keeps the input size and changes only a handful of
cells. Every grid is a ladder: even rows carry a colored
horizontal bar starting near column two plus optional pipe
decorations to the right, odd rows are 7-filled separator
corridors, and every row opens with a 6 lamp in column zero on
a 7 spacer. In the first training pair the twin bars are 1s,
4s, and 9s; in the second 1s, 0s, and 3s; in the third 4s, 2s,
5s, and 3s; the test fires six separators among 9, 0, 1, 4, and
2 bars. Wherever two consecutive bars share one color, the
separator between them loses its lamp and a 6 appears somewhere
else in the grid - at the right end of the separator in the
clean second pair, but deep inside the pipe decorations, at the
top or bottom boundary, or beside a distant obstacle in the
others; one test beam crosses twenty rows and turns twice.

Rule: fire every separator whose neighbor bars share one color,
remove all fired lamps at once, and let each lamp travel as a
beam heading right through the 7 corridors - turning at an
obstacle onto its unique open side, refusing a left turn while
the current row still holds its own lamp, resting at boundaries
and two-way junctions, and painting a 6 where it rests.

Algorithm:

The transform first reads the ladder: tb_bar_color_ fetches a
bar row, skips the lamp and spacer columns, and hands the rest
to tb_first_fg_, which scans rightward for the first non-7
cell - so bars that start late, like the 9 bars of the first
pair, still read correctly. A findall collects every odd
separator row whose bar rows directly above and below share one
color, and at least one must fire. tb_douse_ then folds over
the fired rows, turning column zero of each from 6 to 7 on one
shared working grid before any beam is traced - the guard below
makes later beams consult this vacated state, so dousing lazily
per beam would make the outcome depend on trace order.
tb_trace_ walks one beam per fired row from column zero heading
right: while the next cell in the current heading holds 7 the
beam advances, and when the next cell steps off the grid the
beam rests on the cell it occupies. At an obstacle tb_turn_
takes over: it collects the two cells perpendicular to the
heading - up and down for a horizontal heading, left and right
for a vertical one - and tb_open_ keeps those that are inside
the grid and hold 7, on raw openness alone. With exactly one
open side the beam turns there and continues, unless
tb_guarded_ vetoes it: a turn onto heading left is refused
while column zero of the current row still shows a 6. With zero
or two open sides, or a refused left turn, the beam rests where
it stands. Finally tb_light_ folds over the resting cells and
paints a 6 on each, so no beam is blocked by another beam's
delivered lamp. The induce clause dispatches early, directly
after hub_dock, requiring equal first-pair dimensions and every
first-input row opening with a 6 lamp on a 7 spacer, then
verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that the lamp guard on left turns is
what separates two junctions no local rule can tell apart. In
the third training pair a beam descends a pipe and rests beside
an open leftward corridor because that row still holds its 6
lamp; in the test grid a beam turns left along a fired
separator whose lamp has already departed - and the two
junctions are identical in every cell of their neighborhoods.
The vacated column-zero lamps are not cosmetic; they are state
that later beams consult, which is why all fired lamps leave
before any beam walks. A second lesson is that junction arity
is tested on raw openness before the guard is applied: the
third pair's final beam faces a two-way junction and must rest
there, even though the guard would have thinned its choice to a
single side - the first Python prototype applied the guard too
early and failed exactly that pair.

Key predicates:
  tb_douse_/3          - clear one fired separator's lamp
  tb_light_/3          - paint a 6 on one resting cell
  tb_bar_color_/3      - bar color: first non-7 from column two
  tb_first_fg_/2       - scan a row tail for the first non-7
  tb_trace_/9          - walk one beam to its resting cell
  tb_turn_/9           - resolve a block: turn or rest
  tb_open_/6           - side cell inside the grid holding 7
  tb_guarded_/3        - left turn vetoed by the row's own lamp
  arc2_transform(twin_beam, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, twin_beam)

Task solved: 9bbf930d (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 94/120 = 78.33% to 95/120 = 79.17%.

Chapter 317: Wave 99 - hole_beacon (WP-357, Layer 332)

hole_beacon solves ARC-AGI-2 task de809cff. The task gives two
training pairs and one test case. The grids are 20x20 in
training and 30x30 in the test, and the output always keeps the
input size. Every input holds two large rectilinear regions of
two distinct colors on a zero background - 3 and 4 in the first
pair, 1 and 2 in the second, 1 and 6 in the test - disturbed by
three kinds of single-cell noise: background holes punched
inside the regions, wrong-colored cells embedded inside the
regions, and colored strays scattered over the background. The
output cleans all three kinds of noise and marks every hole
with a 3x3 beacon whose ring takes the RIVAL region's color and
whose center is painted 8; the test grid carries twenty-four
beacons, some overlapping each other's rings.

Rule: label every cell region A, region B, or background by a
majority vote over its orthogonal cross neighborhood at
distances one and two, ties broken by the cell's own color;
run the identical vote a second time over the first pass's
labels; erase strays, repair wrong colors, and stamp every
background hole inside a region as a beacon - a rival-colored
3x3 ring around an 8 center, centers painted after all rings.

Algorithm:

The transform first reads the palette: hb_colors_ flattens the
grid and sorts it, requiring exactly [0, A, B] and binding the
two region colors. hb_label_pass_ then labels every cell:
hb_vote_ collects the values of the eight in-bounds cross
probes enumerated by hb_probe_ - one and two steps up, down,
left, and right - and tallies the votes for A, for B, and for
the background. A unique maximum labels the cell directly; on a
tie the cell keeps its own input color when that color is among
the tied leaders and falls to background otherwise, which is
what lets a region cell at a convex corner survive with three
same-color neighbors while a colored bump on a straight edge is
erased with the same three. The same pass then runs a second
time, voting over the first pass's labels instead of the raw
colors - strays that the first pass silenced to background now
stay silent in every neighbor's vote. The final label grid is
itself the base output: background labels erase strays, region
labels repair wrong-colored cells and keep clean cells.
hb_centers_ then collects every cell whose input color is zero
yet whose label names a region - the holes - and hb_ring_ folds
over them, painting the rival region's color over every
in-bounds cell of each 3x3 stamp area through hb_paint_, which
clips at the grid edges. hb_center_ folds over the centers last
and paints each one 8, so adjacent beacons whose rings overlap
each other's centers keep every center visible. The induce
clause dispatches early, directly after twin_beam, requiring
equal first-pair dimensions, a first-input palette of exactly
zero plus two colors, and an 8 in the first output, then
verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that denoising by local majority is
fragile exactly where the noise clusters. On the test grid two
stray cells sit two steps from a background cell near the
region border, and the raw-color vote tips four to three toward
the region - stamping a beacon where none belongs. A
distance-weighted vote repairs the test but breaks both
training pairs at region corners. The cure is the second vote
pass: after pass one the strays already carry background
labels, so the revote falls to background and the spurious
beacon never appears. This is the Wave 98 non-local-state
lesson in reverse - here the transform's own intermediate
labels are the state that separates two locally identical
votes.

Key predicates:
  hb_colors_/3         - palette read: exactly [0, A, B]
  hb_label_pass_/5     - one full cross-vote labeling pass
  hb_probe_/2          - the eight cross offsets at 1 and 2
  hb_vote_/7           - majority label with own-color tie-break
  hb_centers_/3        - input-zero cells labeled as a region
  hb_ring_/5           - stamp one rival-colored 3x3 ring
  hb_paint_/4          - paint one in-bounds cell, clip outside
  hb_center_/4         - paint one beacon center 8
  arc2_transform(hole_beacon, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, hole_beacon)

Task solved: de809cff (2 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 95/120 = 79.17% to 96/120 = 80.00%.

Chapter 318: Wave 100 - corner_glyph (WP-358, Layer 333)

corner_glyph solves ARC-AGI-2 task 4c416de3 - on the one
hundredth wave of the climb. The task gives three training
pairs and one test case. The grids range from 19x22 to 23x23,
and the output always keeps the input size. Every input holds
boxes outlined in the zero border color on a solid background -
1, 8, 2, and 3 across the four grids - some clipped by the grid
edge. One box corner already carries a full multi-cell
decoration, the template, and the template shape differs in
every grid: a two-by-two outward block plus two inward diagonal
cells in the first pair, a six-cell zigzag band in the second,
a four-cell corner cross in the third, and a six-cell hook in
the test. Other corners carry just a single colored cell - a
glyph - sitting at one specific position of the shape: its most
inward cell on the corner's own diagonal. The output completes
every glyph into the full decoration, painted in the glyph's
color and mirrored into that corner's orientation, while
unmarked corners stay bare.

Rule: detect every box corner with its outward orientation by a
tolerant L-junction test; learn the template shape from the one
corner holding at least three same-colored cells nearby, each
cell stored as an offset in the corner's own frame; take the
shape's most inward diagonal offset as the marker position; and
stamp the complete shape, in the marker cell's color, at every
corner whose marker cell holds a non-background, non-zero
color.

Algorithm:

The transform first reads the background with arc2_bg_color_
and requires it to differ from zero. cg_corner_ then scans
every cell and every orientation frame (u, v): a corner cell is
never background, cg_arm_ validates the two border arms running
inward - their first four in-bounds cells must never show the
background, though decoration cells may overwrite border cells
anywhere on the arm, and at least two zero border cells must
survive per arm - while cg_not_zero_ confirms that no border
continues outward and the inward diagonal cell is interior.
cg_template_ enumerates the detected corners and collects every
non-background, non-zero cell within Chebyshev distance two in
frame coordinates (p = dr times u, q = dc times v); a color
reaching at least three cells is the template and its offsets
are the shape, so one canonical shape serves all four corner
orientations. cg_marker_ takes the shape's most inward offset
on its own diagonal - p equals q, p negative, p minimal - as
the marker position; the first pair's template reaches two
diagonal steps inward while the other grids reach one, so the
marker is read off the learned shape, never hard-coded. The
transform then reads each corner's marker cell and keeps every
corner whose marker holds a color that is neither background
nor zero, and cg_stamp_ folds over them, mapping every shape
offset through the corner's own frame and painting the marker
color via hb_paint_, which clips at the grid edges. Template
corners pass the marker test and repaint themselves unchanged,
so the stamp is idempotent. The induce clause dispatches early,
directly after hole_beacon, requiring equal first-pair
dimensions, a nonzero background, zero borders present, and a
template corner found, then verifying every training pair.

The lesson of the wave is that when decorations overwrite the
very structure that locates them - corner stamps painting over
their own box borders - the detector must be exactly as
tolerant as the decoration is destructive, and no more. The
first arm test demanded exact zeros at distances three and four
and lost two corners of the third pair's five-cell box, because
the template at one corner sits within arm reach of the
opposite corner. Forbidding only background on the arm while
counting two surviving zeros admits every true corner and,
checked against all thirty-one corners of the four grids, not
one impostor: every false candidate still fails on the zero
count or on the perpendicular arm.

Key predicates:
  cg_not_zero_/3       - cell out of bounds or not a border zero
  cg_arm_/6            - tolerant inward border arm test
  cg_corner_/6         - box corner with its outward frame
  cg_template_/4       - template color and shape in frame offsets
  cg_marker_/2         - most inward diagonal offset of the shape
  cg_stamp_/4          - stamp one corner's decoration
  arc2_transform(corner_glyph, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, corner_glyph)

Task solved: 4c416de3 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 96/120 = 80.00% to 97/120 = 80.83%.

Chapter 319: Wave 101 - legend_snake (WP-359, Layer 334)

legend_snake solves ARC-AGI-2 task 195c6913. The task gives
three training pairs and one test case. The grids range from
20x20 to 30x30, and the output always keeps the input size.
Every input shows two large regions of two dominant colors
meeting along a jagged diagonal boundary, with stray cells of
each color scattered inside the other region. Near the top-left
corner a legend strip of two-by-two blocks, separated by single
background columns, spells out a color sequence - 2, 2, 8 in
the first pair, 4, 1 in the second, 2, 2, 3 in the third, and
1, 2, 8, 3 in the test. Elsewhere a lone two-by-two block of
one extra color - 4, 9, 8, and 6 across the four grids - names
the turn color. Single marker cells of the legend's first color
sit on the left edge inside one of the regions. In the output
every marker has fired a snake path eastward through its own
region, painted with the legend pattern repeating cyclically;
every time the path ran into the other region, the blocking
cell took the turn color and the snake pivoted north, then east
again at the next block, climbing the boundary like a
staircase; and the legend strip and the lone turn block have
been erased to the region color around them.

Rule: read the two region colors as the two most frequent
colors; collect every two-by-two block of a non-region color,
taking the blocks that share a top row as the legend pattern
and the lone leftover as the turn color; erase them; and walk
each left-edge marker eastward through its own region, painting
the pattern cyclically with the marker as position zero,
marking every blocking cell with the turn color, and pivoting
east to north or north to east without spending a pattern
position.

Algorithm:

The transform first calls ls_region_colors_, which flattens the
grid, counts the palette with clumped, and keeps the two most
frequent colors as the regions A and B. ls_blocks_ enumerates
every cell by row and column index and keeps each cell whose
color is neither A nor B and whose right, lower, and diagonal
neighbors repeat that color - the two-by-two blocks of the
scene. ls_legend_ picks the top row shared by at least two
blocks: those blocks, sorted by column, spell the repeating
pattern left to right, and exactly one block must remain, whose
color is the turn color. ls_markers_ collects every left-edge
row whose first cell carries the pattern's head color and whose
second cell differs from it, reading each marker's region color
from that neighboring cell. ls_erase_ then paints the four
cells of the turn block and of every legend block with the
region color that ls_fill_color_ probes from the block's four
orthogonal neighbors, reusing hb_paint_. Finally ls_path_
launches each marker's snake at pattern position one heading
east, and ls_walk_ with ls_step_ does the walking: an off-grid
next cell ends the path silently; a next cell of the marker's
region color takes the pattern color at position mod length and
advances the position; any other cell is a block - it takes the
turn color, spends no pattern position, and ls_turn_ pivots the
heading from east to north or from north to east, resuming from
the last path cell only if the pivoted neighbor holds the
region color, otherwise the path ends after the single mark.
Region membership is always read from the untouched input grid
while colors accumulate on the erased working copy, so painted
pattern cells can never block a later snake. The induce clause
dispatches early, directly after corner_glyph, requiring equal
first-pair dimensions, a legend strip with a lone turn block,
and at least one edge marker, then verifying every training
pair.

The lesson of the wave is that the turn bookkeeping carries the
whole task. The turn mark spends no pattern position, so the
cyclic pattern flows unbroken around every staircase corner -
verified position by position across all six snakes of the four
grids - and the second training pair's lower snake, whose pivot
direction is blocked immediately, proves that a blocked pivot
ends the path quietly after exactly one mark. The legend strip
is the program and the grid is the machine: reading the strip
left to right as a cyclic program executed by edge-launched
walkers turns a daunting scene into thirty lines of walking
code.

Key predicates:
  ls_region_colors_/3  - two most frequent colors as regions
  ls_blocks_/4         - two-by-two blocks of non-region colors
  ls_legend_/4         - legend pattern and lone turn block
  ls_markers_/3        - left-edge marker rows
  ls_erase_/6          - erase one block to its region color
  ls_fill_color_/6     - region color probed beside a block
  ls_path_/7           - launch one marker's snake eastward
  ls_walk_/12          - walk, paint, and pivot the snake
  ls_step_/15          - one region step or one turn mark
  ls_turn_/4           - east pivots to north, north to east
  arc2_transform(legend_snake, +Grid, -Out)
  arc2_induce_rule(+Pairs, legend_snake)

Task solved: 195c6913 (3 training pairs + 1 test case).
Score: 97/120 = 80.83% to 98/120 = 81.67%.

Chapter 320: Wave 102 - ray_scatter (WP-360, Layer 335)

ray_scatter solves ARC-AGI-2 task 4a21e3da. The task gives
three training pairs and one test case. The training grids are
18x18 and the test grid is 20x20, and the output always keeps
the input size. Every input shows exactly three colors: a
uniform background, one connected blob of a single color
sitting near the middle of the grid, and one or two lone marker
cells resting on the grid border - on the top and right edges
in the first pair, the top edge in the second, and the bottom
edge in the third and in the test. In the output the blob has
been blasted apart. Each marker has fired a ray into the grid
along its own row or column, reaching exactly as far as the
farthest blob cell on that line; the background cells along the
ray are painted the marker color, while the blob cells lying on
the line keep their color and stay welded in place. Every other
blob cell has flown away: the blob's side pieces have
translated rigidly into the corners of the grid, each landing
flush against two walls with its shape preserved cell for cell
- and in the two-ray pair, the bottom-left piece has vanished
entirely.

Rule: rank the three colors by frequency into background, blob,
and marker; fire one ray per border marker to the farthest blob
cell on its line, painting background positions marker-colored;
anchor the on-line blob cells; split the rest into quadrant or
side pieces; translate each piece flush into its corner - away
from each ray across the line, toward the marker's edge along a
single ray - and delete any piece whose corner touches no
marker edge.

Algorithm:

The transform first calls rs_scene_, which flattens the grid,
counts the palette with clumped, and requires exactly three
colors ranked by frequency - the background, the blob, and a
marker color of at most two cells - collecting the marker and
blob cell positions. rs_rays_ and rs_ray_ turn each border
marker into a directed ray term: vertical firing downward from
the top row or upward from the bottom row, horizontal firing
rightward from the left column or leftward from the right
column. rs_ray_far_ collects the blob cells on each ray's line
and takes the farthest one in the travel direction as the
stopping point, and rs_draw_ray_ with rs_ray_span_ and
rs_ray_cell_ paints every line position between the marker and
the stop - blob positions keep the blob color, background
positions take the marker color. rs_on_ray_ then anchors every
blob cell whose column matches a vertical ray or whose row
